topics.title: Milk: Types, Composition, and Factors Affecting Composition of Milk; Physical, Chemical, and Thermal Properties; Reception, Storage, and Cooling of Milk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Milk: Types, Composition, and Factors Affecting Composition of Milk; Physical, Chemical, and Thermal Properties; Reception, Storage, and Cooling of Milk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Milk (KTU / FSSAI Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Definition of Milk (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006):**
> Milk is the **normal mammary secretion** derived from **complete milking of a healthy milch animal** (cow, buffalo, sheep, goat, or camel) without either addition thereto or extraction therefrom. It shall be free from colostrum and shall contain not less than the prescribed percentage of milk fat and milk solids-not-fat (SNF).

For **cow milk (toned)**, the legal minimum standards as per FSSAI are:
- **Milk Fat:** not less than **3.0% (w/w)**
- **Milk Solids-Not-Fat (SNF):** not less than **8.5% (w/w)**
- **Lactose:** approximately **4.5–5.0%**
- **Total Solids (TS):** typically **12.5–13.0%**

For **buffalo milk**:
- **Milk Fat:** not less than **5.0% (w/w)**
- **SNF:** not less than **9.0% (w/w)**
- **TS:** approximately **15.0–17.0%**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine milk as a "biological emulsion-in-suspension cocktail":**
> Think of milk as a complex, ready-to-drink smoothie that a cow produces for her calf. It's not just water — it's:
> - **Tiny fat droplets** (cream) floating like oil bubbles in salad dressing (an **emulsion**)
> - **Protein micelles** (casein) and **whey proteins** acting like molecular sponges
> - **Lactose** (milk sugar) dissolved in water (like sugar in tea)
> - **Minerals** (calcium, phosphorus) dissolved or weakly bound
> 
> When you boil milk, it rises, scorches, and forms a skin — these are the proteins denaturing and fat coalescing. Milk is essentially a **fortified, self-preserving (short-term), nutritionally complete fluid** designed by nature to convert into muscle, bone, and brain tissue.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Compositional Parameters (KTU High-Yield Glossary):**
> - **Total Solids (TS)** = Fat + SNF (everything that is not water)
> - **Solids-Not-Fat (SNF)** = TS − Fat (proteins, lactose, minerals, vitamins, acids)
> - **Specific Gravity of Milk:** **1.028–1.032** at **20 °C**
> - **Freezing Point (Cryoscopic Constant):** **−0.512 °C to −0.550 °C** (used to detect water adulteration)
> - **pH of Fresh Milk:** **6.6–6.8** (slightly acidic due to casein and phosphates)
> - **Titratable Acidity:** **0.14–0.16% lactic acid**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Milk — Types of Milk

Milk can be classified on **species basis**, **composition basis**, or **processing basis**.

#### A. Species-Based Classification

| Species | Fat (%) | SNF (%) | TS (%) | Colour of Fat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cow** | 3.5–5.0 | 8.5–9.0 | 12.5–14.0 | Yellow (β-carotene) |
| **Buffalo** | 5.0–8.0 | 9.0–10.0 | 15.0–17.0 | White (no β-carotene) |
| **Goat** | 3.5–4.5 | 8.0–8.5 | 11.5–13.0 | White |
| **Sheep** | 5.0–7.0 | 10.0–11.0 | 15.0–18.0 | White |
| **Camel** | 3.0–4.5 | 8.5–9.5 | 11.5–14.0 | White |

#### B. Composition-Based Classification (As Per FSSAI)

| Type of Milk | Min Fat (%) | Min SNF (%) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Toned Milk** | 3.0 | 8.5 | Standardized supply |
| **Double-Toned Milk** | 1.5 | 9.0 | Low-fat consumer market |
| **Standardized Milk** | 4.5 | 8.5 | Premium retail |
| **Full Cream Milk** | 6.0 | 9.0 | High-fat preference |
| **Recombined Milk** | 3.0 | 8.5 | From milk powder + water |
| **Reconstituted Milk** | 3.0 | 8.5 | From SMP + water |
| **Skimmed Milk** | <0.5 | 8.7 | Fat-free products |
| **Flavoured Milk** | Variable | 8.5 | Chocolate, etc. |

### 2.2 Detailed Composition of Milk

> [!NOTE]
> The average gross composition of **cow milk** is given as a high-yield memory table:

| Component | Percentage (w/w) | Function / Remark |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | **87.0–87.5** | Continuous phase; dispersion medium |
| **Total Solids (TS)** | **12.5–13.0** | All non-water components |
| — Fat | 3.5–5.0 | Energy dense; emulsion form |
| — Protein | 3.2–3.5 | Building blocks |
|   · Casein | 2.6–2.8 | 80% of protein; curdles with acid/rennet |
|   · Whey | 0.6–0.7 | 20%; remains in solution |
| — Lactose | 4.5–5.0 | Carbohydrate; energy source |
| — Minerals (Ash) | 0.7–0.8 | Ca, P, K, Na, Mg |
| — Vitamins, Acids, Enzymes | trace | B-complex, A, D, E |

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Composition of Milk

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **12 High-Yield Factors — Examiner Favourite for 7-Mark Sub-Questions:**

1. **Species of animal** — Cow vs Buffalo vs Goat (genetic blueprint)
2. **Breed** — Holstein-Friesian (low fat) vs Jersey (high fat); Murrah buffalo (high fat)
3. **Stage of lactation** — Colostrum (high protein, antibodies) → Mid-lactation (standard) → Late lactation (declining fat)
4. **Age of animal** — Older cows tend to produce higher fat
5. **Feed and nutrition** — Forage increases fat; concentrate alters protein ratio
6. **Season** — Summer = lower yield, higher fat; Winter = higher yield, lower fat
7. **Time of milking** — Morning milk generally higher yield; evening milk often richer in fat
8. **Health status** — Mastitis reduces lactose and alters composition drastically
9. **Udder health** — One-quarter milk differs from whole udder
10. **Oestrus / Heat period** — Temporary drop in fat and SNF
11. **Pregnancy** — Late pregnancy alters fat and protein
12. **Interval between milkings** — Shorter interval = lower fat

### 2.4 Physical Properties of Milk (With Reasoning)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Colour:** White-to-creamish yellow (β-carotene in cow milk; pure white in buffalo due to absence of carotene).
> - **Flavour:** Slightly sweet (lactose), bland, slightly aromatic.
> - **Density / Specific Gravity:** **1.028–1.032** at 20 °C. Increases as fat decreases.
> - **Viscosity:** **1.5–2.0 cP** at 20 °C (about 1.5× water); higher in buffalo milk due to larger fat globules and higher TS.
> - **Surface Tension:** Lower than water (**~50 dynes/cm**) due to fat and proteins acting as surfactants.
> - **Freezing Point (FP):** **−0.512 °C to −0.550 °C**. Highly constant — the **Hortvet Cryoscope** exploits this to detect water adulteration (added water raises FP toward 0 °C).
> - **Boiling Point:** **100.55 °C** (slightly above water due to dissolved solids).
> - **Refractive Index:** **1.3440–1.3480** at 20 °C (used in lactometers).
> - **Conductivity:** Increases with mastitis (high Na⁺ and Cl⁻ leakage).

### 2.5 Chemical Properties of Milk

- Milk is a **buffered colloidal system** (pH ~ 6.7, titratable acidity 0.14–0.16% lactic acid).
- **Acidity sources:** Casein (≈ 0.05%), phosphates (≈ 0.04%), citrates, dissolved CO₂.
- **Milk fat** is composed of triglycerides of fatty acids (butyric, caproic, caprylic, capric, lauric, myristic, palmitic, stearic, oleic).
- **Oxidation:** Fat can undergo oxidative rancidity; antioxidants like tocopherol and vitamin A inhibit it.
- **Rancidity types:** Lipolysis (free fatty acids from lipase action) and oxidation (peroxides → aldehydes).
- **Maillard Browning:** Lactose + lysine (non-enzymatic browning during UHT heating).
- **Heat stability:** Influenced by pH, Ca²⁺, phosphate equilibrium.

### 2.6 Thermal Properties of Milk

| Property | Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Heat (cₚ)** | **3.93 kJ/kg·K** (≈ 0.94 cal/g·°C) | Heating/cooling calculations |
| **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | **0.56 W/m·K** | Pasteurizer heat transfer |
| **Thermal Diffusivity (α)** | **~1.4 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s** | Transient heat flow |
| **Boiling Point** | **100.55 °C** | Concentrator design |
| **Freezing Point** | **−0.540 °C** | Cold-storage design |

### 2.7 Reception, Storage, and Cooling of Milk

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-Wise Dairy Reception Protocol (Industry Standard):**

1. **Weighing** of milk cans on platform scales.
2. **Visual / Organoleptic Inspection** — colour, smell, sediment, temperature.
3. **Sampling** for laboratory tests.
4. **Platform Tests:**
   - **Alcohol Test** (68% v/v ethanol) — negative = good; positive clot = high acidity/mastitis.
   - **Clot-on-Boiling (COB) Test** — detects unstable, acidic milk.
   - **Sediment Test** — checks visible dirt.
   - **Acidity Test** (titration with 0.1 N NaOH, phenolphthalein).
   - **Specific Gravity** (lactometer).
   - **Fat estimation** (Gerber method).
5. **Accept / Reject Decision.**
6. **Cooling** to **≤ 4 °C** (legal limit) within **2 hours** of milking.
7. **Storage** in insulated/chilled tanks until processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling Methods:**
> - **Surface Coolers (Chilled Water Type)** — milk flows in thin films over corrugated plates; water cools from inside.
> - **Plate Heat Exchangers (PHE)** — counter-current, regenerative section recovers cold from outgoing milk.
> - **Immersion Coolers** — coils dipped into cans.
> - **Direct Expansion (DX) Bulk Tanks** — refrigerant in tank jacket.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TS = F + SNF$ | TS, F, SNF in % | Solids calculation |
| 2 | $SNF = \dfrac{L \times 0.25 + A}{0.5}$ | L = Lactometer reading, A = % Fat | Richmond's formula for cow milk |
| 3 | $SNF = \dfrac{L \times 0.25 + A + 0.5}{0.58}$ | Buffalo milk variant | Buffalo milk SNF |
| 4 | $\text{Corrected Lactometer Reading} = LR + 0.1(T - 60)$ | T = °F | Lactometer temperature correction |
| 5 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Q = J, m = kg, cₚ = J/kg·K | Heat to be removed in cooling |
| 6 | $\text{Cooling Load} = m \cdot c_p (T_{initial} - T_{final})$ | kJ or kcal | Chiller design |
| 7 | $\text{Added Water \%} = \dfrac{FP_{pure} - FP_{sample}}{FP_{pure}} \times 100$ | Cryoscopic detection | Adulteration test |
| 8 | $\text{Acidity \%} = \dfrac{0.009 \times V \times N}{W} \times 100$ | V = titrant, N = normality, W = milk wt | Titratable acidity |
| 9 | $\text{Heat required for cooling} = W \times C_p \times (T_1 - T_2)$ | W = kg milk, T in °C | Refrigeration load |
| 10 | $\text{Time to cool} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | h = W/m²K | Plate cooler sizing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Richmond's Formula for SNF

The lactometer reading (L) is related to the **specific gravity** of milk. Specific gravity increases when fat decreases and when dissolved solids increase. The relationship between L, fat, and SNF can be derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Definition of Lactometer Reading:**
Lactometer is graduated such that water reads **0** and milk reads **20–40**. The reading L is:

$$L = (\text{Specific Gravity} - 1.000) \times 1000$$

**Step 2 — Relationship between SNF, Specific Gravity, and Fat:**
For cow milk, empirical derivation gives:

$$\text{SNF \%} = \dfrac{L \times 0.25 + A}{0.5}$$

where **A = % Fat** and **L = Lactometer reading**.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
A cow milk sample shows:
- Lactometer reading **L = 30**
- Fat **A = 4.0%**

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNF \%} &= \dfrac{30 \times 0.25 + 4.0}{0.5} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{7.5 + 4.0}{0.5} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{11.5}{0.5} \\[6pt]
&= 11.5 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Solids:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TS \% &= F + SNF \\[6pt]
&= 4.0 + 11.5 \\[6pt]
&= 15.5 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Lactometer Temperature Correction

A lactometer is calibrated at **60 °F (15.5 °C)**. For readings at any other temperature T (°F), apply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{corrected} = L_{observed} + 0.1 \times (T - 60)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Observed reading at 80 °F = 28. Find the reading at 60 °F.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{corrected} &= 28 + 0.1 \times (80 - 60) \\[6pt]
&= 28 + 0.1 \times 20 \\[6pt]
&= 28 + 2.0 \\[6pt]
&= 30.0
\end{aligned}
$$

This corrected value of **L = 30** can then be used in Richmond's formula.

### 3.3 Cooling Load Calculation (Analytical Derivation)

A dairy receives **5,000 kg of milk** at **30 °C** and must cool it to **4 °C**. Using **cₚ = 3.93 kJ/kg·K**, calculate the heat to be removed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\[6pt]
m &= 5{,}000 \text{ kg} \\[6pt]
\Delta T &= (30 - 4) = 26 \text{ °C} \\[6pt]
c_p &= 3.93 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \\[6pt]
Q &= 5{,}000 \times 3.93 \times 26 \\[6pt]
Q &= 5{,}000 \times 102.18 \\[6pt]
Q &= 510{,}900 \text{ kJ} \\[6pt]
Q &\approx 511 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Refrigeration tonnage (1 TR = 13.86 kJ/min = 831.6 kJ/h):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time to cool} &= \dfrac{Q}{\text{Chiller capacity}} \\[6pt]
\text{If chiller} &= 100 \text{ kW} = 360 \text{ MJ/h} \\[6pt]
\text{Time} &= \dfrac{511}{360} = 1.42 \text{ hours} \approx 85 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Added Water Detection Using Freezing Point

The Hortvet freezing point of **pure cow milk ≈ −0.540 °C**. Suppose a sample freezes at **−0.480 °C**. Calculate the % added water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Added Water} &= \dfrac{FP_{pure} - FP_{sample}}{FP_{pure}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{-0.540 - (-0.480)}{-0.540} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{-0.060}{-0.540} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

So **~11% extraneous water** has been added.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cow vs Buffalo Milk

| Parameter | Cow Milk | Buffalo Milk | Engineering / Processing Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fat (%) | 3.5–5.0 | 5.0–8.0 | Buffalo = richer, more cream; ghee yield higher |
| SNF (%) | 8.5–9.0 | 9.0–10.0 | Buffalo = better for condensed milk |
| TS (%) | 12.5–14.0 | 15.0–17.0 | Buffalo milk requires longer boiling time |
| Fat globule size | 1–10 μm (smaller) | 5–15 μm (larger) | Cow cream rises faster by gravity |
| Colour of fat | Yellow (β-carotene) | White (no β-carotene) | Buffalo ghee is pure white |
| Density (g/mL) | 1.028–1.030 | 1.030–1.032 | Slight differences affect lactometer readings |
| Heat stability | Lower | Higher | Buffalo preferred for UHT, evaporated milk |
| Casein ratio (αₛ₁ : αₛ₂ : β : κ) | 4 : 1 : 4 : 1 | 3 : 2 : 4 : 1 | Buffalo higher β-casein → softer curd |
| Cholesterol (mg/100g fat) | 320 | 220 | Buffalo lower per unit fat |

### 3.6 Heat Removed Per Litre — Quick Reference Derivation

Mass of 1 litre of milk ≈ **1.031 kg** (using SG = 1.031).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{per\,litre} &= 1.031 \times 3.93 \times \Delta T \\[6pt]
&= 4.05 \times \Delta T \text{ kJ/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

For cooling from 35 °C → 4 °C (ΔT = 31 °C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 4.05 \times 31 \approx 125.6 \text{ kJ/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a useful design constant for **chilled water and bulk tank sizing**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Milk Reception Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Milk Cans Arrive at Dairy] --> B[Weighing on Platform Scale]
    B --> C[Visual and Organoleptic Inspection]
    C --> D[Sampling for Lab Tests]
    D --> E{Platform Tests}
    E --> E1[Alcohol Test 68 percent]
    E --> E2[Clot on Boiling Test]
    E --> E3[Sediment Test]
    E --> E4[Acidity Test]
    E --> E5[Lactometer Reading]
    E --> E6[Gerber Fat Test]
    E1 --> F{Quality Acceptance Decision}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F -->|Accept| G[Pumping to Chilled Storage Tank]
    F -->|Reject| H[Discard or Downgrade]
    G --> I[Cooling via PHE below 4 deg C]
    I --> J[Bulk Insulated Storage Tank]
    J --> K[Dispatch to Pasteurization Plant]
    K --> L[Further Processing]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) for Milk Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Raw Milk In at 30 deg C] --> B[PHE Heating Section]
    B --> C[Pasteurizer HX at 72 deg C]
    C --> D[Hot Pasteurized Milk Out]
    D --> E[Regenerative Cooling Section]
    E --> F[Cooled Milk Out at 4 deg C]
    G[Chilled Water at 1 deg C] --> H[Final Cooling Section]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Storage Silo]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Composition of Milk Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Milk 100 percent] --> B[Water 87 percent]
    A --> C[Total Solids 13 percent]
    C --> D[Fat 4 percent as emulsion]
    C --> E[SNF 9 percent]
    E --> F[Protein 3.5 percent as colloid]
    F --> F1[Casein 80 percent]
    F --> F2[Whey Protein 20 percent]
    E --> G[Lactose 4.7 percent as solution]
    E --> H[Minerals 0.75 percent as solution]
    E --> I[Vitamins and Acids trace]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Stage-Wise Lactation Curve of Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Colostrum Day 0 to 3] -->|High Protein Antibodies| B[Transition Milk Day 4 to 10]
    B -->|Normalising| C[Mid Lactation Day 11 to 240]
    C -->|Standard Composition| D[Late Lactation Day 240 Plus]
    D -->|Fat Up SNF Down| E[Dry Off Period]
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Bulk Milk Cooling System (DX Tank)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Milk Pump] --> B[Filter Strainer]
    B --> C[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[Direct Expansion Cooling Tank]
    D --> E[Agitator Motor]
    F[Refrigerant Compressor] --> G[Condenser]
    G --> H[Expansion Valve]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Temperature Controller at 4 deg C]
    I --> F
    D --> J[Outlet to Processing]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define milk as per FSSAI. State the minimum legal standards for cow and buffalo milk.**

**Model Answer:**

Milk, as defined under FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006), is the **normal mammary secretion** obtained from the complete milking of a healthy milch animal, free from colostrum, with no addition or removal of any constituent.

- **Cow milk:** Min Fat **3.0%**, Min SNF **8.5%**
- **Buffalo milk:** Min Fat **5.0%**, Min SNF **9.0%**

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 standards breakdown)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**List any six physical properties of milk with their typical values.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Specific gravity:** 1.028–1.032 at 20 °C
2. **Freezing point:** −0.512 to −0.550 °C
3. **Boiling point:** 100.55 °C
4. **Viscosity:** 1.5–2.0 cP at 20 °C
5. **Surface tension:** ~50 dynes/cm
6. **pH:** 6.6–6.8

*(3 marks: 6 properties, half mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **omit Richmond's formula constants** for buffalo milk (use 0.58 not 0.5) — loss of 2 marks.
> - **Do not confuse TS = SNF + Fat** with Fat = TS − SNF ordering (mark deduction 1).
> - For freezing point adulteration, students forget the **negative sign** — FP differences must be subtracted carefully.
> - In cooling load problems, **always use cₚ = 3.93 kJ/kg·K for milk**, NOT 4.18 (water) — common mistake.
> - When listing factors affecting composition, examiners expect **at least 8 factors** — listing only 4–5 loses 3–4 marks.

---

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **chemical composition of cow milk** with a neat labelled table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the composition of milk** with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(a):**

The chemical composition of cow milk is best described as a complex colloidal system of fat, protein, lactose, minerals, vitamins, and water. The average composition is given below:

| Component | % (w/w) | Physical State |
|---|---|---|
| Water | 87.0–87.5 | Continuous phase |
| Fat | 3.5–5.0 | Emulsion (globules 1–10 μm) |
| Protein (Casein 80% + Whey 20%) | 3.2–3.5 | Colloidal dispersion |
| Lactose | 4.5–5.0 | True solution |
| Minerals (ash) | 0.7–0.8 | Dissolved/colloidal |
| Vitamins, acids, enzymes | trace | Dissolved |

**Key Features:**
- **Fat** is in emulsion form — stabilized by milk fat globule membrane (MFGM).
- **Casein** exists as **casein micelles** stabilized by κ-casein and colloidal calcium phosphate.
- **Whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin) are in true solution.
- **Lactose** is the principal carbohydrate, responsible for the slightly sweet taste.
- **Minerals** include Ca, P, K, Na, Mg, Zn — Ca:P ratio is important for stability (~1.2:1).
- **Vitamins** — fat-soluble (A, D, E) in fat globules; water-soluble (B-complex, C) in aqueous phase.

*(7 marks: 3 for table + 4 for explanation)*

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(b):**

The composition of milk is **not constant**; it varies with multiple intrinsic and extrinsic factors. Twelve key factors are:

1. **Species** — Buffalo milk has higher fat (6–8%) than cow milk (3.5–5%).
2. **Breed** — Jersey cow gives higher fat than Holstein-Friesian.
3. **Stage of lactation** — Colostrum is rich in proteins and immunoglobulins; mid-lactation is "normal"; late-lactation declines in lactose and rises in fat.
4. **Age of cow** — Older cows yield higher fat content.
5. **Feed** — Roughage increases acetic acid → higher fat. Concentrates may depress fat if low fibre.
6. **Season** — Hot summer → low yield, higher fat; cool winter → high yield, lower fat.
7. **Time of milking** — Morning = higher volume; evening = higher fat.
8. **Health** — Mastitis reduces lactose and increases chloride; fat globule membrane is disrupted.
9. **Udder infection** — One quarter can have altered composition.
10. **Oestrus (heat period)** — Temporary depression in fat and SNF.
11. **Pregnancy** — Late gestation lowers yield and alters fat-protein ratio.
12. **Milking interval** — Shorter intervals yield less fat.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for table/list + 6 marks for detailed explanation with examples)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Define the following with examples: **Toned milk, Double-toned milk, Standardized milk, Recombined milk, Reconstituted milk.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **physical and thermal properties of milk**. A dairy receives **10,000 kg of milk** at **28 °C** and must cool it to **4 °C**. Calculate the heat to be removed. Take cₚ = **3.93 kJ/kg·K**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(a):**

| Type | Definition | Min Fat (%) | Min SNF (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Toned Milk** | Cow milk + skim milk + water standardized to legal min | 3.0 | 8.5 |
| **Double-Toned Milk** | Cow milk with extra fat removed for low-cal consumers | 1.5 | 9.0 |
| **Standardized Milk** | Milk adjusted to a fixed fat content (e.g., 4.5%) | 4.5 | 8.5 |
| **Recombined Milk** | Made from milk powder + butter oil + water (no fresh milk) | 3.0 | 8.5 |
| **Reconstituted Milk** | Made from SMP (skim milk powder) + water + milk fat | 3.0 | 8.5 |

**Examples:**
- Mother Dairy toned milk (3.0% fat)
- Amul Taza (double-toned, 1.5% fat)
- Heritage Standardized (4.5% fat)

*(7 marks: 5 marks table + 2 marks examples & explanations)*

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(b):**

**Physical Properties of Milk:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Specific gravity | 1.028–1.032 at 20 °C |
| Freezing point | −0.540 °C |
| Boiling point | 100.55 °C |
| Viscosity | 1.5–2.0 cP at 20 °C |
| Surface tension | ~50 dynes/cm |
| pH | 6.6–6.8 |
| Refractive index | 1.3440–1.3480 |
| Conductivity | 0.005 S/cm (fresh) |

**Thermal Properties:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Specific heat (cₚ) | 3.93 kJ/kg·K |
| Thermal conductivity | 0.56 W/m·K |
| Latent heat of freezing | ~287 kJ/kg |
| Boiling point elevation | 0.55 °C above water |

**Cooling Load Calculation:**

Given:
- m = 10,000 kg
- T₁ = 28 °C
- T₂ = 4 °C
- cₚ = 3.93 kJ/kg·K

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\[6pt]
\Delta T &= 28 - 4 = 24 \text{ °C} \\[6pt]
Q &= 10{,}000 \times 3.93 \times 24 \\[6pt]
Q &= 10{,}000 \times 94.32 \\[6pt]
Q &= 943{,}200 \text{ kJ} \\[6pt]
Q &\approx 943.2 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**In refrigeration tons (1 TR = 13.86 kJ/min = 3.516 kW):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cooling rate required} &= \dfrac{943{,}200}{3600 \times 1} = 262 \text{ kW} \\[6pt]
\text{In TR} &= \dfrac{262}{3.516} \approx 74.5 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for physical/thermal table + 4 for derivation and calculation)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **FSSAI definition** — normal mammary secretion, free from colostrum, no additions/removals.
- **Cow milk standards:** Fat ≥ 3.0%, SNF ≥ 8.5%.
- **Buffalo milk standards:** Fat ≥ 5.0%, SNF ≥ 9.0%.
- **Total Solids = Fat + SNF**; rich buffalo milk TS = 15–17%.
- **Specific gravity of milk = 1.028–1.032** at 20 °C.
- **Freezing point of pure milk = −0.540 °C** (Hortvet constant).
- **Richmond's formula (cow):** SNF % = (0.25L + A) / 0.5.
- **Richmond's formula (buffalo):** SNF % = (0.25L + A + 0.5) / 0.58.
- **Lactometer temperature correction:** L_corr = L_obs + 0.1(T − 60).
- **Specific heat of milk = 3.93 kJ/kg·K** (≈ 0.94 kcal/kg·°C) — use this, not 4.18.
- **Cooling requirement:** Milk must be cooled to **≤ 4 °C within 2 hours** of milking (FSSAI).
- **pH of fresh milk = 6.6–6.8**, acidity 0.14–0.16% lactic acid.
- **Fat is an emulsion**; **protein is a colloid (casein micelles)**; **lactose is in true solution**.
- **Colostrum ≠ milk** — must be excluded from sale; high in antibodies (IgG).
- **Plate Heat Exchanger** is the standard for milk cooling & pasteurization.
- **Alcohol test (68%)** — detects acidic, unstable milk; positive means reject.
- **Clot-on-Boiling test** — detects very acidic milk that curdles on heating.
- **Mastitis detection** — conductivity rises (Na⁺/Cl⁻ leakage), lactose falls.
- **12 factors affecting composition:** species, breed, lactation stage, age, feed, season, milking time, health, oestrus, pregnancy, udder, milking interval.
- **Cooling load formula:** Q = m·cₚ·ΔT (J or kJ).
- **Buffalo milk** = white fat (no β-carotene), larger fat globules, higher heat stability → best for UHT and condensed milk.
- **Cow milk** = yellow fat, smaller globules, higher β-carotene and Vitamin A precursor.
- **Added water detection** via freezing point depression or cryoscopic method.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cleaning: Basic Principles and Can Washing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cleaning: Basic Principles and Can Washing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Cleaning in Dairy Processing

**Cleaning** in the dairy industry is defined as the *complete removal of all visible soil, organic matter (milk residues, fats, proteins, lactose, minerals), and a substantial proportion of microorganisms from food-contact surfaces*, achieved through a systematic combination of physical, chemical, and thermal actions so that the surface is visually clean and safe for subsequent sanitization and food contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> Cleaning refers to the removal of food residues, dirt, grease, and other unwanted matter from processing equipment, utensils, and milk cans. It is the *prerequisite* to sanitization — a clean surface must precede a sanitized surface, because residual organic matter neutralizes most sanitizing agents (especially chlorine-based ones).

**Key terminological distinctions the examiner expects:**

| Term | Meaning | Expected Microbial Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Cleaning** | Removal of soil & ~90% microbes | Reduction by 2–3 log cycles |
| **Sanitization** | Reduction of pathogens to safe levels | Reduction by 5 log cycles (99.999%) |
| **Sterilization** | Complete destruction of all microbes | 100% kill (0 survivors) |
| **Disinfection** | Destruction of most pathogenic microbes | 3–5 log reduction |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of cleaning a milk can exactly like **washing a greasy tiffin box that was used for a coconut-oil-rich curry**:

1. **Rinse** → You first run water to remove loose rice/curry bits (analogous to **pre-rinse in dairy CIP**).
2. **Apply detergent (Vim/scrubber)** → The alkali cuts the fat, the surfactant lifts it off, the abrasive scours (analogous to **hot caustic + acid wash in CIP**).
3. **Scrub with brush** → Mechanical force dislodges stuck particles (analogous to **turbulent flow in CIP** or **mechanical brushing in can washers**).
4. **Final rinse** → Wash away all the loosened grime and detergent (analogous to **post-rinse with clean water**).

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important insight for a KTU student:** *Milk soil is uniquely tough because it contains hardened milkstone (a calcium-phosphate-protein complex) that simple water cannot remove. Hence, dairy cleaning always uses a two-step chemistry — alkali to saponify fat and dissolve protein, followed by acid to remove mineral scale.*

---

## 1.3 The "TACT" Principle — The 4 Pillars of Effective Cleaning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every cleaning operation in a dairy plant — whether a milk can, a cream separator, or a 10,000-litre silo — is governed by the **TACT rule**. KTU examiners frequently ask this as a 3-mark question.

**TACT = Time + Action + Chemical + Temperature**

| Pillar | Engineering Parameter | Typical Dairy Value |
|---|---|---|
| **T** — Time | Contact duration of detergent | 5–30 min (circulation) |
| **A** — Action | Mechanical/turbulent energy | Velocity ≥ 1.5 m/s (CIP) |
| **C** — Chemical | Detergent type & concentration | 1–3% NaOH, 0.5–1% HNO₃ |
| **T** — Temperature | Solution heat | 70–85 °C (alkali wash) |

If you reduce one parameter, you must increase another to maintain cleaning efficiency — a principle known as **compensatory adjustment** in dairy engineering.

---

## 1.4 Why Can Washing Holds a Special Place in Dairy Operations

Even in modern plants using stainless-steel silos, **milk cans remain the primary collection and transport vehicle in rural India and Kerala's co-operative dairy network** (KCMMF/Milma, KMSCL). Cans are reused 2–3 times daily. Improperly washed cans are the **#1 source of psychrotrophic bacteria, coliforms, and milkstone buildup** in the cold chain.

**Standard milk can:** 40-litre aluminium can (weight ~6 kg), tapered for nesting, with a 2-inch screw-on lid.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Theoretical Pillars of Cleaning

Cleaning effectiveness is a *multiplicative function* of three independent mechanisms. If any one fails, the overall cleaning efficiency collapses.

$$\boxed{\text{Cleaning Efficiency} = f(\text{Physical}) \times f(\text{Chemical}) \times f(\text{Microbiological})}$$

### 2.1.1 Physical (Mechanical) Action
- **Pre-rinse** with lukewarm water (35–45 °C) — *never hot, because hot water denatures milk protein and "bakes" it onto the metal*.
- **Turbulent flow** during CIP ensures Reynolds number > 4000 (turbulent regime). The boundary layer of soil is sheared off.
- **Manual brushing** in can washing uses nylon/polypropylene bristle brushes — *never metal brushes* (they scratch aluminium cans, creating niches for bacteria).

### 2.1.2 Chemical Action — The Two-Step Chemistry
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dairy cleaning is ALWAYS a two-stage process: Alkaline wash → Acid wash.** KTU frequently tests this as a 7-mark question.

| Stage | Chemical | Target Soil | Typical Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Alkaline wash** | 1–3% **NaOH (Caustic Soda)** | Fats, Proteins, Lactose | 70–85 °C, 15–20 min |
| **2. Acid wash** | 0.5–1% **HNO₃ (Nitric Acid)** | Milkstone (Ca-phosphate, Ca-citrate) | 50–70 °C, 10–15 min |

**Reactions that drive chemical cleaning:**

**Step 1 — Saponification of fat by alkali:**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{R-COONa (Soap)}$$

**Step 2 — Protein dissolution:**
Proteins are hydrolysed by hot alkali into soluble peptides and amino acids. The Na⁺ ions neutralize the negative charges on casein, making it water-soluble.

**Step 3 — Mineral scale removal by acid:**
$$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$$
$$\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

**Why HNO₃ and not HCl?** — Nitric acid also acts as a *sanitizer* (oxidizing action). HCl leaves chloride residues that corrode stainless steel (causing pitting corrosion in welded zones).

### 2.1.3 Microbiological Action
- Proper cleaning removes the **soil that protects bacteria** (biofilm matrix). This alone reduces bacterial load by **90–99.9%**.
- Final sanitization with hot water (90 °C, 2 min) or 200 ppm chlorine kills remaining cells.

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Cleaning Efficiency

| Factor | Effect on Efficiency | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Hardness** | Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ neutralize detergents | Use *soft water* (< 50 ppm hardness) |
| **Temperature** | ↑ T = ↑ chemical reaction rate (Arrhenius) | Maintain 75 ± 5 °C for caustic |
| **Concentration** | Linear up to a point, plateau after | 1–2% NaOH is optimum (cost-effective) |
| **Time** | Diminishing returns after 20 min | 15–20 min circulation is standard |
| **Flow velocity** | Must exceed 1.5 m/s for turbulent flow | Pump selection based on line volume |
| **Surface finish** | Rough surfaces trap soil | Stainless steel: Ra ≤ 0.8 µm |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Use / Significance | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times v$ | Volumetric flow rate in CIP (A = pipe area, v = velocity) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{vD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number — must be > 4000 for turbulent CIP flow | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{\text{pump}} = \Delta P_{\text{friction}} + \Delta P_{\text{elevation}} + \Delta P_{\text{fittings}}$ | Pump head calculation for CIP return | Pa or m of H₂O |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{cleaning}} \propto e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius: efficiency doubles every ~10 °C rise | dimensionless |
| 5 | $M_{\text{detergent}} = C \times V$ | Mass of detergent needed (C = %, V = solution vol) | kg |
| 6 | $\text{Milkstone \%} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100$ | Milkstone deposit on can surface | % |
| 7 | $\log(N_0/N) = k \cdot t$ | First-order microbial inactivation during sanitization | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Heat required} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy to heat wash water from ambient to 75 °C | kJ |
| 9 | $t_{\text{CIP}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{circuit}}}{Q}$ | CIP circulation time | min |
| 10 | $\text{Water consumption} = n \times V_{\text{can}}$ | Total water for n cans (V_can = 40 L typically) | L |

> [!NOTE]
> For dairy cans specifically, the **standard water consumption is 4–6 litres per 40 L can** (i.e., 10–15% of can volume for proper wash + rinse).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The cleaning principles covered here are the **bedrock of HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)** in every dairy plant. The **"cleaning" step itself is a CCP** in most international dairy standards (Codex Alimentarius, FSSAI Schedule 4, ISO 22000). A KTU student who masters this topic can work in:

- Plant hygiene and quality assurance roles
- CIP system design (with Vendology, GEA, Tetra Pak)
- Detergent formulation (Diversey, Ecolab, Johnson Diversey India)
- Dairy plant commissioning and validation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Determining CIP Circulation Pump Capacity

> **Problem Statement:** A dairy plant has a CIP loop consisting of a 2,000 L storage tank and 50 m of 50 mm (ID) stainless-steel piping. What pump flow rate is needed to achieve turbulent flow (Re > 10,000) at a kinematic viscosity of ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s (water at 20 °C)? Also calculate the CIP circulation time.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reynolds Number criterion

For CIP, the industry standard is:

$$Re \geq 10{,}000 \quad \text{(fully turbulent, ensures soil detachment)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for minimum velocity v_min

$$Re = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{\min} = \frac{Re \cdot \nu}{D}$$

Substitute the numerical values (D = 0.050 m, Re = 10,000, ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s):

$$v_{\min} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.050} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-2}}{0.050} = 0.2 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Cross-check with the dairy industry empirical rule

The empirical dairy rule of thumb states that CIP flow velocity should be ≥ 1.5 m/s. Since our calculated v_min (0.2 m/s) is the *hydraulic minimum*, we adopt the **dairy engineering value**:

$$\boxed{v_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \, \text{m/s} \; (\text{standard for dairy CIP})}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate volumetric flow rate Q

$$Q = A \times v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times v$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2}{4} \times 1.5 = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.0025}{4} \times 1.5$$

$$Q = 0.001963 \, \text{m}^2 \times 1.5 \, \text{m/s} = 0.002945 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to litres per minute (1 m³ = 1000 L, 1 min = 60 s):

$$Q = 0.002945 \times 1000 \times 60 = 176.7 \, \text{L/min}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate CIP circulation time t

$$t = \frac{V_{\text{circuit}}}{Q} = \frac{2000 \, \text{L}}{176.7 \, \text{L/min}} = 11.32 \, \text{min}$$

So the **CIP solution completes one full circuit in ~11.3 minutes**, meaning a 20-minute wash cycle gives ~1.77 full passes — sufficient for proper soil removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering judgment:** If we had used the calculated v_min = 0.2 m/s, the circulation time would balloon to 85 minutes — economically unacceptable. The 1.5 m/s empirical standard includes a **safety factor of 7.5×** to handle fouled pipes (where effective diameter shrinks due to milkstone).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Detergent Mass Required for CIP Loop

> **Problem Statement:** A 2,000 L CIP tank is to be charged with a 1.5% (w/v) NaOH solution. Calculate the mass of NaOH flakes required, and the amount of HNO₃ (67% w/w, specific gravity 1.42) needed for a subsequent 0.8% acid wash.

### Step 1 — Mass of NaOH

$$M_{\text{NaOH}} = C_{\%} \times V_{\text{solution}} = 0.015 \times 2000 \, \text{L} = 30 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 2 — Mass of HNO₃ (pure) needed

$$M_{\text{HNO}_3\text{(pure)}} = 0.008 \times 2000 = 16 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 3 — Volume of commercial HNO₃ required

Commercial 67% HNO₃ has a density of 1.42 kg/L. Mass of 1 L of commercial acid:

$$\rho_{\text{commercial}} = 1.42 \, \text{kg/L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HNO}_3 \text{ per L} = 1.42 \times 0.67 = 0.9514 \, \text{kg/L}$$

Volume of commercial acid:

$$V_{\text{commercial}} = \frac{16 \, \text{kg}}{0.9514 \, \text{kg/L}} = 16.82 \, \text{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Add acid to water, never water to acid** — this is a critical safety rule. A KTU student who writes the reverse in a viva loses 1 mark immediately.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Wash Water Hardness on Detergent Efficiency

The **divalent cations** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) in hard water form insoluble salts with both alkali and fatty acids, neutralizing the detergent:

| Water Hardness (as CaCO₃) | Rating | Caustic Soda Wasted | Effect on Cleaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–50 ppm | Soft | Negligible | Excellent |
| 50–150 ppm | Moderately hard | 10–15% | Acceptable |
| 150–300 ppm | Hard | 25–35% | Poor — milkstone forms |
| > 300 ppm | Very hard | > 40% | Unsuitable — must soften |

For a Kerala dairy plant, bore-well water often has 80–250 ppm hardness, so **water softening (ion-exchange) or use of sequestering agents like EDTA/polyphosphates** is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Energy Calculation for Heating Wash Water

> **Problem:** Heat 1,000 L of water from 25 °C to 75 °C for the caustic wash. Specific heat c_p = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K). Density of water = 1 kg/L.

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \times 4.18 \times (75 - 25)$$

$$Q_{\text{heat}} = 1000 \times 4.18 \times 50 = 209{,}000 \, \text{kJ} = 58.06 \, \text{kWh}$$

At a steam cost of ₹5/kWh thermal equivalent, the heating cost per CIP cycle is **~₹290** for this loop — a real operational cost dairy engineers optimize daily.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Standard CIP System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Water Inlet] --> B1[Pre-Rinse Tank 35-45 C]
    B1 --> C1[Caustic Circulation Tank 1-3% NaOH at 75-85 C]
    C1 --> D1[Acid Circulation Tank 0.5-1% HNO3 at 60-70 C]
    D1 --> E1[Final Rinse Tank 90 C Hot Water]
    E1 --> F1[Sanitization Loop 200 ppm Chlorine or 90 C]
    F1 --> G1[Process Equipment Milk Can or Pipeline]
    G1 --> H1[Return Line to CIP Skid]
    H1 --> I1[Separator and Drain]
    I1 --> J1[Effluent Treatment Plant]
    J1 --> K1[Safe Discharge to Drain]
    
    B1 --> L1[Centrifugal Pump 1.5 m/s]
    C1 --> L1
    D1 --> L1
    E1 --> L1
    F1 --> L1
    L1 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: The Two-Stage Can Washing Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Can as Empty Milk Can
    participant Stage1 as Stage 1 Caustic Wash
    participant Stage2 as Stage 2 Acid Wash
    participant Stage3 as Stage 3 Rinse and Sanitize
    participant QC as Quality Check
    
    Can->>Stage1: Cold Pre-Rinse 35 C for 1 min
    Note over Stage1: 1.5% NaOH at 75 C for 10 min
    Stage1->>Stage2: Drain and Transfer
    Note over Stage2: 0.8% HNO3 at 60 C for 8 min
    Stage2->>Stage3: Drain and Transfer
    Note over Stage3: Hot Water Rinse 90 C for 2 min
    Stage3->>QC: Visual and ATP Swab Test
    QC-->>Can: Approved for Refill OR Reject for Reclean
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Milk Can Washer Machine Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infeed Conveyor Dirty Cans] --> B[Can Inverter and Pre-Rinse Jets]
    B --> C[Caustic Spray Tunnel 75 C]
    C --> D[Internal Brush Station Rotating Brushes]
    D --> E[Acid Spray Tunnel 60 C]
    E --> F[Final Rinse Bars 90 C]
    F --> G[Steam Sterilizer or Hot Air Dryer]
    G --> H[Outfeed Conveyor Clean Dry Cans]
    
    H --> I[Storage and Reuse Loop]
    
    style C fill:#ffcc66
    style E fill:#ff9999
    style F fill:#66ccff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow: Detergent Chemistry on Milk Soil

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Soil[Milk Soil on Can Surface] --> P1{Fat Component}
    Soil --> P2{Protein Component}
    Soil --> P3{Mineral Component}
    
    P1 --> Q1[Hot NaOH 75 C]
    Q1 --> R1[Saponification Forms Water Soluble Soap]
    R1 --> S1[Fatty Acid Sodium Salt RCOONa]
    S1 --> T1[Rinsed Away with Water]
    
    P2 --> Q2[Hot NaOH 75 C]
    Q2 --> R2[Protein Hydrolysis to Peptides]
    R2 --> T1
    
    P3 --> Q3[Hot HNO3 60 C]
    Q3 --> R3[Dissolves Ca-Phosphate and Ca-Carbonate]
    R3 --> S3[Soluble Ca NO3 2 Salt]
    S3 --> T1
    
    T1 --> Final[Clean and Sanitized Can Surface]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the TACT principle of cleaning in a dairy plant. What happens if one of the parameters is reduced without compensation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **TACT principle** states that effective cleaning is governed by four interdependent parameters:

- **T — Time:** Duration of detergent contact (typically 15–20 min for circulation CIP).
- **A — Action (Mechanical):** Turbulent flow or brushing that physically dislodges soil.
- **C — Chemical:** Detergent type, concentration, and sequence (alkali then acid).
- **T — Temperature:** Solution temperature (70–85 °C for caustic wash).

**Compensatory adjustment rule:** If any one parameter (e.g., temperature) is reduced, the cleaning efficiency is maintained only by **simultaneously increasing one or more of the other three** (e.g., raising chemical concentration or extending time). A reduction in one parameter without compensation leads to incomplete soil removal, milkstone deposition, and microbial proliferation.

*(3 marks — 1 for stating TACT, 1 for explaining each, 1 for the compensation rule)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between cleaning and sanitization. Why must a surface be cleaned *before* sanitization?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cleaning | Sanitization |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Remove soil, residues, and most microbes | Reduce surviving pathogens to safe levels |
| **Microbial reduction** | 2–3 log (90–99.9%) | 5 log (99.999%) |
| **Primary agent** | Detergent (alkali + acid) | Hot water (90 °C) / chlorine (200 ppm) / quaternary ammonium |
| **Effect on soil** | Physically and chemically dissolves it | Cannot penetrate organic soil |

**Why clean first:** Organic soil (milk fat, protein) **physically shields bacteria** from sanitizers. Additionally, **chlorine is rapidly consumed (neutralized) by organic matter** — 50 ppm chlorine can be reduced to < 5 ppm within 30 seconds of contact with milk residue, rendering it ineffective. A dirty surface therefore cannot be effectively sanitized. Sanitization is only as effective as the cleaning that preceded it.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Similar Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the two-stage cleaning procedure used in a dairy plant. Explain the chemistry of each stage with balanced equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working of an automatic rotary milk can washer. List the sequence of operations performed.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

Dairy cleaning is universally a **two-stage chemical process**, supplemented by pre-rinse and post-rinse steps.

**Stage 1 — Alkaline (Caustic) Wash:**

- **Chemical:** 1–3% **Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)** solution.
- **Temperature:** 70–85 °C.
- **Duration:** 15–20 min circulation.
- **Targets:** Fats, proteins, and carbohydrates.

**Chemistry:**

(i) **Saponification of milk fat:**

Milk fat is primarily triglyceride. Hot NaOH hydrolyses it:

$$\text{(RCOO)}_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{RCOONa}$$

The fatty acid sodium salts (soaps) are water-soluble and rinse off easily.

(ii) **Protein dissolution:**

Heat + alkali hydrolyses casein and whey proteins into soluble peptides, amino acids, and sodium caseinate. The alkaline pH (12–13) denatures and solubilises proteins.

(iii) **Lactose caramelisation removal:**

Hot alkali chars residual lactose, which is then flushed.

**Stage 2 — Acid Wash:**

- **Chemical:** 0.5–1% **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)**.
- **Temperature:** 50–70 °C.
- **Duration:** 10–15 min.
- **Target:** **Milkstone** — the hard, grey-white deposit of calcium phosphate (Ca₃(PO₄)₂), calcium citrate, and denatured protein that accumulates on equipment.

**Chemistry:**

$$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\,\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2$$

$$\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

The acid dissolves the mineral scale, restoring the original surface finish. HNO₃ is preferred over HCl because it also provides mild **sanitising action** (oxidising) and does not cause chloride-induced pitting corrosion of stainless steel.

**The complete cleaning sequence** is therefore: **Pre-rinse (lukewarm water) → Caustic wash → Intermediate rinse → Acid wash → Final rinse (hot water) → Sanitisation**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

An **automatic rotary milk can washer** is a continuous in-line machine that washes, rinses, and sanitises 200–800 cans per hour.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Loading:** Empty, upside-down cans are placed on a rotating carousel or infeed conveyor.
2. **Pre-Rinse:** The can is inverted under cold-water spray nozzles (35 °C) to flush out residual milk.
3. **Caustic Spray:** The can moves into a tunnel where 1.5% NaOH at 75–80 °C is sprayed from multiple jets covering both interior and exterior surfaces.
4. **Internal Brushing:** Rotating nylon brushes enter the can through the mouth to scrub the interior walls (this is the **mechanical action** component of TACT).
5. **External Brushing:** Stationary brushes clean the outer surface.
6. **Intermediate Rinse:** Warm water (45–50 °C) rinses off the caustic.
7. **Acid Wash:** 0.8% HNO₃ at 60 °C is sprayed to remove any milkstone.
8. **Final Rinse:** Hot water at 85–90 °C rinses the can and simultaneously provides heat sanitisation.
9. **Hot Air Drying / Steam Sterilisation:** Sterile hot air (110 °C) or live steam is blown through to dry the can and ensure complete microbial kill.
10. **Unloading:** The clean, dry, hot can is ejected to the outfeed conveyor for immediate refill.

**Key engineering features:**
- All spray headers are made of SS-316.
- Solution tanks are insulated to maintain temperature.
- The machine has a **PLC-controlled cycle** with safety interlocks (e.g., automatic shutdown if caustic temperature drops below 65 °C).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the CIP system? Explain its components and the sequence of operations with a flow diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the factors affecting the efficiency of cleaning in a dairy plant. How is cleaning efficiency evaluated in practice?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**CIP — Clean-in-Place** is a method of cleaning the interior surfaces of pipelines, processing equipment (heat exchangers, evaporators, silos), and associated fittings **without disassembly**, by circulating cleaning solutions through the same path the product took.

**Major Components of a CIP System:**

1. **CIP Supply Tank(s):** Separate tanks for pre-rinse water, caustic solution, acid solution, and final rinse water. Usually fabricated in SS-304.
2. **CIP Return Tank:** Collects the spent solution returning from the process line; includes a stainless-steel mesh strainer to catch large particles.
3. **Centrifugal Pump(s):** Sized to deliver 1.5 m/s velocity; usually one pump per solution.
4. **Plate Heat Exchanger (for heating):** Heats caustic solution to 75–85 °C using plant steam.
5. **Conductivity Controller:** Automatically doses concentrated NaOH/HNO₃ to maintain target %.
6. **Flow Meter & Pressure Gauges:** Monitor line velocity and detect blockages.
7. **Spray Balls / Spray Heads:** For cleaning tanks, silos, and bulk vessels.
8. **Valve Matrix (Pneumatic or Manual):** Routes the correct solution to the correct circuit.
9. **PLC / Microprocessor Controller:** Sequences the entire cycle automatically.

**CIP Sequence of Operations:**

| Step | Solution | Temperature | Time | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-rinse water | 35–45 °C | 5–8 min | Remove loose soil |
| 2 | 1–2% NaOH | 75–85 °C | 15–20 min | Dissolve fat, protein |
| 3 | Intermediate rinse | 45 °C | 3–5 min | Remove residual alkali |
| 4 | 0.5–1% HNO₃ | 60–70 °C | 10–15 min | Remove milkstone |
| 5 | Final rinse | 90 °C | 5 min | Remove acid + heat-sanitise |
| 6 | Sanitiser (optional) | 200 ppm Cl₂ | 2 min | Final microbial kill |

**Flow Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid CIP architecture).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Factors Affecting Cleaning Efficiency:**

1. **Water Quality (Hardness):** Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions precipitate detergents. Soft water (< 50 ppm) is essential.
2. **Temperature:** Higher temperature increases reaction rate (Arrhenius) and reduces fat viscosity. Each 10 °C rise roughly doubles cleaning speed, up to ~80 °C.
3. **Chemical Concentration:** Below 1% NaOH, efficiency drops sharply; above 3%, no significant gain (only cost and corrosion increase).
4. **Time of Contact:** Cleaning follows a logarithmic curve — most soil is removed in the first 5 minutes, but the remaining 15 min ensure removal of strongly adhering films.
5. **Mechanical Action (Velocity / Turbulence):** Must exceed the threshold Re ≈ 10,000; below this, the boundary layer is not disturbed.
6. **Surface Finish:** Stainless steel polished to Ra ≤ 0.8 µm is 3–4× easier to clean than a Ra = 3.2 µm surface.
7. **Type of Soil:** Burnt-on, heat-denatured protein is 10× harder to remove than fresh soil.
8. **Equipment Design:** Sharp corners, dead legs, and crevices trap soil and reduce CIP efficiency.

**Evaluation of Cleaning Efficiency:**

- **Visual Inspection:** Surface must be visually clean, bright, and free of milkstone.
- **ATP Swab Test:** Measures residual adenosine triphosphate (a biological marker). Reading < 100 RLU = clean.
- **Protein Residue Test:** Swab with Biuret or ninhydrin reagent; absence of purple/blue colour = clean.
- **Microbiological Swab:** Total plate count < 1 CFU/cm² on equipment surface.
- **Rinse Water Test:** Final rinse water must have < 100 CFU/mL and zero coliforms.
- **Conductivity Check:** Final rinse conductivity must equal fresh water (no detergent residue).
- **pH Check:** Final rinse pH must be neutral (6.5–7.5).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Writing "CIP" without expansion** in the first sentence — costs ½ mark.
> 2. **Confusing sanitization with sterilization** — a 2-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting the pre-rinse step** — students jump straight to "caustic wash." Pre-rinse is *mandatory* because cold water is the only thing that won't bake protein onto the metal.
> 4. **Reversing acid and alkali** — always alkali FIRST, then acid.
> 5. **Adding water to acid** in the preparation step — KTU examiners (and real plant safety officers) deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in calculations (L/min vs m³/s) — loses ½ to 1 mark.
> 7. **Writing "TACT" but missing the compensatory adjustment principle** — only 2 of 3 marks awarded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cleaning: Basic Principles and Can Washing**

- ✅ Cleaning is the **removal of soil + ~99% microbes**; it precedes sanitization.
- ✅ **TACT = Time, Action, Chemical, Temperature** — all four are interdependent.
- ✅ Dairy cleaning is **always two-stage: alkali (NaOH) first, then acid (HNO₃).**
- ✅ **NaOH** saponifies fat and dissolves protein; **HNO₃** removes milkstone and also sanitises.
- ✅ **Pre-rinse water temperature must be lukewarm (35–45 °C)**, NOT hot — to avoid protein baking.
- ✅ **CIP flow velocity ≥ 1.5 m/s** for turbulent flow (Reynolds number > 10,000).
- ✅ **HNO₃ is preferred over HCl** for acid wash — avoids chloride pitting corrosion.
- ✅ **Milk can** is 40 L aluminium, reused multiple times; #1 source of contamination in rural collection.
- ✅ **Soft water (< 50 ppm hardness)** is essential; hard water wastes 25–40% of detergent.
- ✅ **Can washer** sequence: Pre-rinse → Caustic → Brush → Acid → Hot rinse → Steam/air dry.
- ✅ Cleaning efficiency is checked by **ATP swab, protein swab, microbiological swab, conductivity, and pH.**
- ✅ **Add acid to water, never water to acid** — safety rule.
- ✅ **Standard water consumption = 4–6 L per 40 L milk can** for proper wash.
- ✅ CIP is governed by **FSSAI Schedule 4, ISO 22000, and Codex Alimentarius** as a Critical Control Point.
- ✅ The **two formulae to remember** are: $Q = A \cdot v$ (CIP flow) and $M_{\text{detergent}} = C \cdot V$ (dosing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Can Washers: Cleaning in Centralised and Decentralized CIP Systems; Cleaning of Various Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Can Washers: Cleaning in Centralised and Decentralized CIP Systems; Cleaning of Various Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Can Washers

A **can washer** is a mechanical device used in the dairy industry to automatically clean, sanitize, and dry empty milk cans, tins, and containers before they are refilled or returned to the farm. It is a critical piece of equipment in any dairy plant that receives raw milk in cans (as opposed to bulk milk coolers), ensuring that microbial contamination is minimized at the very first stage of the milk supply chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Can washers are specialized cleaning machines designed to remove milk residues, fats, proteins, and minerals from the inner and outer surfaces of milk cans using a combination of **high-pressure hot water jets (75–85 °C), steam injection, detergent solutions, and mechanical brushing**. They form the first critical control point in the dairy hygiene chain.

## 1.2 Definition of CIP (Cleaned-In-Place) Systems

**Cleaned-In-Place (CIP)** is a method of cleaning the interior surfaces of pipes, vessels, process equipment, filters, and associated fittings **without disassembly**. The cleaning solution is circulated through the equipment in a closed loop, using a combination of chemistry, temperature, mechanical action, and time to remove soil and microbial contamination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** The CIP system replaces the older "Cleaned-Out-Of-Place" (COP) method where equipment had to be manually dismantled. CIP ensures **worker safety**, **uniform cleaning**, and **reduced downtime** in a dairy plant.

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview & Conceptual Analogy

### Analogy for Can Washers:
Imagine you have a greasy tiffin box after lunch. You scrub it with hot water and soap. A can washer does the **exact same thing** — but for hundreds of milk cans per hour, with **boiling water (82 °C), rotating brushes, and high-pressure jets**, completely automatically.

### Analogy for CIP:
Think of your **stomach** — when you eat, food is digested inside your body without you "opening up" the stomach to clean it. Similarly, CIP cleans the **inside** of a pipe or tank by flushing a "detergent juice" through it, dissolving all the milk fat and protein stuck on the walls, and rinsing it out — all without taking the equipment apart.

### Analogy for Centralized vs Decentralized CIP:
- **Centralized CIP** is like a **single municipal water treatment plant** supplying cleaned water to the entire city — one big system, many users.
- **Decentralized CIP** is like each house having its own **small borewell and water purifier** — independent, smaller units, dedicated to one or two pieces of equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard CIP Cycle Parameters (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Pre-rinse:** 40–50 °C water, 5–10 min
> - **Alkaline wash:** 1–3% NaOH, 75–85 °C, 15–20 min
> - **Intermediate rinse:** 40–50 °C water, 5–10 min
> - **Acid wash:** 0.5–1.5% HNO₃, 60–70 °C, 10–15 min
> - **Final rinse:** Cold water, 5–10 min
> - **Sanitization:** 90–95 °C hot water or chemical sanitizer

---

## 1.4 Types of Can Washers (KTU Classification)

| Type | Operating Principle | Throughput |
|------|---------------------|------------|
| **Rotary Can Washer** | Cans rotate on a turntable; jets and brushes clean from all angles | 200–600 cans/hr |
| **Straight-Through (Inline) Washer** | Cans move linearly on a conveyor; sequential cleaning stations | 300–1200 cans/hr |
| **Soak Tank + Manual Wash** | Cans immersed in hot detergent, then manually brushed | 50–100 cans/hr |
| **Automatic Steam-Jet Washer** | Uses direct steam injection + cold-water rinse shock | 200–400 cans/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Context:** Most small-scale dairy co-operatives (KCMMF/Milma collection centres) still use **soak tanks + semi-automatic rotary washers** due to lower capital investment, while large plants (e.g., Mother Dairy, Hatsun) use **straight-through washers**.

---

## 1.5 Cleaning of Various Dairy Equipment — Overview

Dairy equipment, based on the type of milk contact surface and processing function, requires different cleaning protocols:

1. **Storage Tanks / Silos** — bulk milk storage
2. **Plate Heat Exchangers (PHE)** — pasteurization
3. **Tubular Heat Exchangers** — UHT processing
4. **Homogenizers** — high-pressure milk fat emulsification
5. **Centrifugal Separators / Cream Separators** — milk fat standardization
6. **Pipelines and Valves** — process lines
7. **Packaging Machines** — milk pouch / bottle fillers
8. **Evaporators and Spray Dryers** — milk powder plants
9. **Cheese Vats and Butter Churns** — product-specific equipment

Each piece of equipment has a **tailored CIP circuit** designed by the manufacturer (e.g., Tetra Pak, GEA, SPX, APV) to match its geometry, flow paths, and product-soil interaction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of CIP Cleaning (MOST IMPORTANT FOR KTU)

The efficiency of any CIP system depends on four interlinked parameters, often called the **Sinner's Circle** of cleaning:

1. **Chemical Action (Detergent concentration and type)**
2. **Thermal Action (Temperature of cleaning solution)**
3. **Mechanical Action (Flow velocity / turbulence)**
4. **Time (Contact duration)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "What is Sinner's Circle?" — A model showing that cleaning effectiveness is the product of the four factors. Reducing one factor (e.g., temperature) requires compensating with another (e.g., chemical concentration or time).

$$\text{Cleaning Effectiveness} \propto C \times T \times M \times t$$

Where:
- $C$ = Chemical concentration of detergent (%)
- $T$ = Temperature (°C)
- $M$ = Mechanical action (turbulence, expressed as Reynolds number)
- $t$ = Time of contact (min)

## 2.2 Centralized CIP System — Deep Analysis

A **Centralized CIP system** consists of **one or two large CIP tanks** (one for alkaline detergent, one for acid detergent) located in a central CIP room, from which cleaning solutions are **pumped through distribution piping to multiple process lines and equipment** throughout the plant using a dedicated return loop.

### Key Components:
- **CIP supply tanks** (alkaline + acid + water)
- **CIP supply pump** (typically centrifugal, 25–50 m³/h)
- **Plate heat exchanger** for heating cleaning solutions
- **Distribution loop** (ring main, 50–80 mm SS304 piping)
- **Return loop** with conductivity / pH sensors
- **Control panel** (PLC-based sequencing)
- **Spray devices** (static spray balls, rotary jet heads)

### Advantages:
- Single, large capacity system serves many users
- Centralized control, monitoring, and chemical dosing
- Lower per-user chemical consumption (since solutions are reused)
- Better reproducibility and audit compliance (FSSAI, ISO 22000)
- Easier to validate and qualify for HACCP

### Limitations:
- High capital investment (₹30–80 lakh for medium plants)
- Long piping = higher heat and chemical losses
- Cross-contamination risk if return lines are not separated
- A single breakdown halts cleaning of the entire plant

## 2.3 Decentralized CIP System — Deep Analysis

A **Decentralized CIP system** uses **multiple small, independent CIP units**, each dedicated to one specific piece of equipment or one section of the plant. Each unit has its own tank, pump, heater, and controller.

### Key Components (per unit):
- Small CIP tank (200–500 L)
- Single centrifugal or positive-displacement pump
- Electric or steam heater
- Local PLC / timer-based control
- Dedicated supply/return hoses connected to the equipment

### Advantages:
- Lower capital cost (₹2–5 lakh per unit)
- Independent operation — failure of one unit does not stop the plant
- Suitable for small/medium dairies and modular plants
- Easier to retrofit in existing buildings

### Limitations:
- Higher chemical and water consumption per unit
- Less consistent cleaning due to operator dependency
- More frequent maintenance (multiple small units)
- Difficult to maintain uniform records (challenge for FSSAI audits)

## 2.4 Comparative Matrix (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Centralized CIP | Decentralized CIP |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Capital cost** | High | Low |
| **Operating cost** | Low (bulk chemicals) | Higher (per unit) |
| **Plant size suitability** | Large (≥ 50,000 LPD) | Small (≤ 20,000 LPD) |
| **Control** | PLC / SCADA centralized | Local timers / individual PLC |
| **Chemical recovery** | Excellent | Limited |
| **Water consumption** | 1.2–1.5× product volume | 2–3× product volume |
| **Downtime risk** | High (single point failure) | Low (modular) |
| **FSSAI compliance** | Easy | Difficult |

## 2.5 Cleaning of Specific Equipment — Theory

### 2.5.1 Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)
- PHE has narrow gaps (2.5–3.5 mm) prone to **fouling by milk proteins (β-lactoglobulin) and milk stone (calcium phosphate)**.
- Uses **reverse-flow CIP** — cleaning solution flows opposite to milk flow to dislodge deposits.
- **Alkaline wash (1.5% NaOH at 80 °C)** dissolves proteins and fats.
- **Acid wash (1.0% HNO₃ at 70 °C)** removes milk stone.
- **Flow velocity required:** ≥ 1.5 m/s to ensure turbulence (Re > 4000 → turbulent flow).

### 2.5.2 Homogenizer
- Operates at **150–250 bar pressure** with very fine clearances.
- High-pressure homogenizer valves get **protein-fat deposits**.
- Requires **intermediate CIP** after every 4–6 hours of operation.
- Disassemble and manually clean the homogenizer valve once a week.
- The first-stage and second-stage pistons are cleaned using **boiling water and alkaline foam**.

### 2.5.3 Centrifugal Separator
- Internal bowl has **disc stack** with narrow gaps (0.4–1.0 mm).
- Sludge accumulates in the bowl periphery — must be flushed every 2–3 hours.
- CIP involves **intermittent desludging** + alkaline wash circulation at 75–80 °C.

### 2.5.4 Storage Tanks / Silos
- Use **rotary spray balls** or **rotary jet heads** (e.g., Toftejorg, Gamajet) to ensure all interior surfaces are wetted.
- Static spray balls create a coarse spray pattern; rotary heads give a more uniform coverage (3D impact pattern).
- **Spray ball cleaning mechanism:** A pre-rinse with warm water (40 °C) removes loose soil; alkaline wash (1–2% NaOH, 80 °C, 15 min) saponifies fat; acid wash (0.5–1% HNO₃, 70 °C) dissolves milk stone; final rinse with potable water.

### 2.5.5 Pipelines
- Designed to maintain **turbulent flow** during CIP.
- Minimum velocity: 1.5 m/s (often 2.0 m/s) for CIP.
- Piping should have **self-draining slope (1 in 100)** to avoid puddles.

### 2.5.6 Packaging Machines (Pouch / Bottle Fillers)
- Most difficult to clean due to many moving parts.
- Use **COP (Cleaned-Out-Of-Place)** for the filling nozzles + **CIP** for the product lines.
- Daily cleaning with **0.5% NaOH** and weekly acid wash to remove limescale.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---------|--------------------|----------------|-------|
| **Reynolds number (CIP turbulent flow)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $Re \geq 4000$ | dimensionless |
| **CIP flow velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $1.5$ to $2.0$ | m/s |
| **Cleaning effectiveness** | $E \propto C \cdot T \cdot M \cdot t$ | Sinner's Circle | — |
| **CIP time-temperature** | $t \times T = \text{constant}$ | Equivalent lethality | min·°C |
| **Alkaline detergent concentration** | NaOH | $1.0$ to $3.0$ | % w/w |
| **Acid detergent concentration** | HNO₃ | $0.5$ to $1.5$ | % w/w |
| **Final rinse temperature** | Hot water | $90$ to $95$ | °C |
| **Alkaline wash temperature** | Heated NaOH | $75$ to $85$ | °C |
| **Can wash temperature** | Hot water | $75$ to $85$ | °C |
| **Spray ball impact pressure** | $P = \rho g h$ | $\geq 0.5$ to $1.5$ | bar |
| **Pipe slope for drainage** | slope | $1:100$ | unitless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, you may be asked to calculate CIP pump capacity given pipe diameter and required velocity. Use $Q = v \times A$, where $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CIP Pump Capacity Calculation (Numerical Type)

### Problem Setup (Typical KTU 2-mark / 7-mark):
A dairy plant has a CIP loop serving a **50 mm diameter** stainless steel pipeline. The required turbulent flow velocity for CIP is **1.8 m/s**. Calculate:
(a) The cross-sectional area of the pipe
(b) The volumetric flow rate (pump capacity)
(c) The Reynolds number at this flow rate (given $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s)

---

### Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area

The internal cross-sectional area of a circular pipe is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

Substitute $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^{2}}{4} \\
  &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \\
  &= \frac{0.007854}{4} \\
  &= 0.001963 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A 50 mm (2-inch) pipe is a standard dairy CIP line. The cross-section is small but adequate for the high velocities required.

---

### Step 2: Calculate the volumetric flow rate (pump capacity $Q$)

$$
Q = v \times A
$$

Substitute $v = 1.8$ m/s and $A = 0.001963$ m²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 1.8 \times 0.001963 \\
  &= 0.003534 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to litres per hour (1 m³ = 1000 L, 1 hr = 3600 s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{L/h}} &= 0.003534 \times 1000 \times 3600 \\
               &= 12722.4 \text{ L/h} \\
               &= 12.72 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A 50 mm pipe at 1.8 m/s delivers about 12.7 m³/h, which is the typical capacity of a small CIP pump (e.g., 15 m³/h model).

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Reynolds number

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}
$$

Substitute $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $v = 1.8$ m/s, $D = 0.050$ m, $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{1000 \times 1.8 \times 0.050}{0.001} \\
   &= \frac{90}{0.001} \\
   &= 90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re = 90{,}000 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent** — this satisfies the CIP mechanical action requirement.

> **Logic:** CIP must operate in turbulent regime to provide the mechanical scrubbing action that dislodges adhered soil. The high Re confirms our velocity selection.

---

### Step 4: Pressure drop estimation (optional for 14-mark questions)

For turbulent flow in smooth pipes, use the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**:

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

Using Blasius correlation $f = 0.316 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ for $Re = 90{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= 0.316 \times (90{,}000)^{-0.25} \\
  &= 0.316 \times (90{,}000)^{-0.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(90{,}000)^{0.25} = (9 \times 10^{4})^{0.25} \approx 9.74$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{0.316}{9.74} \\
  &= 0.0324
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 50 m long CIP loop ($L = 50$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 0.0324 \times \frac{50}{0.050} \times \frac{1000 \times (1.8)^{2}}{2} \\
        &= 0.0324 \times 1000 \times \frac{3240}{2} \\
        &= 0.0324 \times 1000 \times 1620 \\
        &= 52{,}488 \text{ Pa} \\
        &= 0.525 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A 0.5 bar pressure drop over 50 m is reasonable. The CIP pump must overcome this plus the static head and nozzle losses — typically a 2–3 bar pump is selected.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Soil Type vs Cleaning Approach

| Soil Type | Chemical Required | Temperature | Mechanism |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Milk fat (triglycerides)** | NaOH (alkaline) | 75–85 °C | **Saponification** (fat + NaOH → soap + glycerol) |
| **Milk protein (casein, whey)** | NaOH (alkaline) | 70–80 °C | **Hydrolysis** of peptide bonds |
| **Lactose (caramelized)** | Acid (HNO₃) | 70–80 °C | **Hydrolysis** and dissolution |
| **Milk stone (Ca-phosphate)** | Acid (HNO₃, H₃PO₄) | 60–70 °C | **Acid solubilization** of mineral deposits |
| **Biofilm (bacteria)** | Alkaline + sanitizer | 75–85 °C | **Protein matrix disruption** + cell lysis |
| **Rust / iron deposits** | Acid + chelator | 50–60 °C | **Chelation + dissolution** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-mark classic question:** "Explain why both alkaline and acid detergents are required in dairy CIP, and not just one." The answer is in this table — **alkaline removes organic soil (fat + protein), acid removes mineral soil (milk stone)**. They are complementary, not alternatives.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Can Washer Throughput Calculation

**Problem:** A rotary can washer handles cans of **25 cm height × 20 cm diameter**. The turntable has **24 can-holders** and rotates at **6 rpm**. Calculate the throughput in cans/hour.

### Step 1: Cans processed per revolution
Each holder carries 1 can, and there are 24 holders:

$$
\text{Cans per revolution} = 24
$$

### Step 2: Cans per minute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cans per minute} &= 24 \times 6 \\
                       &= 144 \text{ cans/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Cans per hour

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cans per hour} &= 144 \times 60 \\
                     &= 8640 \text{ cans/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This throughput (8,640 cans/h) is typical of a **large straight-through washer used in a 100,000 LPD dairy plant**. Each can holds 40 L of milk, so this washer can handle up to 3,45,600 LPD — more than adequate for plant capacity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Centralized CIP System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Raw Water Inlet] --> B[Pre-rinse Tank]
    B --> C[Supply Pump 25 to 50 m3/h]
    C --> D[Plate Heat Exchanger for heating]
    D --> E[Distribution Ring Main SS304]
    E --> F1[Process Line 1 PHE]
    E --> F2[Process Line 2 Separator]
    E --> F3[Process Line 3 Homogenizer]
    E --> F4[Process Line 4 Storage Tank]
    F1 --> G[Return Loop Header]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Conductivity Sensor for chemical recovery]
    H --> I[Return Tank]
    I --> J1[Alkaline Detergent Tank 1 to 3 percent NaOH]
    I --> J2[Acid Detergent Tank 0.5 to 1.5 percent HNO3]
    J1 --> D
    J2 --> D
    I --> K[Drain to ETP]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Cleaning solution is prepared in tanks J1 (alkaline) or J2 (acid), heated by D, pumped through the distribution ring E to the equipment, returned via G, and either recycled (if chemistry is OK) or sent to drain K. Conductivity sensor H ensures chemical strength is maintained.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Decentralized CIP System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Equipment 1 PHE] --> B[Local CIP Unit 1 Tank 300 L Pump Heater]
    A2[Equipment 2 Separator] --> C[Local CIP Unit 2 Tank 300 L Pump Heater]
    A3[Equipment 3 Homogenizer] --> D[Local CIP Unit 3 Tank 300 L Pump Heater]
    A4[Equipment 4 Storage Tank] --> E[Local CIP Unit 4 Tank 300 L Pump Heater]
    B --> F1[Local Drain]
    C --> F2[Local Drain]
    D --> F3[Local Drain]
    E --> F4[Local Drain]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each piece of equipment has its own dedicated CIP skid. There is no shared loop. Cleaning is initiated locally by the operator or by a local PLC.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Can Washer (Rotary Type)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Empty Milk Cans Infeed Conveyor] --> B[Pre-rinse Station 60 C water jets]
    B --> C[Detergent Soak Tank 80 C 1.5 percent NaOH]
    C --> D[Rotary Brush Station internal + external brushes]
    D --> E[Hot Water Rinse Station 85 C]
    E --> F[Steam Sterilization Station 100 C direct injection]
    F --> G[Hot Air Drying Station]
    G --> H[Outfeed Conveyor to milk receiving dock]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Cans follow a linear sequence — pre-rinse → detergent soak → mechanical brushing → hot rinse → steam sterilization → drying. The whole cycle takes 60–90 seconds per can.

---

## 4.4 Sequential CIP Cleaning Cycle (State Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Idle State] --> S1[Pre-rinse 40 to 50 C 5 to 10 min]
    S1 --> S2[Alkaline Wash 75 to 85 C 15 to 20 min NaOH]
    S2 --> S3[Intermediate Rinse 40 to 50 C 5 to 10 min]
    S3 --> S4[Acid Wash 60 to 70 C 10 to 15 min HNO3]
    S4 --> S5[Final Rinse Cold water 5 to 10 min]
    S5 --> S6[Sanitization 90 to 95 C hot water OR 200 ppm chlorine 5 min]
    S6 --> S7[Equipment Ready for Production]
    S7 --> S0
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each state represents a phase in the CIP cycle. The cycle is fully automated in a centralized system. In a decentralized system, each transition may be triggered manually by the operator.

---

## 4.5 CIP Cleaning of a Plate Heat Exchanger (Sectional View Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Milk Inlet Plate PHE] --> B[Heat Section 75 C 15 sec HTST]
    B --> C[Hold Tube]
    C --> D[Regenerative Section]
    D --> E[Cooling Section 4 C]
    E --> F[Milk Outlet]
    G[CIP Solution Inlet reverse flow] --> E
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
    A --> H[CIP Solution Outlet to return loop]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** During CIP, the cleaning solution flows **in reverse direction** (bottom to top) compared to milk flow (top to bottom). This is called **"counter-current CIP"** and helps dislodge deposits that have accumulated against the milk flow.

---

## 4.6 Summary Architecture: Equipment-Specific CIP

```mermaid
graph TD
    PHE[Plate Heat Exchanger] --> P1[Reverse flow CIP at 1.8 m/s 80 C]
    HOMO[Homogenizer] --> P2[Disassemble valve weekly Intermediate CIP every 4 hrs]
    SEP[Centrifugal Separator] --> P3[Desludge every 2 to 3 hrs CIP after every batch]
    TANK[Storage Tank] --> P4[Rotary spray ball CIP 80 C alkaline then 70 C acid]
    PIPE[Pipelines] --> P5[Turbulent CIP 1.5 to 2 m/s 80 C]
    FILL[Packaging Machine] --> P6[COP for nozzles CIP for lines 75 C alkaline]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define CIP. List any four parameters that affect the efficiency of a CIP system.**

**Model Answer:**

**CIP (Cleaned-In-Place)** is a method of cleaning the interior surfaces of dairy processing equipment, pipelines, and vessels **without disassembly**, by circulating cleaning solutions through the equipment in a closed loop.

The four key parameters (Sinner's Circle) are:

1. **Chemical action** — concentration and type of detergent (alkaline / acid)
2. **Thermal action** — temperature of cleaning solution (typically 75–85 °C for alkaline)
3. **Mechanical action** — flow velocity / turbulence (Reynolds number ≥ 4000)
4. **Time** — duration of each cleaning phase (15–20 min for alkaline wash)

> **Word count:** ~50 words for a 3-mark answer. **Examiner's note:** Always name the four parameters explicitly. No marks for vague "temperature and time" only.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between centralized and decentralized CIP systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Centralized CIP | Decentralized CIP |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Location** | Single central CIP room | Multiple small units near equipment |
| **Capacity** | One large system (5,000–20,000 L tanks) | Many small units (200–500 L each) |
