The incoming milk is pre-heated to **60 – 65 °C** using waste heat from outgoing milk — no external energy is consumed in this section. 4. **Final Heating (Heater Section)** Hot water (85 – 90 °C), heated in a *steam-injected boiler*, transfers heat through plates to raise milk from ~65 °C to precisely **72 °C** (or higher, per FSSAI standard). 5. **Holding Tube (Time-Temperature Maintenance)** The milk travels through a calibrated *holding tube* whose length is mathematically designed so that the **fastest-moving milk particle (centerline) spends at least 15 seconds** in the tube. This is the *legal pasteurization holding period*. 6. **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) Checkpoint** At the exit of the holding tube, a *temperature sensor + flow diversion valve* continuously checks outlet temperature: - If **≥ 72 °C** → milk flows forward to regenerator/cooler (✓ ACCEPTED) - If **< 72 °C** → milk is automatically **diverted back** to the balance tank (✗ REJECTED) — this is the regulatory "fail-safe" mechanism. 7. **Regenerative Cooling + Final Chilled-Water Cooling** The hot pasteurized milk first pre-cools incoming cold raw milk in the regeneration section (cooling to ~30 °C), then enters the *final cooling section* where chilled water (1 – 2 °C) brings it to **≤ 4 °C** before storage. > [!NOTE] > **Why Regeneration is the "Heart" of HTST Economics** > Without the regenerative section, every litre of milk would require external heating from 4 °C → 72 °C AND external cooling from 72 °C → 4 °C. With **85 % regeneration efficiency**, the heater only has to add ~12 °C and the cooler only removes ~12 °C, slashing energy bills by roughly **80 %**. This is why HTST is the dominant choice for large-scale dairies (Amul, Mother Dairy, Nandini, etc.). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Code) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 5 equations — they appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on this module.** | # | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Typical KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L = v \cdot t_h$ | $L$ = holding tube length (m); $v$ = milk velocity (m/s); $t_h$ = holding time (s) | Calculate holding tube length | | 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s); $A$ = cross-section area; $D$ = tube inner diameter | Determine velocity from line capacity | | 3 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat duty (kJ/h); $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/h); $C_p$ = specific heat ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** for milk; $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) | Heat balance / boiler sizing | | 4 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{raw,out} - T_{raw,in}}{T_{pasteurized,in} - T_{raw,in}} \times 100\%$ | Regenerator efficiency | Energy audit calculations | | 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $Re$ = Reynolds number; $\rho$ ≈ 1030 kg/m³ (milk); $\mu$ ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s | Verify turbulent flow in holding tube (must be **Re > 10,000** for legal HTST) | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dairy Industry Standard:** Every commercial fluid milk plant in India (capacity > 5,000 L/day) uses HTST. - **Pharma & Beverage:** Same plate-heat-exchanger principle is used in fruit juice pasteurization and blood plasma processing. - **Food Safety Compliance:** FSSAI Schedule-IV, EU Regulation 853/2004, and US-FDA Grade "A" PMO all mandate HTST equivalent lethality. - **Automation Platform:** HTST is the **proving ground** for SCADA-based process control in food engineering — students often see this technology in-plant during industrial training. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation #1: Holding Tube Length from First Principles **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Examination Style):** > *"A dairy plant operates an HTST pasteurizer at 72 °C for 15 seconds. The plant processes 10,000 L of milk per hour. The holding tube has an inner diameter of 50 mm. Calculate:* > *(a) Velocity of milk in the holding tube* > *(b) Required length of the holding tube* > *(c) Verify that the flow is turbulent (given ρ = 1030 kg/m³, μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s)."* ### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution **Step 1: Convert the volumetric flow rate to SI units** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= 10{,}000 \text{ L/h} \\ Q &= \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} \\ Q &= \dfrac{10}{3600} \\ Q &= 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* We divide by 1000 to convert litres to cubic metres, and by 3600 to convert hours to seconds, yielding a single SI value in m³/s. **Step 2: Compute the cross-sectional area of the holding tube** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m} \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \\ A &= 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* The area formula uses the inner diameter. We square the diameter and multiply by π/4 (a unitless geometric constant) to get a physical area in m². **Step 3: Calculate the milk velocity (Part a of the question)** $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \dfrac{Q}{A} \\ v &= \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} \\ v &= 1.415 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* Velocity is volumetric flow rate divided by cross-sectional area (continuity equation). A value of ~1.4 m/s is typical for HTST systems — fast enough to ensure turbulent mixing, but not so fast that residence time is lost. **Step 4: Calculate the required holding tube length (Part b)** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= v \cdot t_h \\ L &= 1.415 \text{ m/s} \times 15 \text{ s} \\ L &= 21.23 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* The fastest-moving fluid particle in turbulent flow travels at the **centerline velocity**, which is approximately equal to the average velocity (1.0×). For a non-ideal (laminar) profile, KTU expects the answer to use the **centerline velocity = 2 × average velocity**. We use 1.415 m/s directly because the industry-standard design assumes turbulent flow where centerline ≈ average. **Step 5: Verify turbulence (Part c)** $$ \begin{aligned} Re &= \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} \\ Re &= \dfrac{1030 \times 1.415 \times 0.050}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\ Re &= \dfrac{72.87}{0.001} \\ Re &= 72{,}870 \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* We substitute the four physical quantities (density × velocity × diameter) divided by dynamic viscosity. Since **Re = 72,870 ≫ 4,000**, the flow is **fully turbulent** (as required by FSSAI for HTST to ensure no laminar "slip" zones where under-processed milk could escape). > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** A Reynolds number above 10,000 confirms turbulent flow, which is the legal requirement for the holding tube. If the calculation had yielded Re < 4,000, the answer would be *"laminar — not acceptable for HTST; the design must be changed (e.g., smaller tube diameter)."* --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation #2: Heat Duty in the Heater Section **Problem:** *"A 20,000 L/h HTST system raises milk from 65 °C (post-regenerator) to 72 °C using hot water. Calculate the heat duty required in kJ/h, kW, and kcal/h. Take $C_p$ for milk = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K), and milk density ρ = 1030 kg/m³."* **Step 1: Convert volumetric flow to mass flow** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{V} &= 20{,}000 \text{ L/h} = 20 \text{ m}^3\text{/h} \\ \dot{m} &= \dot{V} \times \rho = 20 \times 1030 = 20{,}600 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify the temperature rise** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= T_{out} - T_{in} = 72 - 65 = 7 \text{ K (or °C, same magnitude)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply the heat-duty equation** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \\ Q &= 20{,}600 \times 3.93 \times 7 \\ Q &= 566{,}802 \text{ kJ/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Convert to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/h)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{kW} &= \dfrac{566{,}802}{3600} \\ Q_{kW} &= 157.4 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Convert to kcal/h (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{kcal/h} &= \dfrac{566{,}802}{4.184} \\ Q_{kcal/h} &= 135{,}469 \text{ kcal/h} \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic Summary:* The 20 m³/h of milk needs ~566,800 kJ of energy per hour to be heated by 7 °C. In real plant terms, this is supplied by a small steam boiler or a hot-water generator sized at ~160 kW. The huge savings come from regeneration: without it, the boiler would have to add 68 °C (4 °C → 72 °C), nearly **10× the heat duty**. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HTST vs Other Pasteurization Methods | Feature | LTLT (Batch) | HTST (Plate) | HHST | UHT (Sterilization) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Temperature | 63 °C | 72 °C | 89 – 100 °C | 135 – 150 °C | | Time | 30 min | 15 s | 1 – 5 s | 2 – 5 s | | Process Type | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous (aseptic) | | Shelf life | 7 – 14 days | 14 – 21 days | 21 – 28 days | 3 – 6 months | | Equipment cost | Low | High | High | Very high | | Energy efficiency | Poor | Excellent (regen) | Good | Good | | Phosphatase test | Negative | Negative | Negative | **Positive** (over-processed) | | KTU focus | Often asked as comparison | **Main topic of Module 2** | Rare | Mentioned only | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HTST Pasteurization Plant (All Components) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Milk
Receiving
4 deg C] --> B[Balance Tank
Float Valve] B --> C[Dual Stage
In-line Filter] C --> D[Feed Pump
Centrifugal] D --> E[PHE Section 1
Regeneration
Heating] E --> F[PHE Section 2
Heating
to 72 deg C] F --> G[Holding Tube
15 sec
21 m length] G --> H{Flow
Diversion
Valve FDV} H -->|Temp below 72| B H -->|Temp 72 or above| I[PHE Section 3
Regenerative
Cooling] I --> J[PHE Section 4
Chilled Water
Cooling to 4 deg C] J --> K[Storage Tank
Pasteurized
Milk] L[Hot Water
Boiler
85 deg C] --> F M[Chilled Water
1 deg C] --> J H --> N[Control Panel
with Recorder] N --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide (for exam):** > - **Regenerative section** = the dual-purpose plate exchanger that first pre-heats raw milk (using outgoing heat) and then pre-cools pasteurized milk. > - **FDV** = the regulatory "fail-safe" component. It is the most-tested topic in KTU Module 2 viva voce. > - **Hot water boiler** is *external* to the milk circuit (indirect heating — prevents milk contamination by steam condensate). > - **Chilled water** is supplied by an in-plant cooling tower + refrigeration plant. ## 4.2 Schematic — Holding Tube Geometry (Textual Schematic) ``` ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ Milk │ │ Milk │ │ Inlet │ │ Outlet │ │ 72 °C │ │ 72 °C │ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ │ ▲ ▼ │ ╔═════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ ●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─● ║ ║ HOLDING TUBE — Length L = 21.23 m ║ ║ Inner Diameter D = 50 mm ║ ║ Flow: Turbulent (Re ≈ 72,870) ║ ║ Minimum Residence Time: 15 s ║ ║ ●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─● ║ ╚═════════════════════════════════════════════╝ ``` ## 4.3 Component-to-Function Mapping Table (High-Yield for Drawing-Based Questions) | S. No. | Component | Function | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Balance tank | Constant head, surge buffer | ★★★★ | | 2 | Feed pump (centrifugal) | Uniform flow delivery | ★★★ | | 3 | In-line filter | Removes sediment & somatic cells | ★★ | | 4 | Plate heat exchanger (PHE) | Heat transfer via stainless plates | ★★★★★ | | 5 | Hot water boiler | Indirect heat source (85 – 90 °C) | ★★★ | | 6 | Holding tube | Time-temperature maintenance | ★★★★★ | | 7 | Flow diversion valve (FDV) | Legal safety checkpoint | ★★★★★ | | 8 | Control panel | Records temp, time, flow | ★★★★ | | 9 | Chilled water system | Final cooling to 4 °C | ★★★ | | 10 | Pasteurized milk storage | Cold storage of finished product | ★★ | | 11 | Homogenizer (optional) | Breaks fat globules to < 2 μm | ★★★ | | 12 | CIP system (cleaning) | In-place cleaning of plates | ★★ | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Define HTST pasteurization. State the legal time-temperature combination prescribed by FSSAI for HTST processing of milk.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** HTST (High Temperature Short Time) pasteurization is a continuous thermal process in which fluid milk is rapidly heated to a minimum of **72 °C**, held at this temperature for at least **15 seconds**, and immediately cooled to ≤ **4 °C**. This combination is prescribed by FSSAI under Regulation 2.1.2 (1) of the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011, and is designed to ensure a 5-log reduction in *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and other vegetative pathogens while retaining the activity of the alkaline phosphatase enzyme as a built-in thermal indicator. *(3 marks: 1 — definition; 1 — time/temp values; 1 — regulatory reference + phosphatase significance)* --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **What is a Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)? Why is it considered the most critical safety component in an HTST system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) is a pneumatically or electrically actuated three-way valve located at the outlet of the holding tube that continuously monitors the temperature of milk leaving the holding section. If the temperature is **< 72 °C**, the FDV automatically diverts the milk back to the balance tank for re-processing; if **≥ 72 °C**, it allows forward flow to the cooling sections. It is considered the most critical safety component because it is the **legal fail-safe checkpoint** that prevents under-processed milk from reaching the consumer — failure of FDV operation is the single most common cause of regulatory non-compliance in dairy plants. *(3 marks: 1 — location/function; 1 — divert logic; 1 — failure consequence)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ❓ QUESTION A (Option 1) > `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a)** Describe with a neat labelled block diagram the **components and working of an HTST pasteurization plant**. List **five advantages** of HTST over the LTLT (Holder) method. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **function of the regenerative (heat-recovery) section** in an HTST plate heat exchanger. A plant processes 15,000 L/h of milk, raising it from 4 °C to 72 °C in the heater section after regeneration. If the regenerative section pre-heats the milk from 4 °C to 60 °C, calculate: **(i)** regeneration efficiency, **(ii)** actual heat duty of the heater section in kW. Take $C_p$ for milk = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K), ρ = 1030 kg/m³. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks] **Working of HTST (4 marks):** The HTST system works in a continuous flow manner through the following sequence: 1. **Balance Tank** — receives raw milk and maintains a constant head via float valve. 2. **Feed Pump** — delivers milk at uniform pressure (~2 bar) through the line. 3. **Filter** — removes sediment and somatic cells. 4. **Regenerative Section** — pre-heats raw milk (4 °C → ~60 °C) using waste heat from outgoing pasteurized milk. 5. **Heating Section** — raises milk from 60 °C to **72 °C** using hot water from a steam-heated boiler. 6. **Holding Tube** — maintains 72 °C for exactly **15 seconds** (length calibrated for turbulent flow with Re > 10,000). 7. **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** — diverts under-processed (< 72 °C) milk back to balance tank; accepts correctly processed milk. 8. **Regenerative Cooling** — pasteurized milk pre-cools incoming raw milk. 9. **Final Cooling** — chilled water (1 °C) brings milk to **≤ 4 °C** for storage. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 (draw neatly with all 9 blocks and arrows). **Five Advantages of HTST over LTLT (1 mark):** 1. Continuous process → higher throughput (5,000 – 30,000 L/h vs 1,000 L/batch). 2. Energy efficient (regenerative section recovers 80 – 95 % heat). 3. Better product quality (less "cooked" flavor, better vitamin retention). 4. Closed system → minimal post-process contamination. 5. Easy to integrate with homogenizer and CIP cleaning. --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks] **Function of Regenerative Section (3 marks):** The regenerative section is a counter-flow plate heat exchanger in which cold incoming raw milk is pre-heated by hot outgoing pasteurized milk across stainless steel plates (separated by gaskets). The two fluids **never mix** but exchange heat through plate walls. This serves three engineering purposes: - **Energy Recovery:** 80 – 95 % of the heat in outgoing milk is recovered, drastically reducing steam consumption. - **Pre-Heating:** Reduces the load on the external boiler. - **Pre-Cooling:** Simultaneously cools the pasteurized milk, reducing chilled-water load. **Calculation (4 marks):** **Given:** $\dot{V} = 15{,}000$ L/h = 15 m³/h; $\dot{m} = 15 \times 1030 = 15{,}450$ kg/h $T_{raw,in} = 4$ °C, $T_{raw,out} = 60$ °C, $T_{pasteurized,in} = 72$ °C $C_p = 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K) **Part (i): Regeneration Efficiency** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{regen} &= \dfrac{T_{raw,out} - T_{raw,in}}{T_{pasteurized,in} - T_{raw,in}} \times 100\% \\ \eta_{regen} &= \dfrac{60 - 4}{72 - 4} \times 100\% \\ \eta_{regen} &= \dfrac{56}{68} \times 100\% \\ \eta_{regen} &= 82.35\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii): Heat Duty of Heater Section** The heater section only needs to raise milk from 60 °C → 72 °C (ΔT = 12 K). $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \\ Q &= 15{,}450 \times 3.93 \times 12 \\ Q &= 728{,}622 \text{ kJ/h} \\ Q_{kW} &= \dfrac{728{,}622}{3600} = 202.4 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ > *Without regeneration*, the heater would have to add 68 K (4 → 72 °C), requiring **1,148 kW** — about **5.7× the energy**. The regenerative section saves the plant roughly **946 kW**, which translates to significant fuel cost savings (₹ lakhs per year at commercial scale). --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) > `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > **(a)** List and explain the **function of any eight components** of an HTST pasteurization system. Briefly describe the **principle of the plate heat exchanger (PHE)** used in HTST. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Enumerate **five advantages and five disadvantages** of HTST pasteurization. Compare HTST with UHT processing on the basis of (i) temperature, (ii) time, (iii) shelf life, and (iv) post-process storage requirement. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks] **Eight Components & Functions (4 marks):** 1. **Balance Tank** — Float-controlled buffer tank; ensures constant hydraulic head for uniform feed-pump operation. 2. **Feed Pump (Centrifugal)** — Provides continuous, non-pulsating flow of milk at controlled pressure. 3. **In-line Filter / Strainer** — Removes dust, hair, sediment, and somatic cells before heat treatment. 4. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — Multi-plate stainless steel unit for heating and cooling in sections. 5. **Heating Section Hot Water System** — Indirect heating via hot water (85 – 90 °C) generated by steam boiler. 6. **Holding Tube** — Calibrated straight tube ensuring minimum 15 s residence at 72 °C. 7. **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** — Three-way diverting valve; sends under-processed milk back to balance tank. 8. **Control Panel** — Records temperature, flow rate, and diverts automatically; provides audit log for FSSAI inspectors. *(Optional 2: Homogenizer — placed between heater and holding tube to break fat globules to < 2 μm; CIP system for in-place cleaning.)* **Principle of Plate Heat Exchanger (3 marks):** A PHE consists of multiple thin stainless steel plates (0.5 – 0.8 mm thick) pressed together in a frame, with gaskets sealing alternate flow paths. Hot and cold fluids flow in **counter-current** direction through alternate channels. Heat is transferred through the plate walls by **conduction** (across the plate) and by **convection** (on both sides). The corrugations on plates induce turbulence, enhancing the overall heat transfer coefficient. Multiple plates are stacked to scale up capacity (each plate adds ~0.1 m² of area). Typical HTST plate packs contain 100 – 300 plates. --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks] **Five Advantages of HTST (2.5 marks):** 1. Continuous process → very high capacity (5,000 – 30,000 L/h). 2. Energy efficient — regenerative section recovers 80 – 95 % of heat. 3. Better nutritional and sensory quality of milk. 4. Easy integration with homogenization and CIP cleaning. 5. Closed, hygienic system → minimal post-process contamination. **Five Disadvantages of HTST (2.5 marks):** 1. High initial capital investment (PHE plates, FDV, control panel). 2. Requires skilled operators and strict maintenance schedules. 3. Sensitive to flow-rate fluctuations — any change can break the 15 s holding time. 4. Plate fouling from milk solids reduces efficiency over time. 5. Not economically viable for very small dairy units (< 1,000 L/day). **Comparison Table: HTST vs UHT (2 marks):** | Parameter | HTST | UHT | |---|---|---| | Temperature | 72 °C | 135 – 150 °C | | Time | 15 s | 2 – 5 s | | Shelf life | 14 – 21 days (refrigerated) | 3 – 6 months (ambient) | | Storage requirement | **Refrigerated (≤ 4 °C)** | **Ambient temperature** | | Phosphatase test | Negative (correct process) | Positive (over-processed) | | Package | Standard bottle/pouch | Aseptic (Tetra Pak) | > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do NOT confuse HTST with UHT** — UHT is **sterilization** (commercial sterility), not pasteurization. UHT milk passes the phosphatase test because the enzyme is destroyed. > - **Always specify the holding time as a *minimum*, not exact** — KTU expects answers to clarify that 15 s is the *floor*, not the average. > - **Regeneration is NOT the same as "heat exchange"** — examiners deduct 1 mark if you write that "regeneration is just heat exchange" without mentioning *heat recovery* and *energy efficiency*. > - **For numerical problems, always convert L/h to m³/s** before substituting in velocity formulas — this is the most common unit-error trap that costs 1 full mark. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 THE 20-POINT RAPID-FIRE REVISION LIST — Read Before Exam** 1. **HTST Standard:** 72 °C / 15 s (FSSAI + Codex Alimentarius). 2. **LTLT Standard:** 63 °C / 30 min (the historical "Holder" method). 3. **Holding Tube:** Length = velocity × 15 s; designed for **turbulent** flow (Re > 10,000). 4. **PHE Sections:** Regeneration → Heating → Holding → Regeneration → Chilled-water cooling. 5. **Regeneration Efficiency:** Typically **80 – 95 %**; biggest energy saver. 6. **FDV Location:** Immediately *after* the holding tube. 7. **FDV Logic:** < 72 °C → divert back to balance tank; ≥ 72 °C → forward flow. 8. **Phosphatase Test:** Must be **negative** post-HSHT (confirms adequate heat treatment). 9. **Peroxidase Test:** Must be **positive** post-HSHT (confirms NOT over-processed). 10. **Five Key Components:** Balance tank, feed pump, PHE, holding tube, FDV. 11. **Optional Components:** Homogenizer (between heater and holding tube), CIP system, in-line filter. 12. **Plate Material:** Stainless steel SS-304 / SS-316, thickness 0.5 – 0.8 mm. 13. **Plate Pack:** 100 – 300 plates in commercial HTST units. 14. **Boiler Water Temperature:** 85 – 90 °C (indirect heating, prevents milk contamination). 15. **Outlet Storage Temp:** ≤ 4 °C (cold-chain mandate). 16. **Regeneration Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{raw,out} - T_{raw,in}}{T_{pasteurized,in} - T_{raw,in}} \times 100\%$ 17. **Heat Duty:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ (with $C_p \approx 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K) for milk). 18. **Holding Tube Length Formula:** $L = \dfrac{4Q \cdot t_h}{\pi D^2}$ (combined form for exam). 19. **Top Advantage:** Energy efficiency via regeneration (saves 80 % fuel). 20. **Top Disadvantage:** High capital cost + skilled operator requirement. > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** Always include a **labelled block diagram** in any 14-mark HTST question. KTU examiners award **2 dedicated marks** for a correct diagram — losing this is a guaranteed point drop. Use the Mermaid-style 9-block layout shown in Section 4.1 as your template.

topics.title: Sterilization: In-Bottle Sterilization, Advantages, and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sterilization: In-Bottle Sterilization, Advantages, and Disadvantages ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **In-Bottle Sterilization** (also termed *In-Container Sterilization* or *Batch Sterilization*) is a *terminal* heat-processing technique in which commercially sterile milk is produced by filling raw or pre-heated milk into hermetically sealable glass or heat-resistant polymer bottles, sealing them, and subsequently subjecting the sealed containers to a high-temperature, time-controlled heat treatment inside a steam-pressurized retort or autoclave, followed by controlled cooling. The process aims to achieve a **commercial sterility** standard — defined as the absence of *viable, pathogenic, and toxin-forming microorganisms* capable of growing in the product under defined storage conditions — without requiring subsequent refrigeration for shelf stability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box** > In-bottle sterilization = **Filling → Sealing → Heating (≥ 108 °C, ≥ 15 min) → Cooling**, all happening **inside the final consumer container**. The bottle is the process vessel itself. **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Board-Approved Values):** | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Process Temperature | **108 °C to 115 °C** | | Holding Time | **15 to 40 minutes** | | Bottle Material | **Heat-resistant glass (Type III) or high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** | | Bottle Volume | **200 mL / 500 mL / 1 L** | | Required F₀ (lethality) | **≥ 3.0 minutes** (minimum 12-log reduction of *Clostridium botulinum* spores) | | Resulting Shelf Life | **6 months at ambient temperature (≤ 25 °C)** unopened | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker on Your Dairy Shelf" Imagine you are cooking food in a **pressure cooker** at home. You place raw ingredients *inside* the cooker, lock the lid (seal it), apply heat from outside, and the trapped steam cooks the food uniformly while keeping it sealed from the outside world. When you open the cooker, the food is safe to eat for a long time at room temperature. In-bottle sterilization works on the **exact same logic**: - The **bottle** = your pressure cooker (the process vessel *and* the final package) - The **sealed crown cap or screw cap** = the locked pressure-cooker lid - The **steam-heated retort/autoclave** = the stove heating the cooker - The **sterilized milk** inside stays safe for months because no new microbes can enter The crucial difference from a domestic pressure cooker is the **rigorous control of time, temperature, and pressure** to satisfy food-safety lethality calculations (F₀ ≥ 3 min) — which is what KTU examiners love to test. > [!NOTE] > **Why "In-Bottle" and not "UHT"?** > - **UHT (Ultra-High Temperature)** sterilizes milk *before* packaging, using a continuous heat exchanger (140–150 °C for 2–5 s) and aseptic filling. > - **In-bottle** sterilizes milk *after* packaging, treating the sealed bottle itself as the heating vessel. It is a *batch* process, not continuous. ### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context In-bottle sterilization was the **dominant method** of producing shelf-stable milk in Europe, North America, and India during the early 20th century (1920s–1970s). Today it survives mainly in: - **Premium/specialty milk markets** (e.g., "glass-bottle sterilised milk" in the UK, Germany) - **Flavoured/fortified milk beverages** - **Regions with poor cold-chain logistics** (parts of rural India, Africa) - **Military rations and expedition foods** It has been progressively replaced by **UHT + aseptic packaging** because of cost and convenience, but understanding in-bottle sterilization remains a **mandatory KTU syllabus topic** because it illustrates the *terminal* nature of sterilization lethality and container integrity. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Principle — "Commercial Sterility Through In-Container Lethality" Commercial sterility is achieved when the **integrated lethal effect of heat** on the most heat-resistant spoilage organism (*Clostridium botulinum* Type B spores, or the surrogate *Bacillus stearothermophilus* / *Geobacillus stearothermophilus*) is sufficient to reduce its population by **12 log cycles** (i.e., a 10⁻¹² reduction, equivalent to making the product safe even if originally contaminated at 1 spore/mL in a 1,000 L batch). The mathematical yardstick used to measure this lethal effect is the **F-value (F₀)**. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the In-Bottle Process 1. **Pre-Treatment of Raw Milk** - Standardization (fat % to required level, usually 3.5 % for full-cream, 1.5 % for toned) - Homogenization (typically at **2000–2500 psi / 14–17 MPa**, two-stage, 60–65 °C) — prevents cream separation in the bottle during long storage - Optional pre-heating to 60–70 °C to reduce thermal shock and shorten retort time 2. **Bottle Preparation** - Glass bottles are washed, sanitized (chlorine rinse, 50–100 ppm free chlorine), and inspected for cracks - HDPE bottles are sterilized separately or purchased pre-sterile 3. **Hot-Filling into Bottles** - Milk is filled at **70–80 °C** to (a) reduce oxygen pickup, (b) create a partial vacuum on cooling, and (c) shorten retort come-up time - Headspace is controlled to **6–10 %** of bottle volume (leaving space for thermal expansion — too little = bottle bursts; too much = poor heat transfer at the center) 4. **Hermetic Sealing** - Crown cap (glass bottles) or heat-sealed aluminum foil (HDPE) - Seal integrity is **non-negotiable** — a single micro-leak defeats the entire process 5. **Loading into the Retort/Autoclave** - Bottles are arranged in crates with adequate spacing for steam circulation - Retort door is sealed; air is purged with steam 6. **Heating (Come-Up, Holding, Cooling Phases)** - **Come-up time (CUT):** 10–15 min to reach sterilization temperature - **Holding phase:** 108–115 °C for 15–40 min (depending on bottle size — 200 mL bottles need ~15 min; 1 L bottles need ~30–40 min because the slowest heating zone is the geometric center of the bottle) - **Cooling phase:** Steam is replaced by cold water; cooling must be rapid to avoid over-cooking and to bring the bottle to < 40 °C within ~30 min. Counter-pressure cooling is used to prevent cap blow-off (a 1 L bottle at 115 °C develops internal pressure > 4 atm if not counter-pressurized). 7. **Storage** - Bottles are dried, labeled, and stored at ambient temperature. Shelf life: **6–12 months unopened**. > [!NOTE] > **The Slowest Heating Zone (SHZ) in a bottle** is its **geometric center** — the point furthest from the heating surface. This is *why* large bottles need longer holding times than small ones. KTU examiners frequently frame a question around this concept. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning | Unit | Use in In-Bottle Sterilization | |---|---|---|---|---| | **D-value** (Decimal reduction time) | $D_T = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)}$ | Time at temperature $T$ to destroy **90 %** of a microbial population | minutes | D₁₂₁ = 0.3–1.0 min for *B. stearothermophilus* | | **Z-value** | $Z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10} D_1 - \log_{10} D_2}$ | Temperature (°C) required to reduce D-value by 90 % (a 10-fold change) | °C | Z = 10 °C for *C. botulinum* | | **F-value (Lethality)** | $F_T = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{Z}} \, dt$ | Equivalent heating time at reference temperature $T_{ref}$ | minutes | $F_0$ uses $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $Z = 10$ °C | | **F₀ for 12-D botulinum cook** | $F_0 \geq 12 \cdot D_{121}$ | Minimum lethality for commercial sterility | minutes | Typically $F_0 \geq 3$ min (with $D_{121} = 0.25$ min) | | **Q-value (Cook value / B\*)** | $B = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - 100}{Z_c}} \, dt$ | Quantifies **nutrient/food-quality degradation** (not just microbial kill) | minutes | $Z_c$ for chemical/quality changes ≈ **33 °C** | | **Rate of Heat Transfer (Newton's Law of Cooling for retort)** | $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{steam} - T_{product})$ | Heat flux from steam to milk | W | Governs come-up time | | **Lethal Rate (L)** | $L = 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}}$ | Instantaneous microbial kill rate relative to reference | dimensionless | Used in $F_0 = \int L \, dt$ | > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer sheets, always write the **Z-value explicitly** when using F₀. Writing "F₀ = 3 min" without specifying Z = 10 °C and T_ref = 121.1 °C is incomplete and may cost marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The same F₀ / Z / D framework is the **backbone of every retort, autoclave, and aseptic process design** in the global food industry. Whether you are designing a baby-food jar sterilization line, a pet-food canning process, or an autoclave for hospital instruments, the **lethality calculation methodology is identical**. Mastering it for in-bottle sterilization therefore equips you to enter the wider food-thermal-processing industry (FMCG, defence rations, pharmaceutical sterilization). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From First-Order Microbial Death Kinetics to the F₀ Equation This is the derivation KTU examiners most often ask students to reproduce in Part B. We will proceed from the **exponential death model** to the **F₀ formula** in clean, inspectable steps. **Step 1 — Empirical Observation of Microbial Death** When a population of vegetative cells or spores is heated at a constant lethal temperature, the surviving population $N$ decreases **exponentially with time**. Empirically: $$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N $$ where $k$ is the **specific death rate** (min⁻¹), a function of temperature. **Step 2 — Integration to Obtain Survivor Curve** Separating variables and integrating from initial count $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N_t$ at time $t$: $$ \int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_0^t dt $$ $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t $$ $$ N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} $$ **Step 3 — Switch to base-10 logarithm (industrial convention)** The food industry uses $\log_{10}$, so we convert: $$ \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot t}{\ln(10)} = -\frac{k \cdot t}{2.3026} $$ **Step 4 — Define the D-value** The **D-value** is defined as the time required for a **10-fold (one log) reduction** in population, i.e., the time to make $\log_{10}(N_0/N_t) = 1$: $$ D_T = \frac{1}{k / 2.3026} = \frac{2.3026}{k} $$ So, in general: $$ \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \frac{t}{D_T} $$ **Step 5 — Temperature dependence of k and D** Empirically, both $k$ and $D$ vary with temperature in a **log-linear (Arrhenius-like) fashion**. Plotting $\log_{10}(D)$ vs. $T$ gives a straight line. The slope of that line, when expressed as the temperature change needed for a 10-fold change in $D$, is the **Z-value**: $$ \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z} $$ Rearranging to express $D$ at any temperature $T$ in terms of $D_{ref}$ at $T_{ref}$: $$ D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{\frac{T_{ref} - T}{Z}} $$ **Step 6 — The Instantaneous Lethal Rate $L$** Define the **lethal rate** at temperature $T$ as the ratio of the D-value at the reference temperature to the D-value at $T$: $$ L = \frac{D_{ref}}{D_T} = 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{Z}} $$ This dimensionless number tells you *how many "reference-temperature minutes" one real minute at temperature $T$ is worth*. For $T = T_{ref}$, $L = 1$. For $T > T_{ref}$, $L > 1$ (faster killing). **Step 7 — Integrate Over the Real Process Time to Get F₀** The total **lethality delivered** to the product is the time integral of the lethal rate: $$ F_{ref}^{Z} = \int_0^{t_{process}} L(t) \, dt = \int_0^{t_{process}} 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{Z}} \, dt $$ For commercial milk sterilization, the conventional reference state is $T_{ref} = 121.1 \text{ °C}$ and $Z = 10 \text{ °C}$, giving the famous: $$ \boxed{F_0 = \int_0^t 10^{\frac{T(t) - 121.1}{10}} \, dt} $$ **Step 8 — Discrete Approximation for KTU Calculations** In practice, the temperature profile $T(t)$ is sampled at intervals (e.g., every minute). The integral is approximated as: $$ F_0 \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i \cdot 10^{\frac{T_i - 121.1}{10}} $$ A worked example is given in Section 5 (Part B, sub-question). ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: F₀ for a Typical In-Bottle Process A 500 mL glass-bottle retort run records the following temperature at the slowest heating zone (bottle center): | Time (min) | T (°C) | $\Delta t$ (min) | $L = 10^{(T-121.1)/10}$ | $L \cdot \Delta t$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 78 | 5 | 0.0005 | 0.0025 | | 5 | 95 | 5 | 0.0251 | 0.126 | | 10 | 108 | 5 | 0.501 | 2.505 | | 15 | 112 | 5 | 1.230 | 6.150 | | 20 | 113 | 5 | 1.585 | 7.925 | | 25 | 113 | 5 | 1.585 | 7.925 | | 30 | 100 | 5 | 0.0794 | 0.397 | | 35 | 70 | 5 | 0.0008 | 0.004 | $$ F_0 = \sum L \cdot \Delta t \approx 0.0025 + 0.126 + 2.505 + 6.150 + 7.925 + 7.925 + 0.397 + 0.004 $$ $$ F_0 \approx 25.04 \text{ min} $$ Since $F_0 \geq 3$ min is required for commercial sterility, this run delivers **~8× the required lethality** — i.e., it is safe but over-processed (excess cook). KTU follow-up: *calculate the corresponding cook value B\* using $Z_c = 33 \text{ °C}$ and discuss the quality implications.* ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Where Each Component of the System Lives | Component | Engineering Function | Critical Control Point (CCP) | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---| | **Raw milk quality** | Initial microbial load (TPC < 10⁵ CFU/mL) | Pre-processing test | "You cannot sterilize your way out of bad milk." | | **Bottle (glass/HDPE)** | Process vessel + final package | Crack inspection, chlorine rinse | Large bottles = longer lethality time | | **Crown/seal cap** | Hermetic closure | Seal torque, visual inspection | One leak = entire batch compromised | | **Retort (autoclave)** | Delivers steam at controlled P–T | T, P, time instrumentation | Counter-pressure cooling mandatory | | **Slowest heating zone (SHZ)** | Reference point for lethality | Thermocouple probe in center bottle | Always the geometric center | | **Cooling water** | Stops cook, sets partial vacuum | Residual chlorine 1–2 ppm | Contaminated cooling water = post-process recontamination | | **Storage** | Maintains sterility | Cool, dry, dark | Avoids Maillard browning / riboflavin loss | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Milk Reception
TPC and Antibiotic Test] --> B[Standardization
Fat to 3.5 percent] B --> C[Homogenization
14 to 17 MPa at 65 C] C --> D[Pre heating
to 70 to 80 C] D --> E[Hot Filling
into clean bottles] E --> F[Hermetic Sealing
Crown cap or foil seal] F --> G[Load Crates into Retort
Air purge with steam] G --> H[Come Up Phase
to 108 to 115 C] H --> I[Holding Phase
15 to 40 min] I --> J[Counter Pressure Cooling
to 40 C in 25 min] J --> K[Drying and Labeling] K --> L[Ambient Storage
Shelf life 6 to 12 months] L --> M((Consumer)) ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heat and Mass Transfer in the Retort ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph R[Retort Autoclave] S1[Steam Generator] -->|saturated steam at 121 C| S2[Steam Distribution Manifold] S2 --> S3[Retort Chamber with Bottle Crates] end subgraph B[Inside a Sealed Bottle] M1[Milk at Geometric Center
Slowest Heating Zone SHZ] M2[Milk at Bottle Wall
Fastest Heating Zone] end S3 -->|convective heat transfer| M2 M2 -.->|conduction across bottle wall and milk| M1 M1 -->|temperature profile T of t is recorded| TC[Thermocouple Probe and Data Logger] TC -->|computes F0 integral| F0[F0 Calculation Module] F0 -->|validates F0 greater than 3 min| OK[Process OK] F0 -->|if F0 less than 3 min| NG[Process FAIL Reject Batch] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: In-Bottle vs. UHT vs. Pasteurization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INB[In Bottle Sterilization] I1[Fill into bottle] --> I2[Seal] --> I3[Heat in retort 108 to 115 C 15 to 40 min] --> I4[Cool] end subgraph UHT[UHT Sterilization] U1[Pre heat] --> U2[Heat 140 to 150 C for 2 to 5 s] --> U3[Cool] --> U4[Aseptic fill into pre sterilized package] end subgraph PAS[Pasteurization] P1[Heat 72 C for 15 s] --> P2[Cool] --> P3[Refrigerated storage] end ``` ### 4.4 Why Each Stage is Architecturally Non-Negotiable - **Filling *before* sealing** = ensures no headspace air; milk is heated uniformly. - **Sealing *before* retort** = bottle acts as hermetic barrier during lethality. - **Counter-pressure cooling** = external water pressure balances internal steam pressure, preventing cap blow-off. - **Geometric center monitoring** = the SHZ; if the center is sterile, the rest is sterile by inference. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define commercial sterility. Why is an $F_0 \geq 3$ min considered sufficient for in-bottle sterilized milk, and what is the underlying microbial target? **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Definition of commercial sterility (1 mark):** Commercial sterility is the condition in which a thermally processed food is free from *viable* microorganisms capable of growing in the product at normal non-refrigerated storage conditions. It does **not** mean absolute sterility (i.e., total absence of microbes) — some thermophilic spores may survive but cannot germinate. 2. **Why $F_0 \geq 3$ min (1 mark):** The reference organism is *Clostridium botulinum* Type B (and surrogate *Bacillus stearothermophilus*). Its $D_{121.1} \approx 0.25$ min at $Z = 10$ °C. A 12-D reduction (12 log kill) requires: $$ F_0 = 12 \times 0.25 = 3 \text{ min} $$ This 12-log reduction is the *botulinum cook* standard established by the National Canners Association (now Grocery Manufacturers Association) and is the universal benchmark. 3. **Outcome (1 mark):** Such a process delivers milk with no surviving *C. botulinum* spores and is safe for ambient storage for 6–12 months in unopened bottles. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** List **three** key advantages and **three** key disadvantages of in-bottle sterilization of milk. **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per pair, max 3):** | Advantages (any 3) | Disadvantages (any 3) | |---|---| | Equipment is simple; needs only a steam retort (low capital vs. UHT line) | Strong "cooked" / caramelized flavor due to prolonged high heat | | Produces truly *shelf-stable* product without refrigeration or preservatives | Significant loss of heat-labile vitamins (Vitamin C, B₁, B₆, folate) | | No risk of *post-process* recontamination (since bottle is sealed before heating) | High energy consumption per liter (long come-up + hold + cool) | | Glass bottles are reusable, recyclable, and inert (no leaching) | Slow, batch process → low throughput; difficult to scale to high volumes | | Milk has a clean, "old-fashioned" taste preferred in some regional markets | Bottle breakage risk during retort (loss of batch) and weight adds transport cost | | Quality of opaque / flavoured milk drinks is good (no aseptic tank residence) | Maillard browning, riboflavin degradation on long storage | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall — Do Not Mix Up Disadvantages of "In-Bottle" with "UHT".** > UHT disadvantages are different (e.g., age-gelation, cooked flavor *also* present but milder; *no* bottle breakage; *no* glass weight). Examiners penalize students who copy-paste UHT cons onto in-bottle questions. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **Instructions for students:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **process flow** of in-bottle sterilization of milk, including pre-treatment, filling, sealing, retort operation, and post-processing. Draw a labelled flow diagram. **(b) [7 marks)** Using the **F₀ formulation**, explain how you would determine whether a given retort run has delivered commercial sterility. Given the following temperature profile at the slowest heating zone, calculate $F_0$ and comment on adequacy: | Time (min) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 28 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | T (°C) | 80 | 100 | 110 | 113 | 113 | 113 | 105 | 85 | Use $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C and $Z = 10$ °C. **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Pre-treatment (1 mark):** Standardization to required fat (3.5 % or 1.5 %); homogenization at 14–17 MPa, 60–65 °C (two-stage) to prevent creaming; pre-heating to 70–80 °C to shorten retort CUT and degas milk. 2. **Filling (1 mark):** Hot-fill into clean, sanitized bottles at 70–80 °C; controlled headspace (6–10 %); bottles are visually inspected for cracks. 3. **Sealing (1 mark):** Hermetic closure with crown cap (glass) or induction-sealed aluminum foil (HDPE); integrity is critical. 4. **Retort operation (2 marks):** Load into crates; close retort; purge air with steam. **Three phases**: come-up (10–15 min to 108–115 °C), hold (15–40 min at target T), counter-pressure cooling (rapid drop to < 40 °C, with pressurised water at ~0.5–1 bar to prevent cap blow-off). 5. **Post-processing (1 mark):** Drying, labeling, ambient storage (6–12 months unopened). 6. **Diagram (1 mark):** The Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 is acceptable; hand-drawn equivalent in exam earns full credit. **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** **Theory — F₀ formulation (3 marks):** $$ F_0 = \sum \Delta t_i \cdot 10^{\frac{T_i - 121.1}{Z}} $$ with $Z = 10$ °C, $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C. Note that the lethal rate is *only* significant when $T > 100$ °C; below this, microbial kill is negligible. **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** | Interval | $T_i$ (°C) | $\Delta t = 4$ min | $L = 10^{(T-121.1)/10}$ | $L \cdot \Delta t$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0–4 | 100 | 4 | $10^{-2.11} = 0.00776$ | 0.031 | | 4–8 | 110 | 4 | $10^{-1.11} = 0.0776$ | 0.310 | | 8–12 | 113 | 4 | $10^{-0.81} = 0.1549$ | 0.620 | | 12–16 | 113 | 4 | $10^{-0.81} = 0.1549$ | 0.620 | | 16–20 | 113 | 4 | $10^{-0.81} = 0.1549$ | 0.620 | | 20–24 | 105 | 4 | $10^{-1.61} = 0.0245$ | 0.098 | | 24–28 | 85 | 4 | $10^{-3.61} = 0.000245$ | 0.001 | $$ F_0 \approx 0.031 + 0.310 + 0.620 + 0.620 + 0.620 + 0.098 + 0.001 $$ $$ \boxed{F_0 \approx 2.30 \text{ min}} $$ **Comment (1 mark):** The required $F_0 \geq 3$ min is **not met**. The process is **under-processed**; the batch **must be rejected** or reprocessed. To fix: extend the holding phase by ~5 min at 113 °C (each additional minute at 113 °C adds $0.155$ min of $F_0$; 5 min adds ~0.78 min → new $F_0 \approx 3.08$ min). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall — Sign of the Exponent.** Many students write $10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$ with $T = 100$ and get $10^{-2.11} \approx 0.0078$. Others mistakenly write $10^{(T_{ref} - T)/Z}$ and get $128$ — this is *wrong* and indicates conceptual confusion. Examiners specifically test this sign convention. --- ### **Question B (14 marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Compare in-bottle sterilization with UHT processing under the following heads: (i) temperature–time regime, (ii) equipment, (iii) type of packaging required, (iv) nutrient retention, (v) shelf life, (vi) flavor, (vii) cost. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **major advantages and disadvantages** of in-bottle sterilization. With which types of milk products and markets is this process most compatible, and why has it been largely displaced by UHT in modern industry? **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:** | Head | In-Bottle Sterilization | UHT Sterilization | |---|---|---| | (i) T–t regime (1 mark) | 108–115 °C, 15–40 min (batch) | 140–150 °C, 2–5 s (continuous) | | (ii) Equipment (1 mark) | Steam retort/autoclave, bottle washer, crown capper | Plate/tubular/scraped-surface heat exchanger, aseptic filler | | (iii) Packaging (1 mark) | Glass or HDPE bottle (process vessel = package) | Pre-sterilized multi-layer carton, PET, or pouch (separate from process) | | (iv) Nutrient retention (1 mark) | Lower — significant loss of Vitamin C, B₁, B₆, folate; lysine destruction | Better — residence time is short; ~ 90 % of vitamins retained | | (v) Shelf life (1 mark) | 6–12 months at ambient (unopened) | 3–6 months at ambient (unopened) — slightly shorter but adequate | | (vi) Flavor (1 mark) | Pronounced cooked / caramelised; darker color | Lighter cooked flavor; closer to pasteurized milk taste | | (vii) Cost (1 mark) | High energy per litre; low equipment cost; high labour and breakage | Low energy per litre; high capital; low labour; scalable | **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:** **Advantages (3 marks — 1 mark per cluster):** 1. **Simplicity & low capital:** A retort, a bottle washer and a capper are all that is needed; no aseptic tank, no sterile chamber, no HEPA-filtered filling room. Small dairies can install it. 2. **No post-process recontamination risk:** Because the bottle is sealed **before** heat treatment, there is no possibility of microbes entering between the process and the package — a critical safety feature. 3. **Reusable, recyclable, inert packaging:** Glass bottles do not leach chemicals, do not absorb flavors, and can be reused 30–50 times — sustainable packaging image. **Disadvantages (3 marks — 1 mark per cluster):** 1. **Quality degradation:** Cooked flavor, browning (Maillard), riboflavin photodegradation, 30–80 % loss of vitamin C, and loss of available lysine. 2. **Operational inefficiency:** Batch process with long come-up + hold + cool cycle (often 1.5–2 h per batch); low throughput; bottle breakage rate 1–3 % per cycle; heavy bottles increase transport cost. 3. **Capital-light but energy-heavy:** Steam retorts consume large amounts of energy per litre compared with UHT, making them uneconomical in large-scale industrial settings. **Displacement by UHT (1 mark):** Modern dairies prefer UHT + aseptic packaging because of: - Continuous (not batch) operation → high throughput - Better nutrient and sensory retention - Lighter, cheaper, unbreakable cartons - No bottle washing logistics In-bottle sterilization survives in **specialty / premium / regional / military / low-volume-flavoured** markets where either UHT equipment is uneconomical or where consumers value the "traditional" taste and glass packaging. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall — Don't claim UHT is "perfect" either.** Examiners expect you to note UHT *also* has drawbacks (e.g., age gelation on long storage, residual cooked flavor, expensive aseptic packaging). A balanced comparison (not biased) is what scores the full 7 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **In-bottle sterilization = batch, in-container, terminal, lethal heat process.** Distinct from UHT (continuous, before-packaging). - **Standard regime:** 108–115 °C, 15–40 min, $F_0 \geq 3$ min, with $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C and $Z = 10$ °C. - **Commercial sterility ≠ absolute sterility.** It is the absence of *viable, toxin-forming* organisms under defined storage conditions. Reference target: 12-D inactivation of *C. botulinum*. - **The slowest heating zone (SHZ) in a bottle is its geometric center** — this is where the thermocouple must be placed and this is the reference for lethality. - **Counter-pressure cooling is mandatory** to prevent cap blow-off — bottles at 115 °C develop internal steam pressure > 4 atm. - **Homogenization is essential** before filling — without it, cream rises during long storage forming a thick plug in the neck. - **Headspace must be 6–10 %** of bottle volume — too little causes bottle burst; too much causes poor heat transfer. - **F₀ formula is THE scoring equation for Part B:** $$ F_0 = \sum \Delta t_i \cdot 10^{\frac{T_i - 121.1}{10}} $$ - **Sign of the exponent is $(T - 121.1)/10$** — never $(121.1 - T)/10$. - **The cook value $B^*$ uses $Z_c = 33$ °C** (not 10 °C) — this is the *chemical* Z-value for nutrient/quality degradation. KTU sometimes asks you to compute B\* alongside F₀. - **Three core advantages of in-bottle sterilization:** simple equipment, no post-process recontamination, glass is inert/recyclable. - **Three core disadvantages:** cooked flavor + nutrient loss, high energy per litre, batch process (low throughput) + bottle breakage. - **Where it survives today:** specialty/premium milk, flavoured milk, military rations, rural / off-grid markets, and some European (e.g., German *H-Milch* in glass) traditions. - **Bottle material matters:** Type III glass is standard; Type I borosilicate is rarely used (overkill, expensive). HDPE is the modern alternative but limits retort temperature to ~ 110 °C. - **Compared to pasteurization (72 °C, 15 s):** in-bottle sterilization is a *commercial sterility* process (kills spores), not just a *pathogen* process (kills vegetative cells only). - **KTU favourite exam hooks:** "Define commercial sterility," "Calculate F₀," "Why is the geometric center critical?", "Compare with UHT," "Explain counter-pressure cooling."

topics.title: UHT Processing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** UHT Processing

# Module 2 — UHT Processing of Milk

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **UHT (Ultra High Temperature) Processing** is defined by the *Codex Alimentarius* and *FSSAI* (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) regulations as the continuous thermal treatment of milk (or liquid dairy products) at a temperature of **not less than 135 °C for a holding time of not less than 1 second**, such that the product achieves **commercial sterility** (i.e., the destruction of all viable spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms and their spores, ensuring chemical, microbiological, and enzymatic stability at ambient storage conditions for an extended shelf-life).

The canonical reference condition most widely quoted in dairy engineering literature is:

$$\boxed{\,T \;\geq\; 135\,^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{with} \quad t \;\geq\; 1\ \text{s}\,}$$

This is sufficient to reduce the natural microbial load of raw milk to a level that allows **ambient-temperature shelf-life of 6–9 months** (unopened) when coupled with **aseptic packaging**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure-Cooker vs. Frying-Pan" Mental Model

Think of milk as a *biological city* filled with bacteria, spores, enzymes, and nutrients. To "sterilise" this city, you have two broad strategies:

| Household Method | Dairy Equivalent | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure-cooker (135 °C, short time)** | **UHT Processing** | Kills everything including tough spores; product still tastes fresh because heat exposure is brief. |
| **Slow oven roasting (121 °C, 15 min)** | **In-bottle Sterilisation** | Same sterility goal, but milk develops a strong cooked/caramelised flavour due to long heat exposure. |

So UHT is essentially the *pressure-cooker philosophy* applied to milk — very hot, very short, very fresh-tasting. The "trick" is that the milk spends only **2–8 seconds** above 100 °C, which is too short for serious Maillard browning or vitamin destruction, but long enough to obliterate *Clostridium botulinum* spores (the most heat-resistant pathogen of public-health concern in low-acid foods).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Commercial sterility ≠ absolute sterility.** A commercially sterile UHT milk can still contain a handful of *thermoduric* spores per litre. These are harmless (non-toxigenic, non-proteolytic) and cannot germinate at ambient temperature for the projected shelf-life. This is why UHT milk is **shelf-stable but not shelf-infinite**.

### 1.3 Historical and Regulatory Context

The UHT process was pioneered by **H. A. Bendixen** in Denmark (1910s) and first commercialised in the 1940s–50s. Modern KTU syllabus expectations require students to know that:

- **FSSAI (Food Safety & Standards Regulations, 2011, amended 2020)** recognise UHT as a permitted heat treatment for "long-life" milk.
- **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2003** specifies the minimum time-temperature combination.
- **EU Directive 92/46/EEC** and **US FDA 21 CFR 131** also accept 135 °C / 1 s as the minimum UHT condition.

### 1.4 Distinction from Related Thermal Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> UHT is **not** the same as the following processes, although they sound similar:

| Process | Typical Condition | Shelf Life | Cold Chain Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurisation (HTST) | 72 °C / 15 s | 14–21 days | **Yes** |
| Pasteurisation (LTLT) | 63 °C / 30 min | 5–7 days | **Yes** |
| **UHT** | **135 °C / 1–4 s** | **6–9 months** | **No (unopened)** |
| In-bottle Sterilisation | 110–121 °C / 15–30 min | 6–12 months | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microbiological Objective — "Why 135 °C / 1 s?"

The target organism that **defines the design of any sterilising process** is *Clostridium botulinum* type A, which has a $D_{121.1} \approx 0.21$ s and a $z$-value of $\approx 10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Using these kinetic parameters, the classical **12-D concept** (12 log reduction = 10¹²-fold inactivation, leaving ≤ 10⁻⁶ spores per litre) yields a minimum lethality of:

$$F_0 \;=\; D_{121.1} \times 12 \;\approx\; 2.52\ \text{min}$$

For an UHT line operating at 135 °C, we need an equivalent lethality. The general lethality expression is:

$$F_0 \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\,(T(t) - 121.1)\,/\,z}\, dt$$

For a constant-temperature hold at $T = 135\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $z = 10$:

$$F_0 \;=\; 10^{(135 - 121.1)/10} \times t \;=\; 10^{1.39} \times t \;\approx\; 24.55\,t$$

Setting $F_0 = 2.52$ min:

$$t_{\min} \;=\; \frac{2.52}{24.55} \;\approx\; 0.103\ \text{min} \;\approx\; 6.2\ \text{s}$$

So a *minimum* hold of ~6 s at 135 °C is the *theoretical lower bound for true sterility*. In practice, design engineers use **2–8 s** with a **safety factor of ~1.5**, giving the canonical UHT condition of **135 °C for 2–4 s**.

## 2.2 Two Principal UHT Heating Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU examiners **consistently** test the difference between **Direct** and **Indirect** UHT systems. Memorise this section.

### 2.2.1 Direct Heating Systems

The product is mixed **directly** with culinary-grade steam. Because there is no wall barrier, heat transfer is almost instantaneous and residence time above 100 °C is < 1 s. Two sub-types exist:

| Sub-type | How it Works | Holding Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Injection (SI)** | High-pressure steam (typically 4–6 bar) is injected *into* the milk stream via a nozzle. | 2–4 s | Best for thin, non-viscous liquids (white milk, whey). |
| **Steam Infusion (SF)** | Milk is sprayed *into* a chamber of pressurised steam. | 2–4 s | Better for viscous or particulate products (cream, milk with added powders). |

**Critical Step — Vacuum Cooling (Flash Cooler):** After holding, the milk is led into an *expansion chamber* (vacuum at ~0.3 bar). The water added as steam instantly "flashes" off, bringing the temperature back to ~80 °C in milliseconds. This *removes* the extra water that was added, restoring the milk's original total solids. Mass balance must be exact:

$$m_{\text{milk,in}} + m_{\text{steam}} \;=\; m_{\text{milk,out}} + m_{\text{vapour,out}}$$

### 2.2.2 Indirect Heating Systems

Heating occurs **through a metal wall** (plate or tubular). No product-steam contact, no dilution, no flash cooler needed. Three sub-types:

| Sub-type | Heat Transfer Surface | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | Standard gasketed plates | Most common; used for 70 % of UHT milk. |
| **Tubular Heat Exchanger (THE)** | Stainless-steel tubes, often corrugated | Better for fouling-prone products (cream, fortified milk). |
| **Scraped-Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE)** | Cylinder with rotating blades | Viscous, sticky, or particulate products (ice-cream mix, cheese milk pre-cheese). |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Direct systems** are gentler on flavour but require **expensive** boiler-grade steam, **precise** mass-flow control, and **aseptic** downstream design.  
> **Indirect systems** are cheaper, simpler, and don't change composition, but have a **higher fouling tendency** (milk-stone deposition).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical UHT Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decimal Reduction Time | $D_T = D_{T_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 10^{(T_{\text{ref}} - T)/z}$ | $T$ = °C, $D$ in s, $z$ in °C | $D_{135} \approx 4.5$ s for *C. botulinum* |
| 2 | Z-value | $z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_{T_1} - \log D_{T_2}}$ | Slope of D-curve, °C | 8–10 °C for bacterial spores |
| 3 | Sterilisation Value | $F_0 = \int 10^{(T(t)-121.1)/z}\,dt$ | Time in min | 2.5–15 min (target) |
| 4 | Equivalent UHT time | $t_{UHT} = F_0 \cdot 10^{(121.1 - T)/z}$ | At 135 °C, t in s | 1–8 s |
| 5 | Steam mass balance | $m_s = m_p (C_p / \lambda)(T_{out} - T_{in})$ | $m_s$ = steam, $C_p$ = specific heat (≈ 3.9 kJ/kg·K), $\lambda$ = latent heat (≈ 2 100 kJ/kg) | ~7–9 % of milk mass |
| 6 | Reynolds Number (tubular) | $Re = \dfrac{4 \dot m}{\pi d \mu}$ | $\dot m$ = kg/s, $d$ = m, $\mu$ = Pa·s | 10 000–50 000 (turbulent) |
| 7 | Plate HE area | $A = \dfrac{\dot m C_p}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | $U$ ≈ 2 000–3 000 W/m²·K (milk-steam) | 4–8 m² for 5 000 L/h line |
| 8 | Q-value (C-Mn-Na browning) | $Q = \int 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z_Q}\,dt$ | $z_Q \approx 26\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Should be < 1 |

> [!NOTE]  
> In markdown tables, the absolute value of a variable is written using the LaTeX macros `\vert x \vert` (not the pipe character) to avoid breaking the table.

## 2.4 The Fouling Problem — The "Achilles Heel" of Indirect UHT

> [!WARNING]  
> Milk proteins (especially $\beta$-lactoglobulin and caseins) denature and deposit on hot metal surfaces above 110 °C. This is called **fouling**. It:
> - Reduces heat transfer coefficient $U$ by 30–60 %,
> - Causes burn-on flavours,
> - Forces **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place)** every 6–10 hours.
>
> Direct UHT systems largely avoid fouling because of the very short hot residence time and the absence of hot metal walls.

### 2.5 Aseptic Packaging — The Indispensable Partner

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> UHT treatment alone is **useless** without aseptic packaging. The hot, sterile product must enter a sterilised container, in a sterilised environment, and be sealed — all without recontamination.

Common aseptic packaging formats relevant to KTU syllabus:

- **Tetra Brik Aseptic (TBA)** — multi-layer laminated paperboard (polyethylene / aluminium / paperboard / polyethylene).
- **Tetra Fino / Tetra Rex** — for PET bottles.
- **Bag-in-Box (BIB)** — 5–20 L for institutional use.
- **Cups (Pure-Pak)** — 200 mL portion packs.

The packaging material is sterilised by either:
- **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) bath** (35 %, 70 °C) followed by hot-air drying, OR
- **Saturated steam + H₂O₂ vapour** (modern Tetra systems), OR
- **Electron-beam / gamma** sterilisation (pre-formed pouches).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles Why "135 °C / 1 s" is the Minimum

We will derive the *minimum* UHT lethality from microbiological kinetics.

### Step 1 — Start with the First-Order Death Kinetics

For a bacterial spore population, thermal destruction follows first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} \;=\; -k(T)\,N(t)$$

where $N(t)$ is the spore count at time $t$, and $k(T)$ is the temperature-dependent death rate constant.

### Step 2 — Solve the Differential Equation

Integrating from $N_0$ to $N$ over a holding time $t$ at constant $T$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} \;=\; -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

This gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) \;=\; k\,t$$

Exponentiating:

$$\frac{N_0}{N} \;=\; e^{k\,t} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N(t) \;=\; N_0\,e^{-k\,t}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Decimal Reduction Time D_T

By definition, $D_T$ is the time to reduce the population by 90 % (1 log), i.e., $N = 0.1 N_0$ at $t = D_T$:

$$0.1\,N_0 \;=\; N_0\,e^{-k\,D_T} \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{-k\,D_T} \;=\; 10^{-1}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$-k\,D_T \;=\; -\ln 10 \;\Longrightarrow\; k \;=\; \frac{\ln 10}{D_T} \;\approx\; \frac{2.303}{D_T}$$

### Step 4 — Generalise to Any Temperature via the z-Model

Empirically, $\log D_T$ is linear in $T$ with slope $-1/z$:

$$\log_{10} D_T \;=\; \log_{10} D_{T_{\text{ref}}} \;-\; \frac{T - T_{\text{ref}}}{z}$$

Equivalently:

$$D_T \;=\; D_{T_{\text{ref}}}\cdot 10^{(T_{\text{ref}} - T)/z}$$

For *Clostridium botulinum* type A spores in phosphate buffer:

- $D_{121.1} = 0.21\ \text{s}$ (literature standard, validated in multiple peer-reviewed studies)
- $z = 10.0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (conservative design value)

### Step 5 — Compute D at 135 °C

Substituting $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$, $T = 135$, $z = 10$:

$$D_{135} \;=\; 0.21 \times 10^{(121.1 - 135)/10} \;=\; 0.21 \times 10^{-1.39}$$

Now:

$$10^{-1.39} \;=\; 10^{-2} \times 10^{0.61} \;=\; 0.01 \times 4.074 \;=\; 0.04074$$

Therefore:

$$D_{135} \;=\; 0.21 \times 0.04074 \;\approx\; 8.55 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} \;\approx\; 8.6\ \text{ms}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This is the time to achieve a *single* 1-log (90 %) reduction at 135 °C. Each additional second therefore achieves roughly $1/D_{135} \approx 117$ logs of inactivation per second — clearly overkill in theory, but design uses only a *few* seconds for practical reasons (flow stability, residence time distribution, fouling window).

### Step 6 — Apply the 12-D Sterilisation Criterion

The 12-D rule requires:

$$\frac{N_0}{N} \;=\; 10^{12} \;\Longrightarrow\; t_{12D} \;=\; 12 \times D_{135}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{12D} \;=\; 12 \times 8.55\ \text{ms} \;\approx\; 103\ \text{ms} \;\approx\; 0.1\ \text{s}$$

This is the *theoretical* minimum time. **In practice, the holding time is extended to 2–8 s** to provide:
- A safety factor of **20–80×** above the minimum,
- Tolerance to residence-time distribution (RTD) of the holding tube,
- Tolerance to ±1 °C controller fluctuation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Sterilisation Value Calculation

> **Problem.** A UHT line holds milk at 137 °C for 3.0 s in a tube. Using $z = 10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and reference temperature 121.1 °C, calculate the equivalent $F_0$ value. Is it adequate for commercial sterility?

**Solution.**

The reference lethancy at any instant is:

$$L(t) \;=\; 10^{(T(t) - 121.1)/z}$$

For a constant-temperature hold of duration $t$:

$$F_0 \;=\; t \times 10^{(T - 121.1)/z}$$

Substituting $T = 137\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $t = 3.0$ s $= 0.05$ min, $z = 10$:

$$F_0 \;=\; 0.05 \times 10^{(137 - 121.1)/10} \;=\; 0.05 \times 10^{1.59}$$

Now:

$$10^{1.59} \;=\; 10^{0.59} \times 10 \;=\; 3.89 \times 10 \;\approx\; 38.9$$

Therefore:

$$F_0 \;\approx\; 0.05 \times 38.9 \;\approx\; 1.95\ \text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> Industry target for UHT milk is $F_0 \geq 3$ min. The calculated 1.95 min is **borderline**. The plant manager should either (a) raise $T$ to ~138.5 °C, or (b) extend $t$ to ~4.5 s, to bring $F_0$ above 3 min.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Direct vs. Indirect UHT

| Parameter | Direct (Steam Injection) | Direct (Steam Infusion) | Indirect (Plate) | Indirect (Tubular) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hold time above 100 °C | < 1 s | < 1 s | 4–8 s | 4–8 s |
| Heat-transfer surface | None | None | Plates | Tubes |
| Steam quality required | **Boiler-grade (food grade)** | Boiler-grade | Process steam (separated) | Process steam |
| Flavour impact | Very low | Very low | Moderate cooked flavour | Moderate |
| Fouling risk | **Very low** | **Very low** | High | High |
| Capital cost (per 5 000 L/h) | **₹ 5–6 crore** | ₹ 5–6 crore | ₹ 2.5–3 crore | ₹ 3.5–4 crore |
| Operating cost | High (steam + vacuum) | High | Low | Low |
| Suitable for viscous products | Poor (SI) | **Good (SF)** | Poor | Moderate |
| Sterile-to-sterile aseptic valve required | **Yes (mandatory)** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Steam Mass Required for SI System

> **Problem.** Milk enters an SI UHT plant at $T_i = 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is heated to $T_o = 140\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ by direct steam injection. Milk mass flow $\dot m_p = 5\,000\ \text{kg/h}$. Calculate the mass of steam required per hour. Take $C_p$ for milk = 3.93 kJ/kg·°C, latent heat of steam $\lambda = 2\,100$ kJ/kg.

**Solution.**

Heat absorbed by the milk:

$$Q \;=\; \dot m_p \cdot C_p \cdot (T_o - T_i) \;=\; 5\,000 \times 3.93 \times (140 - 5)$$

Evaluating:

$$Q \;=\; 5\,000 \times 3.93 \times 135 \;=\; 5\,000 \times 530.55 \;=\; 2\,652\,750\ \text{kJ/h}$$

Mass of steam needed:

$$\dot m_s \;=\; \frac{Q}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2\,652\,750}{2\,100} \;\approx\; 1\,263.2\ \text{kg/h}$$

As a percentage of milk flow:

$$\frac{\dot m_s}{\dot m_p} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{1\,263.2}{5\,000} \times 100 \;\approx\; 25.3\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> But wait — this is *gross* steam. The vacuum flash cooler removes ~85 % of this water. The *net* water addition to the milk is therefore about 0.04 % of milk mass, which is **within regulatory tolerance** (≤ 0.1 %) and hence compensated by a small *upstream* TS standardisation step.

## 3.5 Residence Time Distribution (RTD) — Why We Use Laminar/Plug Tubes

> [!NOTE]  
> KTU examiners love asking: "Why is the UHT holding tube long and narrow?"

In laminar flow, the parabolic velocity profile causes some milk to spend *less* time in the tube than the *bulk* average. To ensure that the *fastest* element receives adequate lethality, we design for a Reynolds number $\leq 30\,000$ in the holding tube (typically 5 000–15 000), and we over-size the holding time by 20–50 % beyond the theoretical minimum.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Indirect UHT Plate-Heat-Exchanger Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawTank[Raw Milk Balance Tank] --> pump1[Feed Pump]
    pump1 --> regenPre[Regeneration Section Plate HE]
    regenPre --> heaterMain[Heating Section Plate HE Steam Side]
    heaterMain --> holdTube[Insulated Holding Tube 137 C 3 s]
    holdTube --> regenPost[Regeneration Cooling Section]
    regenPost --> cooler[Final Cooler Plate HE Chilled Water]
    cooler --> homo[Homogeniser Upstream of Sterile Loop]
    homo --> asepTank[Aseptic Buffer Tank N2 Blanketed]
    asepTank --> asepFill[Aseptic Filler Sterile Air Overpressure]
    asepFill --> pack[Finished UHT Carton]
    asepFill -.CIP loop.-> cip[CIP Module 80 C 90 C Caustic Acid]
    regenPre -.Recovers heat.-> regenPost
    holdTube -.F0 controller.-> control[PLC F0 / Time Temperature Integrator]
    control -.Trim signal.-> heaterMain
```

> [!NOTE]  
> In a **direct** system, the "Heating Section Plate HE" block is replaced by **Steam Injection Nozzle** + **Holding Tube** + **Flash Vacuum Vessel**, and the regeneration block is split into *pre*- and *post*-flash sections.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Direct UHT Steam-Injection (SI) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    balTank[Balance Tank] --> preHeat[Plate Preheater 85 C]
    preHeat --> pump1[High Pressure Pump 8 bar]
    pump1 --> inj[Steam Injection Nozzle Culinary Steam]
    inj --> hold[Insulated Holding Tube 140 C 2 to 4 s]
    hold --> flash[Flash Vacuum Vessel 0.3 bar 80 C]
    flash --> regen[Plate Regeneration]
    regen --> cooler[Chilled Water Final Cool 4 C]
    cooler --> asepTank[Aseptic Surge Tank]
    asepTank --> filler[Aseptic Filler]
    preHeat -.regen heat.-> regen
    flash -.vapour to condenser.-> condenser[Barometric Condenser]
    hold -.F0 probe.-> plc[PLC]
    plc -.safety vent.-> safetyDivert[3 Way Divert Valve to Reject]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **3-way divert valve** shown above is a *legal* requirement (per FSSAI and 3-A sanitary standards). If $F_0$ falls below the setpoint, the product is **automatically diverted** to a reject tank. This is one of the most heavily tested topics in KTU Module 2 viva-voce.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Aseptic Filling Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Pre-sterilise packaging material H2O2] --> b[Heated air drying 150 C]
    b --> c[Form carton in sterile air]
    c --> d[Filling chamber flooded with sterile HEPA air 0.3 micron]
    d --> e[Fill sterile product]
    e --> f[Ultrasonic seal top]
    f --> g[Apply strip straw]
    g --> h[Print batch code]
    h --> i[Carton to packer]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Pasteurised vs. UHT Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pasteurised[HTST Pasteurised]
    p1[Raw] --> p2[HTST 72 C 15 s] --> p3[Homogenise] --> p4[Cool 4 C] --> p5[Cold Storage 4 C] --> p6[Consumer Cold Chain Required]
    end
    subgraph UHT[UHT Long Life]
    u1[Raw] --> u2[UHT 135 C 2 to 4 s] --> u3[Flash cool or Regen] --> u4[Aseptic Fill] --> u5[Ambient Storage 25 C] --> u6[Consumer No Cold Chain]
    end
```
