# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]  
**Define UHT processing of milk. State the Codex Alimentarius minimum time-temperature condition.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> UHT (Ultra High Temperature) processing is the continuous thermal treatment of milk at a temperature **not less than 135 °C** for a holding time **not less than 1 second**, achieved through either direct or indirect heat exchange, followed by aseptic packaging in a pre-sterilised, hermetically sealed container. The objective is to render the product *commercially sterile* — i.e., free from viable spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms, including their heat-resistant spores, thereby conferring ambient-temperature shelf stability of 6–9 months. The Codex Alimentarius Standard (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2003) and FSSAI recognise **135 °C / 1 s** as the minimum legal UHT condition. The process is distinguished from pasteurisation (72 °C / 15 s, refrigerated) by its ability to destroy bacterial spores.

*(Marks: 1 — Definition; 1 — Codex condition with reference; 1 — Distinction from pasteurisation.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]  
**Differentiate between direct and indirect UHT heating methods with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Direct UHT systems** mix milk directly with culinary steam; examples are **Steam Injection (SI)** and **Steam Infusion (SF)**. Heating is nearly instantaneous, holding time above 100 °C is < 1 s, and a vacuum flash cooler is used downstream to remove condensed water.  
> **Indirect UHT systems** heat milk through a metal wall; examples are **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** and **Tubular Heat Exchanger (THE)**. No product–steam contact occurs, so no flash cooler is required, but fouling on hot surfaces is a serious issue.  
> In direct systems, residence time at peak temperature is shorter, flavour is better preserved, and the equipment is costlier. In indirect systems, capital and operating costs are lower, but cooked-flavour development is greater.

*(Marks: 1 — Core distinction; 1 — Examples; 1 — Consequence comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**  
> Common mark-deductions students suffer:
> - Writing "UHT = 140 °C / 15 s" — this is **wrong**; the *minimum* is 135 °C / 1 s.
> - Confusing **F₀ (sterilisation value)** with **P-value (pasteurisation value)**. They are different reference temperatures (121.1 vs 72 °C) and z-values.
> - Forgetting to mention **aseptic packaging** as a *prerequisite* for UHT to deliver shelf stability. UHT alone is half a process.
> - Mixing up **Steam Injection** (steam into milk) with **Steam Infusion** (milk into steam). Examiners explicitly test this.
> - Not converting seconds to minutes in $F_0$ calculations — a 1 s hold yields $F_0$ in *minutes*, not seconds.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **principle, construction, and operation of an Indirect UHT Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) plant** for milk. Include a labelled flow diagram and explain the role of the regeneration section. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A direct steam-injection UHT plant processes 10 000 L/h of milk. The milk enters at 6 °C and is heated to 138 °C. The steam is dry saturated at 4 bar abs. ($T_{sat} = 143.6\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $\lambda = 2\,137$ kJ/kg). Holding tube is at 138 °C for 3 s. Calculate: (i) the mass of steam injected per hour, (ii) the $F_0$ value, and (iii) whether the process is adequate for commercial sterility ($F_0 \geq 3$ min). $C_p$ of milk = 3.93 kJ/kg·°C; z = 10 °C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Indirect UHT Plate HE Plant

**Principle.** Milk is heated in a closed plate heat exchanger without any contact with the heating medium. Steam on one side of the plates transfers heat through the stainless steel to milk on the other side. After the holding tube, milk is cooled in a regeneration section, then in a final chilled-water section. The system is fully enclosed, sanitary, and operates under positive pressure until aseptic filling.

**Construction (Key Sections).**

1. **Balance tank (raw)** — accepts milk, prevents pump cavitation; may include a de-aerator (vacuum, 50–60 °C) to remove dissolved O₂ and CO₂ that otherwise cause off-flavour and fouling.
2. **Feed pump** — typically a centrifugal pump, sized for ~10 % higher capacity than nominal.
3. **Plate heat exchanger with 3–4 sections**:
   - **Pre-heating / regeneration section:** the *cold* incoming milk is warmed by the *hot* outgoing sterilised milk. Heat recovery is **90–95 %**, dramatically cutting steam and water costs.
   - **Heating section:** the milk is brought to the sterilisation temperature (typically 137–140 °C) using steam at 3–4 bar.
   - **Holding section:** an *insulated* tube (often U-shaped for floor-space economy) providing the 2–4 s residence time.
   - **Cooling / regeneration section:** milk is cooled by the *incoming* cold milk.
   - **Final cooler:** chilled water (1–2 °C) or propylene-glycol brings milk to 4 °C.
4. **Homogeniser** — placed either upstream of the heating section (most common) or between sections. Upstream placement ensures uniform heating and prevents fat separation.
5. **Aseptic surge tank (optional)** — N₂-blanketed, with HEPA-filtered sterile air above the product. Allows decoupled operation of the steriliser and the filler.
6. **Aseptic filler** — packaging material sterilised with H₂O₂ + heat; filling chamber flooded with sterile HEPA air.
7. **PLC + F₀ integrator** — automatically computes lethality; if $F_0$ falls below threshold, a 3-way divert valve sends the milk to a reject tank.
8. **CIP module** — automated Cleaning-In-Place, typically caustic (1–2 %, 80 °C) and acid (0.5–1 % nitric, 70 °C).

**Operation.** A typical 5 000 L/h line uses 1.5–2.5 kg of steam per kg of milk, 0.8–1.0 m³ of cooling water, and 0.1–0.2 kWh of electrical energy. The plant can run 8–10 h between CIPs.

**Role of Regeneration Section.** The single most important energy-saving feature. Cold raw milk (4–6 °C) is pre-heated to ~80 °C by the hot sterilised milk (137 °C → 80 °C). Because 90–95 % of the heat is "recycled", steam consumption drops from ~0.7 kg/L (no regeneration) to ~0.07 kg/L (with regeneration). This is why modern UHT economics favour **plate** systems over **tubular** for white milk.

*(Marks distribution: Principle 1; Construction 3; Operation 1; Role of regeneration 2.)*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**Given:** $\dot V = 10\,000$ L/h $\Rightarrow \dot m_p = 10\,000$ kg/h (density of milk ≈ 1.03 kg/L is *sometimes* considered, but here we use 1 kg/L for simplicity unless stated); $T_i = 6\,^{\circ}\text{C}$; $T_o = 138\,^{\circ}\text{C}$; $\lambda = 2\,137$ kJ/kg; $C_p = 3.93$ kJ/kg·°C; $t = 3$ s; $z = 10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Part (i) — Mass of steam per hour.**

Heat absorbed by milk:

$$Q \;=\; \dot m_p \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 10\,000 \times 3.93 \times (138 - 6) \;=\; 10\,000 \times 3.93 \times 132$$

Computing:

$$Q \;=\; 10\,000 \times 518.76 \;=\; 5\,187\,600\ \text{kJ/h}$$

Mass of steam:

$$\dot m_s \;=\; \frac{Q}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{5\,187\,600}{2\,137} \;\approx\; 2\,427.4\ \text{kg/h}$$

So approximately **2 427 kg of steam per hour**.

**Part (ii) — F₀ calculation.**

Convert hold time to minutes:

$$t \;=\; 3\ \text{s} \;=\; \frac{3}{60}\ \text{min} \;=\; 0.05\ \text{min}$$

Compute lethancy at 138 °C:

$$L \;=\; 10^{(T - 121.1)/z} \;=\; 10^{(138 - 121.1)/10} \;=\; 10^{1.69}$$

Now:

$$10^{1.69} \;=\; 10^{0.69} \times 10 \;\approx\; 4.90 \times 10 \;\approx\; 49.0$$

Therefore:

$$F_0 \;=\; t \times L \;=\; 0.05 \times 49.0 \;\approx\; 2.45\ \text{min}$$

**Part (iii) — Adequacy check.**

Industry target: $F_0 \geq 3$ min. The calculated $F_0 = 2.45$ min is **below target**. Therefore, this process is **not adequate** for the most conservative definition of commercial sterility. Recommended actions:
- Raise $T$ to 140 °C: new $F_0 = 0.05 \times 10^{(140-121.1)/10} = 0.05 \times 77.6 \approx 3.88$ min ✔
- Or extend hold to 4 s: $F_0 = 0.0667 \times 49.0 \approx 3.27$ min ✔

*(Marks distribution: Part (i) 2; Part (ii) 3; Part (iii) 2.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **principle of direct steam-injection UHT processing**. With a neat labelled flow diagram, describe its construction and operation. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with the indirect plate system. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail the **microbiological, chemical, and nutritional changes** that occur in milk during UHT processing. Indicate how these affect shelf life and consumer acceptability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Direct Steam Injection UHT

**Principle.** Steam is injected *directly* into a stream of pre-heated milk via a small orifice. Because the heating medium and the product are in *direct* contact, heat transfer is essentially instantaneous and is governed by steam condensation. The temperature rise is determined by the mass of steam injected per unit mass of milk. After a short hold (2–4 s) in an insulated tube, the milk is led into a *vacuum flash chamber* (typically 0.3 bar absolute) where the excess water "flashes off", simultaneously cooling the milk back to ~80 °C.

**Construction (Major Components).**

1. **Balance tank** with de-aerator.
2. **Plate preheater** — milk warmed to 75–85 °C, ideally using *regenerative* heat from the outgoing sterilised milk.
3. **High-pressure pump** — raises milk pressure to 7–9 bar so it can flow through the narrow injection nozzle without backflow of steam.
4. **Steam injector nozzle** — culinary-grade steam (must be *boiler-grade* — clean, food-contact, dry-saturated) injected through a 0.5–2 mm orifice.
5. **Holding tube** — insulated, 2–4 s residence.
6. **Flash vacuum vessel** — operates at 0.3 bar; milk cooled from 137 °C to 80 °C; vapour is condensed in a barometric condenser.
7. **Plate regeneration section** + final chiller (4 °C exit).
8. **Aseptic surge tank** + **aseptic filler**.
9. **F₀ integrator and divert valve** (mandatory).
10. **Steam mass-flow controller** (Coriolis or differential-pressure type) for precise injection.

**Operation.** Milk at 80 °C is pumped to 8 bar, steam at 4 bar (143 °C, 1 kg = 2 137 kJ) is injected; the temperature rises to 137–140 °C. After 3 s, the milk flashes in the vacuum chamber, restoring its original TS. Steam consumption is ~7–10 % of milk mass, but most of this water is removed in the flash cooler.

**Comparison with Indirect Plate UHT.**

| Aspect | Direct (SI) | Indirect (PHE) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer | Instantaneous | Wall-mediated (slower) |
| Holding time | 2–4 s | 4–8 s |
| Flavour | **Fresh, very light cooked** | **Noticeable cooked** |
| Fouling | **Almost none** | Significant |
| Steam quality | **Culinary only** | Process steam OK |
| Capital cost | High (40–60 % more) | Lower |
| Aseptic surge tank | **Mandatory** | Optional |

**Advantages of Direct SI:** superior flavour, very short hot residence, less fouling, lower Maillard browning.
**Disadvantages of Direct SI:** higher capital cost, requires culinary steam, requires flash cooler + condenser, more energy per kg, requires tighter process control.

*(Marks distribution: Principle 1; Construction & diagram 2; Operation 1; Comparison 3.)*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Microbiological, Chemical & Nutritional Changes

**1. Microbiological Changes.**

- **Pathogens destroyed:** *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (D₀.₅₅ = ~3 min, easily inactivated), *Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Staphylococcus aureus* toxins (staphylococcal enterotoxins are heat-labile above 100 °C).
- **Spore destruction:** *Bacillus* spp. (most are inactivated at 135 °C / 1 s), *Clostridium* spp. (including *C. botulinum* with appropriate F₀). The 12-D concept for *C. botulinum* type A sets the *minimum* design target.
- **Survivors (the "thermoduric" minority):** *Bacillus sporothermodurans* (the most common UHT survivor, first reported in 1996 in Germany). It does not grow below 6 °C and does not cause food poisoning, so it does not violate commercial sterility.
- **Enzymes:** Most native enzymes (e.g., plasmin) are partially inactivated; lipase is partially destroyed. Alkaline phosphatase is fully inactivated (used as a *legal* indicator of pasteurisation but not of UHT, as it is destroyed at much lower temperatures).

**2. Chemical Changes.**

- **Maillard reaction (C-M-Na browning):** Lysine + lactose → Amadori products → melanoidins. Indicator: $Q$ value (using $z_Q \approx 26\,^{\circ}\text{C}$). For good quality UHT milk, $Q$ should be < 1.5.
- **Casein / whey protein denaturation:** $\beta$-lactoglobulin denatures ≥ 90 %, exposing thiol groups; some sulfhydryl aggregation occurs, contributing to cooked flavour.
- **Lactulose formation:** A specific *marker* of UHT/sterilisation; < 50 mg/L in pasteurised milk, 100–500 mg/L in UHT, > 500 mg/L in in-bottle sterilised.
- **Lactose isomerisation / epimerisation:** minor.
- **Fat changes:** Some free-fatty-acid liberation by residual lipase; some Maillard-induced oxidative changes in long-storage UHT (6+ months).

**3. Nutritional Changes.**

- **Vitamins:** Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is lost 30–50 % in UHT (versus 10–20 % in pasteurisation). B-vitamins (B1, B2, B6, folate) are largely retained (losses 5–15 %). Vitamin A and D are not affected. Vitamin B12 is reasonably stable.
- **Lysine loss:** Lysine is the most heat-sensitive essential amino acid; losses are typically 5–10 % in UHT versus 15–25 % in in-bottle sterilisation.
- **Minerals (Ca, P, Mg):** Unchanged; calcium-phosphate equilibria may shift slightly with pH, but nutritionally insignificant.
- **Bioactive proteins:** Lactoferrin and immunoglobulins are largely destroyed, but UHT milk is not marketed for these properties anyway.

**4. Effect on Shelf Life and Consumer Acceptance.**

- UHT milk, when aseptically packaged, has an ambient shelf life of 6–9 months (unopened) due to the absence of spoilage microbes and minimal residual enzymes.
- Opened UHT milk must still be refrigerated and used within 3–5 days (psychrotrophs, yeasts, moulds re-enter).
- Consumer acceptability is dominated by the *cooked flavour* (methyl ketone, aldehydes, H₂S) which *decreases* with storage as volatiles dissipate. Most Indian consumers report acceptance after 7–14 days of storage.
- Gelling (age gelation) can occur in UHT milk stored > 3 months at 25 °C due to proteolysis of residual $\beta$-casein by plasmin → sedimentation. This is a major *quality* (not safety) concern.

*(Marks distribution: Microbiological 2; Chemical 2; Nutritional 2; Shelf-life/consumer 1.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute Recall Block** — read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.

- ✅ **UHT = 135 °C / 1 s minimum** (Codex + FSSAI). *Not* 140 °C / 15 s.
- ✅ **F₀ ≥ 3 min** is the industry target; ≤ 2.5 min is the absolute minimum.
- ✅ **z = 10 °C** for *C. botulinum*; **z_Q = 26 °C** for Maillard browning (C-M-Na).
- ✅ **D₁₂₁.₁(C. botulinum) = 0.21 s** (commit to memory).
- ✅ **Direct UHT →** SI / SF → flash cooler **mandatory** → no fouling → expensive.
- ✅ **Indirect UHT →** PHE / THE / SSHE → regeneration section saves ~90 % energy → fouling on hot plates.
- ✅ **Aseptic packaging is non-negotiable** — UHT + non-aseptic = waste.
- ✅ **Lactulose** = marker of UHT; < 50 mg/L raw, 100–500 mg/L UHT.
- ✅ **B. sporothermodurans** = the classic UHT survivor; harmless.
- ✅ **3-way divert valve + F₀ integrator** = mandatory control hardware.
- ✅ **Holding tube design** is **laminar / Re ≤ 30 000**, length-to-diameter ≥ 100:1, with a 20–50 % safety factor on hold time.
- ✅ **Two key quality markers:** cooked flavour (↓ with storage) and age gelation (↑ with storage).
- ✅ **HTST = 72 °C / 15 s; LTLT = 63 °C / 30 min** — do not confuse with UHT.
- ✅ **Steam quality:** Direct UHT demands **culinary (boiler-grade) steam**; indirect UHT tolerates *process* steam because there is no product-steam contact.
- ✅ **C-M-Na (Maillard) browning** is the *bottleneck* for indirect UHT — it limits run time between CIPs.
- ✅ **F₀ formula** is **F₀ = t × 10^((T − 121.1)/z)** for constant-temperature hold; *time must be in minutes* for the numerical value to be in minutes.

---

> **End of Module 2 — UHT Processing Notes**  
> *Designed for PCFTT502 — KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homogenization: Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Homogenization: Theory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Homogenization** is a mechanical/physical processing operation in dairy technology wherein milk (or milk products) is forced under high pressure through a narrow orifice or restrictive valve, causing the larger native fat globules (average diameter **3.0–8.0 μm**) to be mechanically disrupted and reduced to a uniform, finer size (**typically < 2.0 μm**, average **0.2–2.0 μm**). The process simultaneously creates a stable, homogenous fat-in-water (O/W) emulsion in which the newly formed smaller fat globules are recoated by a reformed surface layer composed of casein micelles and whey proteins, preventing recoalescence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Homogenization is **NOT** a separation technique — it is a **size-reduction and stabilization** technique. It does not change the total fat content of milk, only the physical state and dispersion of fat. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Oil & Vinegar Salad Dressing" Model** > > Imagine shaking a bottle of salad dressing. The oil breaks into tiny droplets that temporarily disperse in the vinegar. However, if you stop shaking, the droplets quickly merge back (coalesce) and separate. The salad dressing is **unstable**. > > Now, imagine passing this mixture through an extremely narrow nozzle under extreme pressure (like squeezing it through a tiny hole with great force). The droplets are torn apart into **extremely fine, uniform particles**. Furthermore, native milk proteins (analogous to a natural "emulsifier soap") instantly coat each new tiny droplet, preventing them from re-joining. The mixture is now **stable and homogenous** — this is precisely what a dairy homogenizer does to milk fat globules. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | **Homogenization Pressure** | **100 – 250 bar** (single-stage); **Up to 300 bar** (two-stage) | Driving force for globule disruption | | **Operating Temperature** | **55 – 75 °C** | Critical for low-viscosity flow and membrane reformation | | **Native Fat Globule Size** | **0.2 – 15.0 μm** (mean ~3.4 μm) | Pre-homogenization state | | **Post-Homogenization Size** | **< 2.0 μm** (mean ~0.4–1.0 μm) | Target state | | **Surface Tension of Milk Fat (σ)** | **~ 1.0 – 4.5 mN/m** | Resistance to disruption | | **Homogenization Efficiency (HE)** | **> 95%** desired | Quality indicator | | **Laplace (Capillary) Pressure** | Depends on σ and radius | Theoretical disruption threshold | > [!NOTE] > The single most important physical principle underlying homogenization is the **Laplace pressure** — the internal pressure difference across a curved fat–water interface. Smaller globules require exponentially more pressure to disrupt further, which is why homogenization cannot reduce size indefinitely. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Theoretical Basis of Homogenization The theoretical framework of homogenization rests on three intertwined pillars: (a) the **Laplace pressure principle**, (b) the **turbulence and cavitation mechanism**, and (c) the **membrane reformation kinetics**. Each must be understood in sequence. #### 2.1.1 The Laplace Pressure Principle Every fat globule in milk exists as a discrete spherical droplet of liquid fat (lipid core) surrounded by the milk fat globule membrane (**MFGM**). This membrane creates a curved interface with the surrounding aqueous serum phase. The internal pressure of a globule is always higher than the external pressure by an amount called the **Laplace pressure (ΔP_L)**, defined as: > The Laplace pressure is the pressure difference that must be overcome to deform, elongate, and ultimately rupture a fat globule during homogenization. **Why it matters:** The smaller the globule radius, the higher the internal pressure. This is why homogenization, which reduces radius drastically, must apply enormous disruptive external pressure. #### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Disruption — Turbulence, Cavitation & Shear When milk is forced through the homogenizer valve (gap typically **0.1 – 0.3 mm**) at high velocity (**100 – 300 m/s**), three disruptive forces act on the fat globules: 1. **Shear Stress (η · dv/dy):** The extreme velocity gradient across the narrow valve gap stretches and elongates the globule, increasing its surface area. 2. **Turbulent Inertial Forces (ρ · v²):** Localized eddies in the turbulent flow field exert impact forces that shatter elongated globules. 3. **Cavitation:** The static pressure in the valve drops below the vapor pressure of milk, forming transient vapor bubbles. When these bubbles collapse downstream of the valve (in the higher-pressure region), they generate micro-jets and shockwaves that physically rupture adjacent fat globules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In modern high-pressure homogenizers, **cavitation is responsible for ~ 70–80%** of the disruptive work, while shear and turbulence contribute the remaining 20–30%. This is why homogenization efficiency is highly sensitive to valve design geometry. #### 2.1.3 Membrane Reformation — The "Casein Migration" Phenomenon Native fat globules are stabilized by the **MFGM**, which is rich in phospholipids, glycolipids, and membrane-specific proteins (e.g., butyrophilin, xanthine oxidase). However, when a globule is ruptured during homogenization, this native membrane is insufficient to fully cover the **vastly expanded new surface area** (surface area increases as the inverse of radius, A ∝ 1/r). To compensate, the body of milk provides an alternative coating material: - **Casein micelles** (which are surface-active due to their amphiphilic κ-casein "hairs") rapidly adsorb onto the new fat-water interface. - **Whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin)** also participate, especially when homogenization is performed at temperatures > 60 °C, where whey proteins are partially denatured. This reformed surface layer is called the **secondary membrane** or **recombined membrane**, and it is the principal reason homogenized milk does not exhibit creaming. #### 2.1.4 Factors Affecting Homogenization Efficiency | Factor | Effect on Homogenization | Optimum | |--------|--------------------------|---------| | **Pressure** | Higher pressure → smaller globules | 150–200 bar (single stage) | | **Temperature** | Higher T → lower viscosity, better disruption | 60–70 °C | | **Fat Content** | Higher fat → more globules, more work | 3.5–4.5% standard | | **Valve Design** | Affects shear vs. cavitation balance | Knife-edge, conical valves | | **Single vs. Two-Stage** | 2nd stage breaks clusters formed by 1st | Two-stage preferred for ≥3% fat | | **Flow Rate** | Affects residence time in valve | Match to capacity | ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---------------------|------------------|---------------| | 1 | $\Delta P_L = \dfrac{2\sigma}{r}$ | Laplace pressure across curved fat interface | Pa (N/m²) | | 2 | $\Delta P_L = \dfrac{4\sigma}{r}$ (for elongated globule) | Laplace pressure of a cylinder | Pa | | 3 | $A = 4\pi r^2$ | Surface area of a spherical globule | m² | | 4 | $A_{new} = A_{old} \cdot \dfrac{r_{old}}{r_{new}}$ | Surface area increase upon size reduction | m² | | 5 | $HE = \dfrac{N_0 - N_t}{N_0} \times 100$ | Homogenization Efficiency (% reduction in large globules) | % | | 6 | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{valve}}$ | Average velocity through homogenizer gap | m/s | | 7 | $P_{total} = P_1 + P_2$ (two-stage) | Cumulative homogenization pressure | bar | | 8 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Shear stress in valve gap | Pa | | 9 | $\sigma_{critical} = \Delta P_{external} \cdot \dfrac{r}{2}$ | Critical interfacial tension needed | mN/m | | 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in homogenizer valve | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for KTU Board Exams:** Always convert σ from mN/m to N/m (× 10⁻³) and r from μm to m (× 10⁻⁶) before substituting into the Laplace equation, or the calculated ΔP will be off by a factor of 10⁹. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Pressure Across a Spherical Fat Globule **Starting Principle:** Consider a single spherical fat globule of radius `r` surrounded by a thin interfacial membrane. The interfacial tension `σ` acts tangentially along the membrane surface, attempting to minimize surface area. The internal pressure of the globule (P_in) must exceed the external milk serum pressure (P_out) to maintain mechanical equilibrium against this contracting tension. **Step 1 — Force Balance on a Hemispherical Cap:** Consider the fat globule cut into two equal hemispheres. The internal pressure acts on the projected circular cross-section of area `πr²`, generating a separating force: $$F_{internal} = P_{in} \cdot \pi r^2$$ The interfacial tension acts along the circular boundary of circumference `2πr`, pulling the two hemispheres together: $$F_{surface} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r$$ **Step 2 — Apply Mechanical Equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the separating force equals the contracting force: $$P_{in} \cdot \pi r^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the Pressure Difference:** Dividing both sides by `πr`: $$P_{in} \cdot r = 2\sigma$$ Therefore: $$\Delta P_L = P_{in} - P_{out} = \frac{2\sigma}{r}$$ > **Result:** $\Delta P_L = \dfrac{2\sigma}{r}$ — the classical Laplace (Young–Laplace) equation for a spherical interface. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Surface Area Increase During Homogenization **Step 1 — Initial State:** A single native fat globule of radius $r_1$ has surface area: $$A_1 = 4\pi r_1^2$$ **Step 2 — Final State After Homogenization:** After disruption, the same fat mass (volume conserved, ignoring density difference) is distributed into `n` smaller globules, each of radius $r_2$, where $r_2 < r_1$. By conservation of fat volume: $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r_1^3 = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r_2^3$$ Solving for n: $$n = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^3$$ **Step 3 — Total New Surface Area:** $$A_2 = n \cdot 4\pi r_2^2 = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^3 \cdot 4\pi r_2^2 = 4\pi r_1^3 \cdot \frac{1}{r_2}$$ **Step 4 — Ratio of New to Old Surface Area:** $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{4\pi r_1^3 / r_2}{4\pi r_1^2} = \frac{r_1}{r_2}$$ > **Result:** $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$ — the surface area increases in **inverse proportion to the new radius**. **Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favorite):** > [!IMPORTANT] > If native globule radius $r_1 = 3.0 \,\mu m$ and post-homogenization $r_2 = 0.5 \,\mu m$: > $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{3.0}{0.5} = 6 \text{ times the original surface area}$$ > This **6× increase** must be fully recoated by casein/whey proteins to prevent coalescence. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Homogenization Efficiency (HE) **Definition:** Homogenization efficiency is the percentage reduction in the number of fat globules above a critical size threshold (commonly **2.0 μm**). **Step 1 — Count Globules Above Threshold Before Homogenization:** Let $N_0$ = number of fat globules per mL with diameter > 2 μm **before** homogenization. **Step 2 — Count Globules Above Threshold After Homogenization:** Let $N_t$ = number of fat globules per mL with diameter > 2 μm **after** homogenization. **Step 3 — Apply the Efficiency Formula:** $$HE = \frac{N_0 - N_t}{N_0} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A well-designed homogenizer at 200 bar / 65 °C achieves HE > 95%, meaning fewer than 5% of globules remain above the 2 μm threshold. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Homogenization States | Parameter | Native Milk | After Homogenization | |-----------|-------------|----------------------| | Mean globule diameter | 3.0–8.0 μm | 0.4–1.0 μm | | Total surface area | Reference (1×) | 6–10× higher | | Coating material | Native MFGM (phospholipids) | Casein micelles + whey proteins | | Creaming rate | Rapid (overnight) | Negligible (weeks) | | White opacity | Yellowish-white | Whitish (Tyndall effect) | | Digestibility | Lower | Higher (↑ lipase access) | | Susceptibility to lipolysis | Low (MFGM protected) | High (casein coat permeable) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Working Principle of a High-Pressure Homogenizer ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Raw Milk Inlet
55 to 70 C"] --> nodeB["High Pressure
Piston Pump
100 to 250 bar"] nodeB --> nodeC["Homogenizing Valve
Narrow Orifice
0.1 to 0.3 mm gap"] nodeC --> nodeD{"Globule Rupture
Zone"} nodeD --> nodeE["Cavitation
Bubble Collapse"] nodeD --> nodeF["Turbulent
Shear Field"] nodeD --> nodeG["Inertial
Impact Force"] nodeE --> nodeH["Reformed Fat Globules
Size less than 2 micrometer"] nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI["Secondary Membrane
Casein and Whey Protein
Coating"] nodeI --> nodeJ["Homogenized Milk
Outlet"] nodeC -.->|Optional second stage| nodeK["Second Valve
50 to 80 bar
Cluster Breaker"] nodeK --> nodeH style nodeA fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#8B7500 style nodeB fill:#FFD1DC,stroke:#8B0000 style nodeC fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#00308F style nodeD fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B6914 style nodeH fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#006400 style nodeI fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B style nodeJ fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Native vs. Homogenized Fat Globule ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["NATIVE FAT GLOBULE
Diameter 3 to 8 micrometer
Coated with MFGM"] -->|Homogenization
High Pressure| nodeB["ELONGATED DROPLET
Stretched in valve gap"] nodeB -->|Turbulence and Cavitation| nodeC["RUPTURED GLOBULE
Forms many small droplets"] nodeC -->|Casein adsorption| nodeD["RECOATED GLOBULE
Diameter less than 2 micrometer
Coated with Casein"] nodeD -->|Stable O/W Emulsion| nodeE["HOMOGENIZED MILK
No creaming for weeks"] style nodeA fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000 style nodeB fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B6914 style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B style nodeD fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style nodeE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Homogenizer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["POWER AND DRIVE UNIT"] powerA["Electric Motor
High Power"] powerB["Gear Reducer
Speed Reduction"] end subgraph PUMP["HIGH PRESSURE PUMP SECTION"] pumpA["Piston Plunger
3 to 5 Pistons"] pumpB["Suction Valve
Inlet Check"] pumpC["Discharge Valve
Outlet Check"] end subgraph VALVE["HOMOGENIZING VALVE ASSEMBLY"] valA["Valve Seat
Fixed Geometry"] valB["Valve Rod
Adjustable Gap"] valC["Impact Ring
Cavitation Zone"] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL AND INSTRUMENTATION"] ctrlA["Pressure Gauge
Manometer"] ctrlB["Temperature Sensor
RTD or Thermocouple"] ctrlC["Flow Meter
Capacity Monitor"] end powerA --> powerB powerB --> pumpA pumpA --> pumpB pumpB --> pumpC pumpC --> valA valA --> valB valB --> valC valC --> ctrlA valC --> ctrlB valC --> ctrlC style POWER fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00 style PUMP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B style VALVE fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B style CONTROL fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Single-Stage vs. Two-Stage Homogenization ``` SINGLE-STAGE HOMOGENIZATION ============================= Milk In ───► [ PUMP ] ──► [ VALVE STAGE 1 ] ──► Homogenized Milk Out 200 bar 200 bar | (some re-clustered globules exit) TWO-STAGE HOMOGENIZATION (RECOMMENDED FOR HIGH-FAT MILK) ========================================================= Milk In ──► [ PUMP ] ──► [ VALVE 1 ] ──► [ VALVE 2 ] ──► Homogenized Out 250 bar 200 bar 50 bar | | Rupture zone Cluster-breaking stage ``` > [!NOTE] > The **first valve** performs the actual size reduction. The **second valve** (lower pressure, ~ 25–35% of first stage) is used to **break up clusters** of small globules that tend to re-aggregate immediately after the first stage, improving stability and reducing viscosity. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define homogenization. What is the role of the homogenizing valve in the process?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Homogenization is a mechanical process in which milk is forced under high pressure (**100–250 bar**) through a narrow homogenizing valve to break the native fat globules (mean diameter **3.0–8.0 μm**) into much smaller, uniform globules (mean diameter **< 2.0 μm**), thereby producing a stable emulsion that resists creaming. The **homogenizing valve** is the heart of the process. It consists of a fixed valve seat and an adjustable valve rod, creating a narrow gap (**0.1–0.3 mm**). The milk accelerates through this gap to a velocity of **100–300 m/s**, generating intense shear, turbulence, and **cavitation**, which together disrupt the fat globules. (1 Mark: Definition; 1 Mark: Pressure/globule size; 1 Mark: Valve mechanism) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the Laplace equation. Explain its significance in the homogenization of milk.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Laplace equation gives the pressure difference across a curved liquid-liquid interface: $$\Delta P_L = \frac{2\sigma}{r}$$ where $\sigma$ = interfacial tension (N/m) and $r$ = radius of the globule (m). **Significance in homogenization:** The Laplace equation shows that the **internal pressure of a fat globule is inversely proportional to its radius**. Smaller globules have a much higher internal pressure and are therefore thermodynamically unstable. To disrupt a globule of radius $r$, the external disruptive pressure supplied by the homogenizer must exceed this $\Delta P_L$. As homogenization proceeds and globule size decreases, progressively higher pressure is required for further disruption, which is why homogenization is performed at very high pressures (**150–250 bar**) and why it cannot reduce globule size indefinitely. (1 Mark: Equation; 1 Mark: Inverse relationship; 1 Mark: Application to homogenization) --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **theory of homogenization** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of **cavitation, shear, and turbulence** in fat globule disruption. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Laplace pressure equation** for a spherical fat globule and apply it to calculate the internal pressure of a fat globule of radius **2 μm** in milk, given that the interfacial tension of milk fat against milk serum is **3.5 mN/m**. State the engineering implication of your result. --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Theory of Homogenization [7 Marks]** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Homogenization is a high-pressure mechanical process developed by **Auguste Gaulin** in 1899, primarily to prevent the formation of the visible cream layer in bottled milk. The principle is to reduce fat globule size so that the upward buoyant velocity (governed by Stokes' law) becomes negligible. **Mechanism of Disruption (4 Marks):** When milk is pressurized by a multi-piston pump and forced through the homogenizing valve, the following three disruptive forces act simultaneously on the fat globules: 1. **Shear Stress:** The extreme velocity gradient (dv/dy) across the narrow valve gap (≈ 0.1 mm) produces a shear stress $\tau = \eta \cdot (dv/dy)$ that elongates and deforms the spherical globule into a thread-like shape, increasing surface area and ultimately rupturing it. 2. **Turbulent Inertial Forces:** At Reynolds numbers > 4000 in the valve, the flow is highly turbulent. Localized eddies carry kinetic energy proportional to $\rho v^2$ and shatter the elongated globules through inertial impact. 3. **Cavitation:** As milk accelerates through the valve, the local static pressure drops below the vapor pressure of milk, forming vapor cavities. These cavities collapse downstream in the higher-pressure zone, generating **micro-jets and shockwaves** that physically disrupt adjacent fat globules. Cavitation contributes **~70–80%** of the total disruptive work. **Membrane Reformation (1 Mark):** After rupture, the new globule surface must be immediately recoated. The native **MFGM** is insufficient, so **casein micelles** (and partially denatured whey proteins) adsorb onto the new interface, forming the **secondary membrane** that prevents re-coalescence. **Sketch (1 Mark):** A neat labelled diagram showing the valve assembly, flow path, and fat globule rupture mechanism (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid block diagram). --- **(b) Laplace Equation Derivation + Numerical Application [7 Marks]** **Derivation (5 Marks):** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation)* Force balance on a hemispherical section: $$P_{in} \cdot \pi r^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P_L = \frac{2\sigma}{r}}$$ **Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):** Given: - $r = 2.0 \,\mu m = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m$ - $\sigma = 3.5 \, mN/m = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m$ Substituting: $$\Delta P_L = \frac{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_L = \frac{7.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_L = 3500 \, Pa = 3.5 \, kPa$$ > **Engineering Implication:** > The internal pressure of a 2 μm fat globule is only ~ 3.5 kPa — this is a **very small** pressure. In contrast, a homogenizer supplies **15–25 MPa** (150–250 bar), which is approximately **4,000–7,000 times greater** than the Laplace pressure. This enormous excess pressure ensures complete and efficient disruption of fat globules down to the target size of < 2 μm. However, as globules become smaller, $\Delta P_L$ rises sharply (inversely with r), which is why practical homogenization plateaus at ~ 0.4–1.0 μm and cannot proceed to nano-scale without prohibitive energy input. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting homogenization efficiency**. Explain the difference between **single-stage and two-stage homogenization** with suitable diagrams. **(b) [7 Marks]** A milk sample has **2 × 10⁹ fat globules per mL** above 2 μm size before homogenization. After homogenization, this number reduces to **8 × 10⁷ per mL**. Calculate the **homogenization efficiency**. If the original mean globule diameter was **4.0 μm** and after homogenization it becomes **0.8 μm**, calculate the **factor by which total fat surface area has increased**. --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Factors Affecting Homogenization + Single vs. Two-Stage [7 Marks]** **Factors (4 Marks):** | Factor | Explanation | |--------|-------------| | **Pressure** | Higher pressure → smaller globules; optimum 150–200 bar single-stage | | **Temperature** | Higher T → lower milk viscosity → better disruption; optimum 60–70 °C | | **Fat Content** | Higher fat content requires more disruptive work; recommended 3–4.5% | | **Valve Design & Wear** | Sharp-edged valves generate more cavitation; worn valves reduce efficiency | | **Flow Rate / Throughput** | Must match pump capacity to valve design for stable pressure | | **Number of Stages** | Two-stage improves cluster-breaking for high-fat products | **Single-Stage vs. Two-Stage (3 Marks):** - **Single-stage:** Milk passes through **one** homogenizing valve at high pressure (150–250 bar). Simple, economical, but small globules tend to re-cluster immediately after rupture due to high collision frequency. Suitable for low-fat milk and many standard products. - **Two-stage:** Milk passes through a **first** valve at high pressure (e.g., 200 bar) for size reduction, then immediately through a **second** valve at lower pressure (50–80 bar) to break up clusters formed after stage 1. The second-stage valve is **not** for further size reduction but for cluster dispersion. **Two-stage is preferred for products with ≥ 3% fat**, flavored milk, cream, and recombined milk. **Diagram (2 Marks included in the 3):** Refer to the ASCII schematic in Section 4.4 — both stages must be labelled with their respective pressures. --- **(b) Numerical Calculations [7 Marks]** **Given:** - $N_0 = 2 \times 10^9$ globules/mL (before) - $N_t = 8 \times 10^7$ globules/mL (after) - $d_1 = 4.0 \,\mu m$ → $r_1 = 2.0 \,\mu m$ - $d_2 = 0.8 \,\mu m$ → $r_2 = 0.4 \,\mu m$ **Part 1 — Homogenization Efficiency (3 Marks):** $$HE = \frac{N_0 - N_t}{N_0} \times 100$$ $$HE = \frac{(2 \times 10^9) - (8 \times 10^7)}{2 \times 10^9} \times 100$$ $$HE = \frac{1.92 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^9} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{HE = 96\%}$$ > **Comment:** This is an **excellent** homogenization efficiency, well above the industrial standard of > 95%. **Part 2 — Surface Area Increase Factor (4 Marks):** Using the derived formula (Section 3.2): $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{2.0}{0.4} = 5$$ $$\boxed{A_2 = 5 \times A_1}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** The total fat surface area has increased **5-fold**. This means 5× more casein micelles must adsorb onto the new interface to maintain emulsion stability. If insufficient protein is available (e.g., in skim milk or whey-diluted systems), partial coalescence and creaming may recur. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors in Laplace Equation:** Students frequently plug in σ in mN/m and r in μm directly into ΔP = 2σ/r, getting answers off by a factor of 10⁹. **Always** convert σ to N/m (× 10⁻³) and r to m (× 10⁻⁶) before substituting. > > 2. **Confusing Single-Stage and Two-Stage Functions:** Many students incorrectly state that the **second stage performs further size reduction**. In reality, the second stage (lower pressure) is for **cluster-breaking** only. > > 3. **Forgetting the Membrane Reformation Step:** A complete answer on homogenization theory **must** include the formation of the secondary membrane (casein adsorption). Skipping this is a 1–2 mark deduction. > > 4. **Mistaking Homogenization for Pasteurization / Separation:** These are independent processes. Homogenization does **not** kill microorganisms (although it does mechanically damage some bacteria) and does **not** separate fat. > > 5. **Writing Homogenization Efficiency in Decimal:** Always express HE as a **percentage** (e.g., 96%, not 0.96). Examiners often deduct marks for unit inconsistency. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Homogenization Theory** - [x] **Definition:** High-pressure mechanical process to reduce fat globule size from **3–8 μm to < 2 μm** and stabilize the emulsion. - [x] **Inventor:** Auguste Gaulin, 1899. - [x] **Primary Goal:** Prevent creaming (gravitational separation of fat). - [x] **Key Equipment:** Multi-piston pump + adjustable homogenizing valve (gap 0.1–0.3 mm). - [x] **Operating Conditions:** Pressure **100–250 bar**, Temperature **55–75 °C**, Single or two-stage. - [x] **Laplace Equation:** $\Delta P_L = \dfrac{2\sigma}{r}$ — internal pressure ∝ 1/radius. - [x] **Surface Area Increase:** $A_2 / A_1 = r_1 / r_2$ — must be recoated by casein micelles. - [x] **Disruption Forces (in order of importance):** Cavitation (~75%) > Turbulence (~15%) > Shear (~10%). - [x] **Membrane Reformation:** Native MFGM is replaced by casein micelles + denatured whey proteins. - [x] **Homogenization Efficiency Formula:** $HE = \dfrac{N_0 - N_t}{N_0} \times 100 \, [\%]$; target > 95%. - [x] **Two-Stage Advantage:** Stage 1 (high P) → size reduction; Stage 2 (low P) → cluster breaking. - [x] **Side Effects:** Whiter color (Tyndall effect), increased susceptibility to lipolysis, slightly altered flavor, possible activation of natural lipases. - [x] **Industries Using:** Fluid milk, cream, ice cream mix, evaporated milk, recombined milk, flavored milk, infant formula. - [x] **NOT Used For:** Hard cheese (interferes with curd formation), butter (unnecessary), and some fermented products where fat destabilization is desired.

topics.title: Effects of Homogenization on Milk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects of Homogenization on Milk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Homogenization** is a mechanical/physical process in which the coarse emulsion of milk fat in the aqueous phase of milk is forced through a narrow orifice under high pressure (typically **100–250 bar**), resulting in the **disruption of large fat globules** into much smaller ones of sub-micron size. This dramatically alters the physical, chemical, biochemical, and sensory properties of milk.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, homogenization is classified as a **non-thermal unit operation** belonging to the family of mechanical emulsification techniques, governed primarily by turbulent flow, shear stress, and cavitation phenomena inside the homogenizing valve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Homogenization Parameters (KTU Reference):**
> - **Pressure:** 150–200 bar (single-stage); 175 + 35 bar (two-stage)
> - **Temperature:** 55–65 °C (warm-stage); 70–80 °C (hot-stage)
> - **Mean fat globule size (before):** ~3.0–3.5 µm
> - **Mean fat globule size (after):** ~0.2–1.0 µm
> - **Surface area increase:** 6× to 10× the original value

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose Through a Pinhole" Mental Model

Imagine you have a **large soap bubble (~3 mm)** floating in air. Now imagine forcing that big bubble through a tiny nozzle (smaller than the bubble itself). The bubble cannot pass intact — it **shatters into dozens of tiny droplets**. Each tiny droplet needs a *new outer "skin"* (membrane) to stay stable.

That is exactly what happens in a homogenizer:

- **Big fat globules** = the big soap bubble (native milk fat).
- **Homogenizing valve** = the tiny nozzle.
- **High pressure (150–200 bar)** = the squeezing force.
- **New fat surface** = new "skin" made of **casein micelles** and **whey proteins** that adsorb instantly.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "BLAST":** The 5 major effects of homogenization are:
> **B**igger surface area • **L**ighter (whiter) color • **A**nti-creaming stability • **S**ensory richness (mouthfeel) • **T**ighter protein-fat bonding (new membrane)

---

## 1.3 Why Homogenization Is Performed — Engineering Justification

Native milk, when left to stand, forms a thick **cream layer** at the top due to the buoyancy of large fat globules. This is governed by **Stokes' Law** (covered in Section 2). Homogenization permanently suppresses this separation by reducing globule size below the threshold where gravitational force can overcome Brownian motion and viscosity drag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> The Board Examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between the **four categories of effects** of homogenization:
> 1. **Physical effects** (size, surface area, viscosity, density, color)
> 2. **Chemical/Biochemical effects** (membrane composition, lipolysis, oxidation)
> 3. **Sensory effects** (taste, mouthfeel, texture)
> 4. **Technological effects** (heat stability, curd tension, foam stability)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Homogenization — Physics Breakdown

The disruption of fat globules inside the homogenizer valve occurs due to **three simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Shear stress (laminar & turbulent):** The liquid experiences intense velocity gradients as it accelerates through the valve gap (typically 100–300 µm wide).
2. **Cavitation:** Local pressure drops below the vapor pressure of milk water, forming vapor bubbles that violently collapse downstream — generating shock waves that shatter globules.
3. **Impact (turbulent flow):** Globules collide with the valve seat and each other at supersonic velocities.

> [!NOTE]
> The **newly created fat surface** is thermodynamically unstable. Within **milliseconds**, it is **re-coated** by:
> - **Casein micelles** (primary coating — preferentially adsorbed due to surface-active properties)
> - **Whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin > α-lactalbumin)
> - **Native MFGM fragments** (residual)
>
> This forms the **secondary or "replacement" membrane** that stabilizes the homogenized fat globule.

## 2.2 Comprehensive List of Effects of Homogenization on Milk

### A. Physical Effects

| Effect | Description | Quantitative Value |
|--------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Fat Globule Size Reduction** | Mean diameter falls from ~3.5 µm to <1 µm | 80–90 % reduction in diameter |
| **Surface Area Increase** | New total interfacial area | **6× to 10×** original |
| **Creaming Rate Reduction** | Stokes' Law predicts dramatic fall | Almost zero cream line |
| **Increased Whiteness** | Tyndall/Rayleigh scattering increases | Reflectance rises from ~50 % to ~70 % |
| **Viscosity** | Slight increase due to more particles | 5–15 % rise |
| **Density** | Marginal increase (more protein coating) | ~0.5–1 % rise |

### B. Chemical/Biochemical Effects

| Effect | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **New Membrane Formation** | Casein micelles (especially αₛ₁- and β-casein) replace native MFGM |
| **Lipolysis Susceptibility** | Initial increase — **danger of rancidity** if not pasteurized promptly |
| **Lipase Activation** | Native lipoprotein lipase acts on disrupted membranes |
| **Mineral Redistribution** | Calcium phosphate equilibrium shifts slightly |
| **Protein-Fat Interaction** | Stronger, more irreversible binding |
| **Oxidative Stability** | Depends — improves under proper cold storage; deteriorates if copper/iron present |

### C. Sensory Effects

| Effect | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Mouthfeel** | Smoother, richer, fuller body |
| **Taste** | Enhanced perception of fat-derived flavor |
| **Color** | Distinctively **whiter** (more visible light scattering) |
| **No Cream Plug** | Uniform appearance throughout |

### D. Technological / Processing Effects

| Effect | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Heat Stability** | Marginally improved (more casein available) |
| **Foam Stability** | Improved — important for cappuccino, mousses |
| **Curd Tension** | Softer, more fragile curd in cheese-making |
| **Coffee Feathering** | Markedly reduced |
| **Fermentation** | Faster acid development in yogurt/cheese |
| **Emulsion Stability** | Permanent (kinetically stable for months) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear regularly in **Part B (14-mark)** derivations and numerical problems in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{2r^2(\rho_f - \rho_p)g}{9\eta}$ | **Stokes' Law** — creaming velocity of fat globule |
| 2 | $A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} \times \left(\dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}\right)$ | Surface area scaling with diameter |
| 3 | $N_{\text{new}} = N_{\text{old}} \times \left(\dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}\right)^{3}$ | Number of globules scaling |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | **Poiseuille's Law** — pressure drop in homogenizer gap |
| 5 | $P_{\text{req}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}$ | Net homogenization pressure |
| 6 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | **Shear stress** acting on globule |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — turbulence check |
| 8 | $v_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{valve}}}$ | Mean velocity in valve gap |
| 9 | $t_{\text{residence}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{valve}}}{Q}$ | Time globule spends in valve (µs) |
| 10 | $E_{\text{kin}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy per unit volume |

**Where:**
- $r$ = fat globule radius (m)
- $\rho_f$ = density of fat (~915 kg/m³ at 20 °C)
- $\rho_p$ = density of skim milk phase (~1035 kg/m³)
- $\eta$ or $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $d$ = diameter, $A$ = area, $N$ = number of globules
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $L$ = length of valve gap (m)
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The principles governing homogenization effects are directly transferable to:

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — liposomal drug encapsulation, nano-emulsion injectables.
- **Cosmetics** — stable lotions, creams, and serums.
- **Petrochemicals** — fuel emulsion stability.
- **Food biotechnology** — probiotic encapsulation.

Mastering these equations in dairy context equips the engineer with a transferable **fluid mechanics + colloid chemistry** skillset.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Surface Area Increase Due to Homogenization

We start with the fundamental premise that **total fat volume is conserved** during homogenization — only the globule size changes, not the total fat content.

### Step 1 — Assume Original Globule Population

Let there be $N_{\text{old}}$ fat globules, each of diameter $d_{\text{old}}$, giving total fat volume:

$$V_{\text{fat}} = N_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_{\text{old}}^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Assume New Globule Population After Homogenization

Let the new globule diameter be $d_{\text{new}}$, with $N_{\text{new}}$ globules. By conservation of fat volume:

$$V_{\text{fat}} = N_{\text{new}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_{\text{new}}^{3}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Volumes

$$N_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_{\text{old}}^{3} = N_{\text{new}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_{\text{new}}^{3}$$

The $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ factor cancels:

$$N_{\text{old}} \cdot d_{\text{old}}^{3} = N_{\text{new}} \cdot d_{\text{new}}^{3}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $N_{\text{new}}$

$$N_{\text{new}} = N_{\text{old}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}\right)^{3}$$

This is the **number scaling equation** listed in the Formula Sheet as Equation 3.

### Step 5 — Compute Original Surface Area

$$A_{\text{old}} = N_{\text{old}} \cdot \pi d_{\text{old}}^{2}$$

### Step 6 — Compute New Surface Area

$$A_{\text{new}} = N_{\text{new}} \cdot \pi d_{\text{new}}^{2}$$

### Step 7 — Form the Ratio

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{new}} \cdot \pi d_{\text{new}}^{2}}{N_{\text{old}} \cdot \pi d_{\text{old}}^{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Substitute $N_{\text{new}}$

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{old}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}\right)^{3} \cdot d_{\text{new}}^{2}}{N_{\text{old}} \cdot d_{\text{old}}^{2}}$$

The $N_{\text{old}}$ cancels:

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{d_{\text{old}}^{3}}{d_{\text{new}}^{3}}\right) \cdot d_{\text{new}}^{2}}{d_{\text{old}}^{2}}$$

### Step 9 — Simplify

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{old}}^{3} \cdot d_{\text{new}}^{2}}{d_{\text{new}}^{3} \cdot d_{\text{old}}^{2}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}$$

### Step 10 — Final Compact Form

$$A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}$$

This is **Equation 2** of the Formula Sheet. It is one of the most-tested derivations in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** If $d_{\text{old}} = 3.5 \, \mu m$ and $d_{\text{new}} = 0.7 \, \mu m$, then:
> $$A_{\text{new}} / A_{\text{old}} = 3.5 / 0.7 = 5.0$$
> This means **surface area increases 5-fold**, which matches the typical literature value of 6×–10× (using slightly different ratios).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stokes' Law Application to Creaming Rate Reduction

### Step 1 — State Stokes' Law

The terminal velocity of a fat globule rising through milk serum is:

$$v = \dfrac{2 r^{2} (\rho_f - \rho_p) \, g}{9 \, \eta}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values for Native Milk

Take $r = 1.75 \, \mu m = 1.75 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, $\rho_f = 915 \, kg/m^{3}$, $\rho_p = 1035 \, kg/m^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$, $\eta = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$:

$$v_{\text{native}} = \dfrac{2 \times (1.75 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (915 - 1035) \times 9.81}{9 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify Numerator

$$\text{Num} = 2 \times 3.0625 \times 10^{-12} \times (-120) \times 9.81$$
$$\text{Num} = -7.21 \times 10^{-9} \, (SI \; units)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Denominator

$$\text{Den} = 9.018 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 5 — Compute $v_{\text{native}}$

$$v_{\text{native}} = \dfrac{-7.21 \times 10^{-9}}{9.018 \times 10^{-3}} = -7.99 \times 10^{-7} \, m/s$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Practical Units (mm/day)

$$v_{\text{native}} = -7.99 \times 10^{-7} \times 86400 \times 10^{3} \, mm/day$$
$$v_{\text{native}} \approx -69 \, mm/day$$

(Negative sign indicates upward motion → cream rises.)

### Step 7 — Apply Homogenization (r becomes 0.35 µm)

$$v_{\text{homog}} = v_{\text{native}} \times \left(\dfrac{r_{\text{new}}}{r_{\text{old}}}\right)^{2}$$

$$v_{\text{homog}} = -69 \times \left(\dfrac{0.35}{1.75}\right)^{2} = -69 \times 0.04 = -2.76 \, mm/day$$

### Step 8 — Interpret

Creaming rate **drops by 25-fold**, which is why homogenized milk shows **no visible cream line** even after weeks of storage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Native vs. Homogenized Milk

| Property | Native (Unhomogenized) Milk | Homogenized Milk | Magnitude of Change |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Mean fat globule diameter | 3.0–3.5 µm | 0.2–1.0 µm | ↓ 70–90 % |
| Specific surface area | ~2 m²/g fat | ~12–20 m²/g fat | ↑ 6–10× |
| Creaming velocity (Stokes) | ~70 mm/day | ~3 mm/day | ↓ 25× |
| Cream-line visibility | Visible in 2 h | None (permanent) | ↓ 100 % |
| Color (L* value) | L* ~ 88 | L* ~ 92 | ↑ 4 % |
| Viscosity | 1.8 mPa·s | 2.0 mPa·s | ↑ 10 % |
| Membrane composition | Native MFGM (polar lipids + glycoproteins) | Casein-dominated (replacement membrane) | **Qualitative change** |
| Lipolysis risk | Low (MFGM protective) | Higher initially | ↑ ↑ |
| Heat stability index | ~3–4 min | ~4–6 min | ↑ 30–50 % |
| Curd tension (in cheese) | Firm | Soft, fragile | ↓ 30–40 % |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Discuss the effects of homogenization on milk," never give a one-line answer. Always structure the response under these **four sub-headings**: Physical, Chemical, Sensory, Technological — and quote at least **one numerical value** in each section.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Native vs. Homogenized Fat Globule Membrane Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph native["NATIVE MILK FAT GLOBULE (3.5 um diameter)"]
        nf1[Triacylglycerol Core] --> nf2[Inner Polar Lipid Layer]
        nf2 --> nf3[Protein Coat - Butyrophilin]
        nf3 --> nf4[Xanthine Oxidoreductase]
        nf4 --> nf5[Outer Glycoprotein Layer]
        nf5 --> nf6[Continuous Native MFGM]
    end

    subgraph homog["HOMOGENIZED FAT GLOBULE (0.5 um diameter)"]
        hf1[Triacylglycerol Core] --> hf2[Disrupted Native Membrane Fragments]
        hf2 --> hf3[Adsorbed Casein Micelles]
        hf3 --> hf4[Submicelles - beta Casein]
        hf4 --> hf5[Whey Protein Layer]
        hf5 --> hf6[Replacement Membrane Casein Dominated]
    end

    native ==>|"High Pressure 150 to 200 bar"| homog
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Multi-Effect Cascade of Homogenization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Whole Milk] --> B[Preheating 55 to 65 C]
    B --> C[Homogenizing Valve]
    C --> D[First Stage 175 bar]
    D --> E[Second Stage 35 bar]
    E --> F[New Membrane Formation in milliseconds]
    F --> G1[Physical Effect Size reduction]
    F --> G2[Chemical Effect Membrane change]
    F --> G3[Sensory Effect Whiter color]
    F --> G4[Technological Effect Anti creaming]
    G1 --> H[Stable Homogenized Milk]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Block — Native vs. Homogenized (Structural)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["PHYSICAL EFFECTS"]
        P1a[Size down arrow] --> P1b[Surface area up arrow 6 to 10x]
        P1b --> P1c[Creaming velocity down arrow 25x]
        P1c --> P1d[Whiteness up arrow 4 percent]
    end

    subgraph P2["CHEMICAL EFFECTS"]
        P2a[MFGM disrupted] --> P2b[Casein adsorbs rapidly]
        P2b --> P2c[Lipase access increased]
        P2c --> P2d[Mineral balance shift]
    end

    subgraph P3["SENSORY EFFECTS"]
        P3a[Smoother mouthfeel] --> P3b[Richer flavor]
        P3b --> P3c[No cream plug]
    end

    subgraph P4["TECHNOLOGICAL EFFECTS"]
        P4a[Heat stability up] --> P4b[Foam stability up]
        P4b --> P4c[Feathering in coffee down]
        P4c --> P4d[Curd softer]
    end

    P1 --> OUT[OVERALL: Stable, White, Rich, Uniform Milk]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Detailed Process Map — Inside the Homogenizer Valve (KTU Sketch Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Inlet Milk at 55 to 65 C] --> M2[High Pressure Pump 200 bar]
    M2 --> M3[Inlet to Valve Gap 100 to 300 um]
    M3 --> M4[Acceleration to supersonic velocity]
    M4 --> M5[Shear + Cavitation + Impact Zone]
    M5 --> M6[Globule Shattering]
    M6 --> M7[New Membrane Formation in 0.001 sec]
    M7 --> M8[Outlet - Homogenized Milk]
    M5 -.->|"Recovery"| M9[Pressure Recovery Zone]
    M9 --> M8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List any six effects of homogenization on milk."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reduction in fat globule size** from ~3.5 µm to <1 µm.
2. **Increase in surface area** by 6 to 10 times.
3. **Elimination of cream-line formation** (anti-creaming stability).
4. **Whiter appearance** due to increased light scattering.
5. **Smoother, richer mouthfeel** due to uniform fat distribution.
6. **Replacement of native MFGM by casein-dominated membrane** within milliseconds.
7. (Bonus) Increased lipolysis susceptibility and improved foam stability.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Why is homogenized milk whiter than unhomogenized milk?"**

**Model Answer:**

Homogenization dramatically **reduces the diameter** of fat globules (from ~3.5 µm to <1 µm). When the size becomes comparable to or smaller than the wavelength of visible light (0.4–0.7 µm), the globules undergo intense **Tyndall/Rayleigh scattering** of all visible wavelengths equally. This scattering of *all* wavelengths in equal proportion makes the milk appear **white and opaque**, rather than translucent yellow (the natural fat color). The total reflectance rises from ~50 % (native) to ~70 % (homogenized).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the **physical effects of homogenization on milk**, with emphasis on fat globule size distribution, surface area increase, and color changes.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a suitable derivation, show how the **surface area of fat globules increases upon homogenization**. Given that native fat globule diameter is 3.5 µm and homogenized diameter is 0.7 µm, calculate the **fold increase** in surface area.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Detailed Discussion:**

Homogenization is a **mechanical emulsification** process in which whole milk is forced at **150–200 bar** through a narrow valve gap (100–300 µm). The physical effects include:

1. **Fat globule size reduction:** The mean diameter falls from **3.0–3.5 µm to 0.2–1.0 µm**. The size distribution shifts from broad (heterogeneous) to narrow and unimodal, peaking around 0.4–0.8 µm.

2. **Surface area increase:** Per unit mass of fat, the interfacial area increases by a factor of **6 to 10 times**. This is due to the inverse relationship between diameter and surface area for a constant total volume.

3. **Color change:** Native milk appears faintly yellow-cream due to carotenoids dissolved in fat. After homogenization, milk appears **distinctly whiter**. This is because:
   - The smaller globules scatter light of **all wavelengths** uniformly (Rayleigh-Tyndall regime).
   - More globules per unit volume → **higher scattering efficiency**.
   - Reflectance in the visible spectrum rises from ~50 % to ~70 %.

4. **Viscosity change:** Viscosity increases marginally (5–15 %) because:
   - More particles obstruct flow.
   - Hydration of newly adsorbed casein increases water binding.

5. **Creaming elimination:** Stokes' Law velocity becomes negligible because of $r^{2}$ dependence on diameter.

**Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical:**

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{old}}}{d_{\text{new}}}$$

Substituting $d_{\text{old}} = 3.5 \, \mu m$ and $d_{\text{new}} = 0.7 \, \mu m$:

$$\dfrac{A_{\text{new}}}{A_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{3.5}{0.7} = 5.0$$

**Therefore, surface area increases by 5-fold.** In practice, observed values are **6× to 10×** due to formation of even smaller sub-micron fragments in the cavitation zone.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **chemical and biochemical effects of homogenization** on milk fat globule membrane. Discuss the role of **lipoprotein lipase**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Using **Stokes' Law**, calculate and compare the **creaming rate** of a fat globule of radius 2 µm vs. 0.4 µm in milk serum at 20 °C. Assume $\rho_f = 915 \, kg/m^{3}$, $\rho_p = 1035 \, kg/m^{3}$, $\eta = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Chemical/Biochemical Effects:**

The **native milk fat globule membrane (MFGM)** is a complex biomembrane composed of phospholipids, sphingolipids, cholesterol, glycoproteins (butyrophilin, xanthine oxidoreductase), and enzymes. Homogenization **disrupts** this membrane, exposing the triacylglycerol core.

Within **microseconds**, the new fat surface is **re-coated** by:
- **Casein micelles** (primary adsorbents) — particularly $\beta$- and $\alpha_{s1}$-casein due to their amphiphilic, surface-active nature.
- **Whey proteins** ($\beta$-lactoglobulin, $\alpha$-lactalbumin).
- Residual MFGM fragments.

This forms the **replacement membrane**, dominated by casein (a 180° reversal in composition). The consequences are:

1. **Lipoprotein lipase (LPL)**, normally anchored to MFGM, becomes **activated and accessible** to the fat core → **lipolysis** and **free fatty acid release** → hydrolytic rancidity if milk is not pasteurized immediately.
2. **Mineral redistribution** — colloidal calcium phosphate equilibrium shifts slightly.
3. **Protein-fat interaction** becomes stronger and more irreversible than native MFGM binding.

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical solution:** To prevent homogenization-induced lipolysis, the industry always **pasteurizes BEFORE homogenization** (or immediately after, within 30 seconds), thereby inactivating lipoprotein lipase.

**Part (b) — Stokes' Law Numerical:**

For native globule ($r_1 = 2 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, m$):

$$v_1 = \dfrac{2 (2 \times 10^{-6})^{2} (915 - 1035) (9.81)}{9 (1.002 \times 10^{-3})}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times (-120) \times 9.81 = -9.42 \times 10^{-9}$

Denominator: $9.018 \times 10^{-3}$

$$v_1 = \dfrac{-9.42 \times 10^{-9}}{9.018 \times 10^{-3}} = -1.044 \times 10^{-6} \, m/s$$

Convert to mm/day: $v_1 = -1.044 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 \times 10^{3} = -90.2 \, mm/day$

For homogenized globule ($r_2 = 0.4 \, \mu m = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \, m$):

$$v_2 = v_1 \times \left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{2} = -90.2 \times \left(\dfrac{0.4}{2}\right)^{2} = -90.2 \times 0.04 = -3.61 \, mm/day$$

**Therefore, creaming velocity drops by a factor of 25** (from ~90 mm/day to ~3.6 mm/day), explaining why homogenized milk shows **no visible cream line** even after extended storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** — Stokes' velocity for a rising fat globule is *upward* (conventionally negative in y-axis convention). Losing this sign costs **½ mark**.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions** — Students frequently forget to convert from m/s to mm/day. Always show the conversion step: $1 \, m/s = 86400 \times 10^{3} \, mm/day$.
> 3. **Confusing diameter with radius** — Stokes' Law uses **radius**, not diameter. A radius vs. diameter mistake will cause the answer to be off by a factor of **4** (because of the $r^{2}$ dependence).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert µm to m** — Stokes' Law requires SI units. Substituting µm directly will give numerically absurd answers.
> 5. **Omitting the four-category structure** (Physical, Chemical, Sensory, Technological) in the 14-mark answer will lead to incomplete coverage and **3–4 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Exam Revision — Memorize These Bullets:**

- **Mean diameter:** Native ~3.5 µm → Homogenized ~0.5–1.0 µm.
- **Surface area:** Increases **6× to 10×** (formula: $A \propto 1/d$).
- **Number of globules:** Increases by factor of $(d_{\text{old}}/d_{\text{new}})^3$.
- **Stokes' velocity:** Decreases by factor of $(r_{\text{old}}/r_{\text{new}})^2$ — typically **25× reduction**.
- **Homogenization pressure:** 150–200 bar single-stage; **175 + 35 bar** two-stage.
- **Temperature:** 55–65 °C (warm) or 70–80 °C (hot).
- **New membrane:** **Casein-dominated** (β-casein + αₛ₁-casein), NOT native MFGM.
- **Lipase risk:** Use **pasteurization-before-homogenization** to inactivate lipoprotein lipase.
- **Color:** **Whiter** due to Rayleigh-Tyndall scattering at sub-wavelength sizes.
- **Mouthfeel:** **Smoother, richer** because uniform sub-micron fat distribution.
- **Cream line:** **Eliminated** permanently.
- **Heat stability:** **Marginally improved** (more casein in colloidal phase).
- **Curd tension (cheese):** **Softer, more fragile** — affects Cheddar/cheese yield.
- **Foam stability:** **Improved** (cappuccino, milkshake applications).
- **Feathering in coffee:** **Reduced** significantly.
- **Acid development in yogurt:** **Faster** (more casein available for lactic acid bacteria).
- **Cavitation + Shear + Impact** = the three physical mechanisms of globule disruption.
- **Equipment types:** APV Gaulin, Rannie, Tetra Alex — all use high-pressure positive-displacement pumps.
- **Two-stage advantage:** First stage disrupts; second stage breaks up clusters formed downstream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Board Exam Mantra:** Always conclude a 14-mark answer on effects of homogenization with a **closing engineering statement**, e.g.:
> *"In summary, homogenization transforms milk from an unstable coarse emulsion into a kinetically stable colloidal system with superior sensory and processing characteristics, although it introduces new biochemical risks that must be managed through proper sequence integration of pasteurization and homogenization unit operations."*
