> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Homogenizers, Stages of Homogenization, and Their Importance; Centrifugation: Clarification, Clarifiers and Separators, Separation Efficiency, and Bactofugation; New Technologies in Dairy Industry: Membrane Separation and Ultrafiltration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 3.1 Homogenization in Dairy Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Homogenization is a mechanical process that reduces the average diameter of fat globules in milk to less than 2 micrometers, creating a stable emulsion that prevents creaming and ensures uniform distribution of fat.

### Formal Definition

**Homogenization** is a high-pressure mechanical treatment of milk (or milk products) in which the coarse fat globules (average diameter 3–8 µm in raw milk) are disintegrated into extremely fine droplets (average diameter < 1 µm) through forced flow under high pressure (typically **100–250 bar**) through a narrow orifice or valve. The newly formed droplets are simultaneously coated with a protective film of milk proteins (casein and whey proteins), preventing re-coalescence.

The process is governed by the **Laplace pressure equation** for droplet disruption:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across the homogenizing valve (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension of fat globule membrane (≈ 1.5 mN/m for milk)
- $r$ = radius of the fat globule (m)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Salad Dressing Test"

Imagine you have a bottle of salad dressing (oil + vinegar) sitting on a shelf. Without shaking, the oil separates and floats to the top — this is exactly what happens in unhomogenized milk (the cream rises). Now, if you shake the bottle vigorously, the oil breaks into tiny droplets that stay temporarily suspended. **Homogenization is essentially an industrial, super-precise version of that shaking** — it forces milk through a microscopic gap at extreme pressure, breaking every single fat globule into ~50–100 smaller droplets. Then milk proteins rush in and "wrap" each new droplet like a tiny protective coat (similar to how egg yolk stabilizes mayonnaise), preventing them from rejoining.

### Why Homogenization Matters in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Exam Definition to Memorize:**
> *Homogenization is a mechanical process of size reduction of fat globules in milk to < 2 µm using high pressure (100–250 bar), thereby preventing creaming, improving whiteness, texture, digestibility, and stability of milk and milk products.*

### Objectives of Homogenization (CO-aligned)

| Objective | Engineering Mechanism | Practical Outcome |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Prevent creaming | Fat droplet size ↓ | Uniform fat distribution, no cream layer |
| Improve whiteness | Increased light scattering by tiny droplets | Whiter, more appealing milk |
| Enhance digestibility | Increased surface area for lipase action | Better fat absorption |
| Improve texture in products | Better fat-protein interaction | Smoother ice cream, stable cream |
| Prevent fat separation in tea/coffee | Smaller droplets remain dispersed | No oil rings on hot beverages |
| Increase viscosity & richness | Higher number of fat globules | Fuller mouthfeel |

---

## 3.2 Types of Homogenizers

Homogenizers are broadly classified based on the **mechanism of pressure generation and droplet disruption**.

### 3.2.1 High-Pressure Homogenizers (HPH) — Most Common Industrial Type

**Working Principle:** Milk is forced under high pressure (100–250 bar) through a narrow annular gap between a homogenizing valve and a valve seat.

#### (a) Single-Stage Homogenizer

- Uses **one homogenizing valve** only.
- Pressure: typically **140–200 bar** at the first stage.
- Suitable for fluid milk where moderate fat globule reduction is acceptable.

#### (b) Two-Stage (Double-Stage) Homogenizer

- Uses **two valves in series**.
- **First stage:** High pressure (**150–250 bar**) — breaks fat globules.
- **Second stage:** Lower pressure (**30–50 bar**) — breaks up clusters (agglutinates) of newly formed globules.
- Produces a more uniform size distribution and is preferred for **cream, condensed milk, and infant formula**.

### 3.2.2 Other Commercial Types

| Homogenizer Type | Mechanism | Application | Typical Pressure |
|------------------|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **APV Gaulin / Manton-Gaulin** | Piston-type positive displacement pump + valve | Industrial fluid milk | 100–250 bar |
| **Rannie / Tetra Alex** | Plunger pump + homogenizing valve | Dairy, beverages | 100–200 bar |
| **Microfluidizer** | Liquid jet + microchannels | Premium milk, UHT | up to 1500 bar |
| **Ultrasonic Homogenizer** | High-frequency sound waves (20 kHz) | Lab/specialty products | Low pressure, cavitation-based |
| **Valve-less Homogenizer** | Fixed geometry nozzle | Pilot-scale research | 50–100 bar |

---

## 3.3 Centrifugation — Clarification, Separation, and Bactofugation

### 3.3.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugation** in dairy processing uses high centrifugal force (**3000–10000 × g**) to separate milk components based on density differences. The denser phases (skim milk, bacteria, sediment) move outward, while lighter phases (cream, fat) move toward the axis of rotation.

### 3.3.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Salad Spinner Physics"

Think of a washing machine's spin cycle. Wet clothes contain water (heavy) clinging to fabric (lighter). During high-speed spinning, water is flung outward through the drum holes due to centrifugal force, leaving clothes "separated" from water. A **cream separator works identically** — milk is fed into a rapidly spinning bowl; the heavy skim milk is pushed to the outer wall, while the lighter cream collects near the center and exits through a separate outlet.

### 3.3.3 Clarification vs Separation vs Bactofugation

| Process | Purpose | Force (×g) | Output | Removal Target |
|---------|---------|-----------|--------|----------------|
| **Clarification** | Remove solid impurities | 3000–6000 | Clean milk | Sediment, dust, somatic cells |
| **Separation** | Separate cream from skim milk | 5000–8000 | Cream + Skim milk | Fat globules |
| **Bactofugation** | Remove bacteria & spores | 9000–10000 | Bacteria concentrate | Bacteria, spores |

---

## 3.4 Membrane Separation & Ultrafiltration (New Technologies)

### 3.4.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Membrane separation** is a physical separation process in which milk is passed through a semi-permeable membrane under pressure, allowing selective passage of components based on their molecular size, shape, and charge.

### 3.4.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Filter on Steroids"

A common tea strainer holds back tea leaves but lets water and flavor through. Now imagine a strainer so precise it can separate molecules by **size** — letting water, lactose, and minerals pass but blocking proteins. That's exactly what **ultrafiltration (UF) membranes** do in dairy. They are typically made of **polyethersulfone (PES)** or **regenerated cellulose** with pore sizes of **0.001–0.1 µm**, equivalent to a **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of 1,000–1,000,000 Daltons**.

### 3.4.3 Types of Membrane Processes

| Process | Pore Size | MWCO | Retains | Permeates |
|---------|-----------|------|---------|-----------|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1–10 µm | > 1,000,000 Da | Bacteria, fat, somatic cells | Proteins, lactose, minerals |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 0.001–0.1 µm | 1,000–1,000,000 Da | Proteins, large peptides | Lactose, water, minerals |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 0.0005–0.005 µm | 200–1,000 Da | Lactose, divalent salts | Monovalent salts, water |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.0005 µm | < 200 Da | All solutes | Pure water only |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 4.1 Stages of Homogenization — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### Stage 1: Pre-Homogenization (Pretreatment Considerations)

Before milk enters the homogenizer, several pretreatment steps are critical:

1. **Standardization of fat content** — Required to meet legal/market standards (e.g., 3.5% fat for toned milk).
2. **Heating to 55–65°C** — Reduces milk viscosity, lowers fat globule membrane rigidity, and prevents fat crystallization. Higher temperatures also improve homogenization efficiency.

$$\eta_{heated} \ll \eta_{cold}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Temperature Rule (Board Exam Favorite):** Homogenization efficiency improves at higher temperatures because viscosity decreases and the fat is in a fully liquid state. **Most commercial homogenization is done at 60–70°C.**

### Stage 2: Pressurization (Pump Stage)

A **triplex positive-displacement piston pump** (most common) or **plunger pump** generates the high pressure required.

**Theoretical flow rate of a piston pump:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N}{4}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $D$ = piston diameter (m)
- $L$ = stroke length (m)
- $n$ = number of pistons (typically 3)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)

**Pressure generated:**

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\frac{\pi D^2}{4}}$$

### Stage 3: Disruption (Homogenizing Valve Stage) — The Core Step

The pressurized milk passes through the **annular gap** (typically 0.1–0.3 mm wide) between the **homogenizing valve** and the **valve seat**.

**Three simultaneous mechanisms of fat globule disruption:**

1. **Shear stress** — Layers of milk flow at different velocities through the gap, creating shear that pulls globules apart.
2. **Cavitation / Turbulence** — Sudden pressure drop after the valve causes vapor bubbles to collapse violently (implode), generating micro-jets that shatter fat globules.
3. **Impact against valve seat** — The jet of milk strikes the valve seat wall at high velocity.

**Shear stress in the homogenizing gap:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\mu$ = milk viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient across the gap (s⁻¹)

### Stage 4: Re-Coalescence Prevention (Protein Coating)

When a fat globule is broken, a new fat surface is exposed. **Casein micelles** rapidly migrate and adsorb onto this fresh interface, forming a new stabilizing film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why casein?** Casein micelles are amphiphilic (have both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions). Within **microseconds**, they orient themselves so that hydrophobic regions face the fat and hydrophilic regions face the water — forming a robust protein "skin" that prevents re-coalescence.

**Surface coverage needed:**

$$\theta = \frac{A_{casein} \cdot N_{casein}}{A_{fat\ droplet}}$$

Where $\theta$ is the fractional surface coverage. For stability, $\theta \geq 0.7$ is desired.

### Stage 5: Post-Homogenization

- **Cooling** to < 4°C if for refrigerated storage.
- **Immediate pasteurization** to prevent lipolysis (free fatty acid formation) by native lipase.

---

## 4.2 Two-Stage Homogenization — Engineering Justification

After the **first stage** (200 bar), fat globules are broken but tend to **clump together (re-agglomerate)** due to high surface energy. The **second stage at 30–50 bar** breaks these clusters, improving:

- **Size uniformity** (narrower distribution).
- **Cream plug prevention** in stored milk.
- **Visual stability** (less fat separation on the surface).

---

## 4.3 Centrifugal Separation — Theoretical Foundation

### 4.3.1 Centrifugal Force and Sedimentation

For a particle in a rotating bowl:

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m \cdot \frac{v^2}{r}$$

Where:
- $F_c$ = centrifugal force (N)
- $m$ = mass of particle (kg)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $r$ = distance from axis of rotation (m)
- $v$ = tangential velocity (m/s)

### 4.3.2 Separation Efficiency (η)

The **separation efficiency** is the ratio of solids actually removed to solids entering the system:

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $C_{in}$ = concentration of impurity in feed (mg/kg)
- $C_{out}$ = concentration of impurity in clarified output (mg/kg)

### 4.3.3 Equivalent Clarification Area (Sigma Concept)

The performance of any separator is benchmarked against an ideal gravitational settler:

$$\Sigma = \frac{V_{feed}}{v_g}$$

Where $\Sigma$ (m²) is the **equivalent clarification area** and $v_g$ is the gravitational settling velocity.

For a disc-stack bowl:

$$\Sigma = \frac{2\pi \cdot n^3 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (r_2^3 - r_1^3)}{3 \cdot g \cdot \tan\theta}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of discs
- $r_1, r_2$ = inner and outer disc radii
- $\theta$ = disc half-angle

---

## 4.4 Bactofugation — Theoretical Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bactofugation** is high-speed centrifugation at **9000–10000 × g** specifically designed to remove **bacterial cells and spores** from milk, especially *Bacillus cereus* spores which are heat-resistant and survive pasteurization.

The Stokes-type settling velocity in a centrifugal field:

$$v_s = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

Where:
- $d$ = bacterial diameter (~1 µm)
- $\rho_p$ = density of particle (~1.1 g/cm³ for bacteria)
- $\rho_f$ = density of milk (~1.03 g/cm³)
- $\mu$ = viscosity of milk (~1.0 cP at 55°C)

**Removal efficiency:** Bactofugation removes **95–99% of vegetative bacteria and 90–95% of spores** in a single pass.

---

## 4.5 Membrane Separation — Theoretical Foundation

### 4.5.1 Flux Equation (Darcy's Law for Membranes)

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

Where:
- $J$ = permeate flux (L/m²·h or LMH)
- $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ = viscosity of permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = membrane hydraulic resistance (m⁻¹)

### 4.5.2 Concentration Polarization

As solutes accumulate at the membrane surface, they form a gel layer that increases resistance:

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_g$$

Where $R_g$ is the resistance of the **gel polarization layer**.

**Real-world operating fluxes in dairy UF:**

| Product | Membrane MWCO | TMP (bar) | Flux (LMH) |
|---------|---------------|-----------|------------|
| Whey protein concentration | 10 kDa | 1–4 | 30–80 |
| Milk protein concentration | 10 kDa | 2–5 | 20–60 |
| Cheese milk standardization | 10 kDa | 1–3 | 50–100 |

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Application | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Laplace pressure for fat globule disruption | Pa |
| 2 | $Q = \frac{\pi D^2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot N}{4}$ | Pump flow rate calculation | m³/s |
| 3 | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress in homogenizer gap | Pa |
| 4 | $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal force on particle | N |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Separation efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\Sigma = \frac{2\pi n \omega^2 (r_2^3 - r_1^3)}{3g\tan\theta}$ | Sigma value for separator | m² |
| 7 | $v_s = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)\omega^2 r}{18\mu}$ | Stokes settling in centrifuge | m/s |
| 8 | $J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ | Membrane permeate flux | m/s |
| 9 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_g$ | Total membrane resistance | m⁻¹ |
| 10 | $Q = \frac{V}{t}$ | Volumetric throughput | m³/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 5.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Required to Disrupt a Fat Globule

### Problem Setup
A fat globule of radius $r = 4 \mu m$ in milk has an interfacial tension $\gamma = 1.5 mN/m$. Calculate the minimum pressure differential required to disrupt this globule in a homogenizer.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (Young-Laplace equation):**

The pressure difference across a curved interface (like a fat globule membrane) is given by:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Convert all units to SI:**

$$\gamma = 1.5 \, mN/m = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m$$

$$r = 4 \, \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-6} \, m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator separately:**

Numerator: $2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $4 \times 10^{-6}$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.0}{4} \times 10^{-3-(-6)} = 0.75 \times 10^{3}$$

$$\Delta P = 750 \, Pa$$

### Engineering Interpretation

> The theoretical Laplace pressure to disrupt a 4 µm fat globule is only **750 Pa (≈ 0.0075 bar)**. However, industrial homogenizers operate at **100–250 bar** — i.e., **13,000 to 33,000 times higher** than the theoretical minimum.

**Why the huge discrepancy?** Because real disruption requires overcoming:
1. **Membrane rigidity** of the native milk fat globule membrane (MFGM).
2. **Viscosity forces** in turbulent flow.
3. **Cavitation effects** which are highly non-linear.

This explains why industrial homogenizers require massive energy input.

---

## 5.2 Derivation 2 — Centrifugal Force on a Bacterial Spore

### Problem Setup
A *Bacillus cereus* spore of mass $m = 1.5 \times 10^{-15} \, kg$ is located at radius $r = 0.15 \, m$ in a bactofuge rotating at $N = 6500 \, rpm$. Calculate the centrifugal force acting on the spore.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 6500}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 6500}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 6500}{60} = \frac{40840.8}{60}$$

$$\omega = 680.68 \, rad/s$$

**Step 3 — Apply centrifugal force equation:**

$$F_c = m \omega^2 r$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega^2$:**

$$\omega^2 = (680.68)^2 = 463,325 \, rad^2/s^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all values:**

$$F_c = (1.5 \times 10^{-15}) \times (463,325) \times (0.15)$$

**Step 6 — Multiply sequentially:**

$$1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 463,325 = 6.95 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$6.95 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.15 = 1.04 \times 10^{-10} \, N$$

### Result

$$\boxed{F_c \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-10} \, N}$$

### Engineering Interpretation

Although the absolute force on a single spore is microscopic, when multiplied across **billions of spores** in milk and amplified by the **huge relative density difference between spore and milk serum**, the cumulative separation efficiency becomes **> 95%**. This is why bactofugation is the **gold standard** for spore removal in **European-style cheese milk** where *B. cereus* causes late-blowing defects.

---

## 5.3 Derivation 3 — Membrane Flux Calculation

### Problem Setup
An ultrafiltration membrane with hydraulic resistance $R_m = 5 \times 10^{13} \, m^{-1}$ is used to concentrate whey proteins at 50°C. The transmembrane pressure (TMP) is $\Delta P = 3 \, bar$, and the viscosity of the permeate (water + lactose) is $\mu = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$. Calculate the permeate flux.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI:**

$$\Delta P = 3 \, bar = 3 \times 10^5 \, Pa$$

$$\mu = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law:**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$J = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{(0.6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{13})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$(0.6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{13}) = 3.0 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5 — Perform division:**

$$J = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{3.0 \times 10^{10}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \, m/s$$

**Step 6 — Convert to standard dairy LMH units (L/m²·h):**

$$J = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \, m^3 / (m^2 \cdot s)$$

$$J = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 \times 3600 \, L / (m^2 \cdot h)$$

$$J = 36 \, LMH$$

### Result

$$\boxed{J = 36 \, L/m^2 \cdot h}$$

This value lies **comfortably within the typical industrial UF flux range of 20–80 LMH**, validating the operating parameters.

---

## 5.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Technologies

| Parameter | Homogenization | Centrifugal Separation | Bactofugation | Ultrafiltration |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Driving force** | High pressure (100–250 bar) | Centrifugal force (5000–10000 × g) | Centrifugal force (9000–10000 × g) | TMP (1–5 bar) |
| **Target component** | Fat globules | Fat globules (separation) / impurities (clarification) | Bacteria & spores | Proteins (casein, whey) |
| **Mechanism** | Shear + cavitation | Density difference | Density difference | Size exclusion |
| **Temperature** | 55–70°C | 50–60°C | 50–60°C | 10–55°C |
| **Removal efficiency** | 95% fat globule < 2 µm | 99.5% fat separation | 95% bacteria, 90% spores | > 99% protein retention |
| **Energy intensity** | High (0.005–0.01 kWh/L) | Moderate (0.002–0.005 kWh/L) | High (0.01–0.02 kWh/L) | Moderate (0.003–0.008 kWh/L) |
| **Product impact** | Improved texture & stability | Standardization of fat | Microbial reduction | Protein concentration |
| **KTU Exam frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 6.1 Block Diagram — Two-Stage Homogenizer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    milkIn["Raw Standardized Milk"] --> heater["Plate Heat Exchanger 55-70 C"]
    heater --> pump["Triplex Piston Pump 200 bar"]
    pump --> valve1["First Stage Homogenizing Valve 150-250 bar"]
    valve1 --> disruptZone["Disruption Zone Cavitation Shear Impact"]
    disruptZone --> valve2["Second Stage Valve 30-50 bar Cluster Breakup"]
    valve2 --> cooler["Cooler Less than 4 C"]
    cooler --> pasteurizer["HTST Pasteurization 72 C 15 sec"]
    pasteurizer --> finalProd["Homogenized Pasteurized Milk"]
    
    style milkIn fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 7.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define homogenization. State any two objectives of homogenization of milk.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Homogenization** is a mechanical process of reducing the average diameter of fat globules in milk to less than 2 µm by forcing milk under high pressure (100–250 bar) through a narrow orifice or valve.

**Objectives (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Prevention of creaming** — Fat globule size reduction prevents the cream layer formation on standing.
2. **Improvement of whiteness and visual appeal** — Smaller globules scatter more light, giving milk a whiter appearance.
3. **Better digestibility** — Increased surface area facilitates lipase action.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is bactofugation? Why is it preferred over pasteurization for spore removal?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bactofugation** is a high-speed centrifugal separation process (9000–10000 × g) used to remove bacteria and bacterial spores from milk.

**Why preferred for spores:**
- *Bacillus cereus* and other spore-formers are **highly heat-resistant** and survive HTST pasteurization (72°C/15 s).
- Bactofugation physically removes **~90–95% of spores** based on their slightly higher density (~1.1 g/cm³) compared to milk serum.
- It does not affect flavor, color, or nutritional quality unlike ultra-high heat treatments.
- It is **non-thermal**, making it ideal for **artisanal cheese milk** where raw-milk flavor is desired but safety is critical.

---

## 7.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a neat diagram the working of a two-stage homogenizer. Discuss the importance of each stage.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Differentiate between clarification, separation, and bactofugation. State the operating speeds and applications of each.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question A (a)

**Two-Stage Homogenizer — Working Principle:**

A two-stage homogenizer consists of a **high-pressure triplex piston pump** coupled in series with **two homogenizing valves**. Standardized milk (at 55–70°C) is pressurized and forced through each valve in sequence.

**Stage 1 (High Pressure 150–250 bar):**
- Milk passes through the narrow annular gap between the **homogenizing valve** and **valve seat**.
- **Three simultaneous mechanisms** disrupt fat globules:
  - **Intense shear stress** due to high velocity gradient across the gap.
  - **Cavitation** — sudden pressure drop causes vapor bubble collapse, generating micro-jets.
  - **Impact** — the high-velocity jet strikes the valve seat.
- The **objective** is to reduce fat globule size from 3–8 µm to < 1 µm.

**Stage 2 (Low Pressure 30–50 bar):**
- Newly formed fat globules have a tendency to **clump together** (re-coalescence) due to high surface energy.
- Stage 2 **breaks these clusters** into a uniform dispersion.
- This prevents the formation of a "cream plug" on storage.

**Importance of Each Stage:**
- **Stage 1** is responsible for **globule disruption** — the core objective of homogenization.
- **Stage 2** ensures **dispersion stability** by preventing clustering and providing a **narrow size distribution**.

---

### Model Answer for Question A (b)

**Differences between Clarification, Separation, and Bactofugation:**

| Parameter | Clarification | Separation | Bactofugation |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Purpose** | Remove impurities, sediment, somatic cells | Separate cream from skim milk | Remove bacteria & spores |
| **Operating speed** | 3000–6000 rpm | 5000–8000 rpm | 9000–10000 rpm |
| **Centrifugal force** | 3000–6000 × g | 5000–8000 × g | 9000–10000 × g |
| **Output** | Clean milk | Cream + Skim milk | Bacteria concentrate + Reduced-bacteria milk |
| **Application** | Pre-treatment for pasteurization | Fat standardization | Cheese milk, ESL milk |
| **Removal efficiency** | Removes 90% sediment | 99.5% fat separation | 95% bacteria, 90% spores |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students frequently confuse **clarification** with **separation**. Remember:
> - **Clarification** = impurity removal (no two product streams).
> - **Separation** = fat standardization (two product streams: cream & skim milk).
> - **Bactofugation** = specifically targeted at microbes — higher speed, lower throughput.
> Mixing these in exam answers typically results in a **3-mark deduction**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the principles of membrane separation with special reference to ultrafiltration. Discuss the applications of UF in the dairy industry.** (7 Marks)

**(b) With neat sketches, explain the working of a disc-bowl centrifugal separator. Define separation efficiency and write its formula.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question B (a)

**Membrane Separation & Ultrafiltration — Principles:**

Membrane separation is a **physical, non-thermal** process in which milk is passed through a semi-permeable membrane under pressure. Components are separated based on **molecular size, shape, and charge** relative to the membrane pore size.

**Ultrafiltration (UF) — Working Principle:**
- UF membranes have a **pore size of 0.001–0.1 µm** and a **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of 1,000–1,000,000 Daltons**.
- Typical dairy UF membranes are made of **polyethersulfone (PES)** or **regenerated cellulose**, configured as **hollow fiber** or **spiral wound**.
- Milk is fed **tangentially (cross-flow)** across the membrane surface at **1–5 bar TMP**.
- **Proteins (casein, whey)** are retained as **retentate**; **water, lactose, minerals** pass through as **permeate**.

**Driving Equation — Darcy's Law:**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

**Applications in Dairy Industry:**

| Application | Product | Membrane Function |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| Cheese milk standardization | High-protein milk for cheese | Concentration of casein |
| Whey protein concentration | WPC-35, WPC-80 | Concentration of β-lactoglobulin |
| Greek yogurt | Thick protein-rich yogurt | Removal of water & lactose |
| Lactose-free milk base | Pre-treated for lactase hydrolysis | Removal of proteins before enzyme treatment |
| Milk protein isolate (MPI) | Functional protein ingredient | Selective protein concentration |
| Pre-Evaporation | Reduced volume for downstream | Volume reduction |

---

### Model Answer for Question B (b)

**Disc-Bowl Centrifugal Separator — Working:**

A disc-bowl separator consists of a **vertically mounted bowl** containing a stack of **conical discs** (typically 30–150) separated by thin spacers (~0.5–2 mm). The discs have angled holes (45°) that align to form vertical channels.

**Working Steps:**

1. **Milk enters** through a central inlet pipe at the top of the bowl.
2. **Distributor** directs milk into the disc stack channels.
3. **Bowl rotates at 5000–8000 rpm** generating centrifugal force.
4. **Heavier skim milk** is thrown outward to the bowl wall and rises to exit at the top.
5. **Lighter cream** moves toward the center (axis) and exits through a separate outlet.
6. **Centrifugal force (×g)** is given by $\omega^2 r / g$.

**Separation Efficiency Formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

Where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are the impurity concentrations in the feed and clarified output respectively.

---

## 7.3 TOPIC RECAP — IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU Board Exam 2024)**

- ☐ **Homogenization** reduces fat globule size to **< 2 µm** at **100–250 bar** pressure.
- ☐ **Laplace equation** $\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$ governs theoretical globule disruption pressure.
- ☐ **Two-stage homogenization**: Stage 1 = disruption (150–250 bar); Stage 2 = cluster breakup (30–50 bar).
- ☐ **Optimal homogenization temperature** = **55–70°C** (lower viscosity, liquid fat).
- ☐ **Casein micelles** adsorb onto newly formed fat surfaces and prevent re-coalescence.
- ☐ **Clarification** removes impurities (no fat separation) at **3000–6000 rpm**.
- ☐ **Separation** standardizes fat (cream + skim milk) at **5000–8000 rpm**.
- ☐ **Bactofugation** removes bacteria & spores at **9000–10000 rpm** (×g).
- ☐ **Bactofugation removes ~95% bacteria** and **~90% spores** — critical for cheese milk.
- ☐ **Separation efficiency** $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Stokes settling velocity** in centrifugal field: $v_s = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$.
- ☐ **Membrane processes** in increasing selectivity: **MF → UF → NF → RO**.
- ☐ **Ultrafiltration** retains **proteins** (MWCO 1–1000 kDa) and permeates **lactose, water, minerals**.
- ☐ **UF applications**: cheese milk standardization, WPC production, Greek yogurt, MPI.
- ☐ **Darcy's Law for membranes**: $J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ — the higher the TMP, the higher the flux.
- ☐ **Concentration polarization** reduces effective flux — countered by high cross-flow velocity.
- ☐ **Bactofugate** (bacteria-rich stream) constitutes ~3% of total milk — can be heat-treated and recombined.
- ☐ **Homogenized milk appears whiter** due to increased light scattering from smaller globules.
- ☐ **Two-stage homogenization is preferred for cream and condensed milk**, single-stage for fluid milk.
- ☐ **Microfluidizers** operate at very high pressure (up to 1500 bar) for premium UHT milk.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Pitfall:** In numerical problems, always **convert rpm to rad/s** by using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$. Many students lose 1–2 marks by leaving angular velocity in rpm units in centrifugal force calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reverse Osmosis Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reverse Osmosis Process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a semipermeable membrane selectively permits the passage of **water (solvent)** while rejecting dissolved solutes (salts, lactose, proteins, minerals) when the applied hydraulic pressure on the feed side **exceeds the natural osmotic pressure** of the solution. In the context of dairy processing (PCFTT502), RO is defined by KTU as a **membrane concentration technique** used for: - **Pre-concentration of whole milk, skim milk, and whey** prior to evaporation and spray drying. - **Water removal** from milk and dairy effluents. - **Volume reduction** for transport economics. - **Partial demineralization** when combined with ion exchange or nanofiltration stages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Box:** Reverse Osmosis is a *physical separation* technique where hydrostatic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the feed is applied across a semipermeable membrane to force the solvent (water) from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration — the **reverse** of the natural osmotic flow. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of RO like **squeezing a wet sponge wrapped in a special cloth**: - The **sponge** is your milk (full of water + dissolved nutrients). - The **special cloth** is the RO membrane — its pores are so tiny that they let only the **water** (the small molecules) pass through, but block the **bigger milk solids** (proteins, fats, lactose, minerals). - When you **squeeze harder** than the natural tendency of water to stay with the milk solids (i.e., you exceed osmotic pressure), pure water drips out, leaving behind **concentrated milk** with all its nutrients intact. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Callout:** In **osmosis**, water naturally moves *from dilute to concentrated* side (like a thirsty plant absorbing water). In **Reverse Osmosis**, we *reverse* this by applying pressure — water is forced *from concentrated milk to the pure water side*, leaving the milk solids behind. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value/Range | Units | |---|---|---| | Operating Pressure (Milk RO) | **15 – 40 bar** (typical **25–30 bar** for milk) | bar / MPa | | Operating Temperature | **30 – 50 °C** | °C | | Membrane Pore Size (RO) | **< 1 nm** (non-porous in true sense) | nm | | Rejection (NaCl reference) | **> 99 %** | % | | pH Range | **3 – 11** (depending on membrane) | – | | Typical Flux | **10 – 50 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** | LMH | | Concentration Achieved | Up to **~25–30 % Total Solids** (single stage) | % TS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** RO is classified under **membrane separation processes** along with Ultrafiltration (UF), Nanofiltration (NF), and Microfiltration (MF). All these are governed by the same fundamental transport equations but differ in **pore size** and **applied pressure**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Osmosis vs. Reverse Osmosis — The "Why" Behind RO **Step 1 — Natural Osmosis:** When two solutions of different concentrations are separated by a semipermeable membrane, water (solvent) flows from the **low-concentration (dilute)** side to the **high-concentration (concentrate)** side until equilibrium is reached. **Step 2 — Osmotic Pressure (π):** The pressure that must be applied on the concentrated side to **stop** this natural flow is the **osmotic pressure (π)**. It is a colligative property of the solution. **Step 3 — Reverse Osmosis:** When the applied pressure **ΔP** on the concentrated side is **greater than π**, the natural flow direction is reversed — water is forced from the concentrated side to the dilute side, leaving solutes behind. **Step 4 — Why It Matters in Milk:** Milk is a complex colloidal system containing water, lactose, casein, whey proteins, fats, and minerals. The osmotic pressure of milk is significant (≈ 0.7 – 1.0 MPa or 7–10 bar for whole milk). To concentrate milk, applied pressure must comfortably exceed this. ### 2.2 Process Parameters — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Feed Preparation:** Raw or pasteurized milk is pre-heated to **45–50 °C** to reduce viscosity and increase flux. 2. **High-Pressure Pump:** A positive-displacement pump raises feed pressure to the operating range. 3. **Membrane Module:** Feed flows across the membrane surface; permeate (water) passes through; retentate (concentrated milk) is recirculated. 4. **Pressure Drop Management:** Pressure gradually drops along the module due to friction; staged designs compensate. 5. **Concentration Polarization Control:** Cross-flow velocity is maintained to sweep away accumulated solute at the membrane surface. 6. **Recycle Loop:** Retentate is either taken out at desired TS or recycled for further concentration. 7. **Cleaning-In-Place (CIP):** Periodic alkaline + acid wash restores flux. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY KTU EXAM FORMULAS — Memorize the table below.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | Van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure (ideal dilute solutions) | Pa | | 2 | $J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Solvent (water) flux through RO membrane | m·s⁻¹ or L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ | | 3 | $J_s = B \cdot (C_m - C_p)$ | Solute flux through RO membrane | kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 4 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Rejection coefficient (R = 1 → perfect rejection) | dimensionless | | 5 | $C_m = C_b \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J_w}{k}\right)$ | Concentration polarization (film theory) | kg·m⁻³ | | 6 | $\text{Volume Concentration Ratio (VCR)} = \dfrac{V_f}{V_r}$ | Ratio of feed to retentate volume | dimensionless | | 7 | $\% \text{ TS}_{\text{retentate}} = \%\text{TS}_{\text{feed}} \times \text{VCR}$ | Mass balance for concentration | % | | 8 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of feed recovered as permeate | % | **Symbol Glossary:** - $i$ = van't Hoff factor (≈ 1 for non-electrolytes, ≈ 2 for NaCl) - $C$ = molar concentration (mol·m⁻³) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $A$ = membrane water permeability constant (m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) - $B$ = membrane solute permeability constant (m·s⁻¹) - $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa) - $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (Pa) - $C_f, C_p, C_b, C_m$ = concentrations in feed, permeate, bulk, and at membrane surface - $k$ = mass transfer coefficient (m·s⁻¹) - $V_f, V_r, V_p$ = volumes of feed, retentate, and permeate ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy Efficiency:** RO uses **5–10 times less energy** than thermal evaporation for the same water removal (≈ 4–7 kWh per 1000 kg water removed vs. 25–50 kWh for evaporators). - **Pre-concentration Synergy:** RO is universally installed **before multi-effect evaporators** in modern dairy plants — the "RO + TVC evaporator" combo is industry standard. - **Effluent Treatment:** Dairy wastewater (BOD ~ 3000 mg/L) can be concentrated by RO, reducing disposal volume by 80–90 %. - **Whey Valorization:** Acid/cheese whey concentrated from ~6 % TS to 25 % TS using RO before spray drying into WPC/WPI. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** RO does **not** change the phase of water (no boiling/condensation), so heat-sensitive milk components (whey proteins, vitamins) are preserved — a major advantage over evaporation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Flux Equation $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ Starting from the **solution-diffusion model** (the most accepted model for RO transport): **Step 1:** The chemical potential of water in the feed side is: $$\mu_w^{feed} = \mu_w^{\circ} + V_w (P_f - P^{\circ}) - V_w \pi_f$$ **Step 2:** On the permeate side, the chemical potential is: $$\mu_w^{perm} = \mu_w^{\circ} + V_w (P_p - P^{\circ}) - V_w \pi_p$$ **Step 3:** Net driving force per mole = difference in chemical potential: $$\Delta \mu_w = V_w [(P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p)]$$ **Step 4:** Since permeate is essentially pure water, $\pi_p \approx 0$, and $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$, $\Delta \pi = \pi_f - \pi_p = \pi_f$: $$\Delta \mu_w = V_w (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ **Step 5:** Flux is proportional to driving force (Fick's law analog for membranes): $$J_w = \frac{D_w \cdot c_w}{R T} \cdot \Delta \mu_w$$ **Step 6:** Substituting and defining $A = \dfrac{D_w c_w V_w}{R T}$ as the **membrane water permeability constant**: $$\boxed{J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}$$ **Interpretation:** $J_w = 0$ when $\Delta P = \Delta \pi$ (equilibrium); $J_w > 0$ only when applied pressure exceeds osmotic pressure. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rejection Coefficient $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$ By definition, the rejection coefficient is the fraction of solute **retained** by the membrane: **Step 1:** Mass of solute entering (feed side) per unit area per unit time = $J_f \cdot C_f$ **Step 2:** Mass of solute leaving in permeate = $J_p \cdot C_p$ **Step 3:** Mass retained = $J_f \cdot C_f - J_p \cdot C_p$ **Step 4:** Assuming volumetric flux is conserved ($J_f \approx J_p$ at steady state): $$\text{Retained solute flux} = J_w (C_f - C_p)$$ **Step 5:** Define rejection as ratio of retained to fed: $$R = \frac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$ **Interpretation:** $R = 1$ means complete rejection (e.g., RO for NaCl is 99 %); $R = 0$ means no rejection. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentration Polarization $C_m = C_b \cdot \exp(J_w / k)$ **Step 1:** Consider a thin boundary layer (thickness $\delta$) adjacent to the membrane surface. Solute is convectively carried toward the membrane by $J_w$. **Step 2:** At steady state, convective flux toward membrane = back-diffusive flux + permeate solute flux: $$J_w \cdot C = D \frac{dC}{dy} + J_w \cdot C_p$$ **Step 3:** Rearranging: $$J_w (C - C_p) = D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ **Step 4:** Separating variables and integrating from bulk ($C = C_b$, $y = 0$) to membrane surface ($C = C_m$, $y = \delta$): $$\int_{C_b}^{C_m} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = \frac{J_w}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy$$ **Step 5:** The left integral evaluates to $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$. The right side becomes $J_w \cdot \delta / D = J_w / k$ where $k = D / \delta$ is the mass transfer coefficient. **Step 6:** For RO, $C_p \ll C_b$ and $C_p \ll C_m$, so: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_m}{C_b}\right) = \frac{J_w}{k}$$ **Step 7:** Exponentiating both sides: $$\boxed{\frac{C_m}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)}$$ **Interpretation:** $C_m > C_b$ always — the membrane "sees" a higher concentration than the bulk. Higher flux or lower mass transfer (i.e., lower cross-flow) → worse polarization → potential fouling. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style Problem) **Problem:** A dairy plant processes **10,000 L/day of skim milk** containing **9 % Total Solids (TS)**. Reverse osmosis concentrates it to **22 % TS** in a single stage. The membrane has water permeability $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ and operates at $\Delta P = 30$ bar. Average osmotic pressure of milk at 22 % TS ≈ 25 bar. The feed is at 45 °C. Calculate: 1. Net driving pressure (NDP) 2. Water flux $J_w$ 3. Volume Concentration Ratio (VCR) 4. Final retentate volume 5. Permeate volume **Solution:** **Part (a) — Net Driving Pressure:** $$\Delta P = 30 \text{ bar} = 30 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\Delta \pi = 25 \text{ bar} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\text{NDP} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi = (3.0 - 2.5) \times 10^6 = 5.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ bar}$$ **Part (b) — Water Flux:** $$J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi) = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 5.0 \times 10^5$$ $$J_w = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m·s}^{-1} = 21.6 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$$ **Part (c) — VCR (from mass balance):** Solids are conserved. Initial solids = $10{,}000 \times 0.09 = 900$ kg. Final %TS = 22 %. $$V_r = \frac{900}{0.22} = 4090.9 \text{ L} \approx 4091 \text{ L}$$ $$\text{VCR} = \frac{V_f}{V_r} = \frac{10{,}000}{4091} \approx 2.44$$ **Part (d) — Permeate Volume:** $$V_p = V_f - V_r = 10{,}000 - 4091 = 5909 \text{ L/day}$$ **Recovery:** $$\text{Recovery} = \frac{5909}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 59.1\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Insight:** As TS rises, $\Delta \pi$ rises and NDP drops → flux decays exponentially. This is why **multi-stage RO** with progressively higher pressure pumps is used in industrial practice. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Osmosis vs Reverse Osmosis (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OSMOSIS["Natural Osmosis - Low Pressure"] D1["Dilute Side Pure Water"] -->|"Water flows naturally"| C1["Concentrated Side Milk Solutes"] end subgraph RO["Reverse Osmosis - High Pressure Applied"] D2["Concentrated Milk"] -->|"Water forced across membrane"| P2["Permeate Pure Water"] HX["High Pressure Pump greater than Osmotic Pressure"] --> D2 end OSMOSIS ~~~ RO ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — RO System Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB FeedTank["Feed Tank
Milk 9 percent TS"] --> HX1["Plate Heat Exchanger
Heat to 45 to 50 C"] HX1 --> HPump["High Pressure Pump
30 to 40 bar"] HPump --> M1["Membrane Module 1
Spiral Wound RO"] M1 -->|"Permeate Water"| Perm["Permeate Collection
or Recycle"] M1 -->|"Retentate 14 percent TS"| M2["Membrane Module 2
Staging for higher TS"] M2 -->|"Permeate Water"| Perm M2 -->|"Final Retentate 22 percent TS"| Prod["Concentrated Milk
to Evaporator or Storage"] M1 -.->|"CIP Loop"| CIP["Cleaning In Place
Alkaline plus Acid Wash"] M2 -.->|"CIP Loop"| CIP ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Membrane Cross-Section & Rejection Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MEM["RO Membrane Cross Section"] FS["Feed Side
Milk Water plus Solutes"] ACT["Active Layer
Polyamide 0.1 to 1 nm effective"] SUP["Support Layer
Polysulfone 50 to 100 microns"] PER["Permeate Side
Pure Water"] end FS -->|"Water molecules pass"| ACT ACT -->|"Lactose 1 nm blocked"| SUP ACT -->|"Proteins 5 to 50 nm blocked"| SUP ACT -->|"Minerals partial rejection"| SUP ACT -->|"Water only"| PER ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Concentration Polarization Phenomenon ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BL["Boundary Layer at Membrane"] Bulk["Bulk Flow
Concentration Cb"] BL1["Concentration Gradient"] MS["Membrane Surface
Concentration Cm greater than Cb"] end Bulk -->|"Convection Jw Cb"| BL1 BL1 -->|"Back Diffusion D dCdy"| MS MS -->|"Permeation"| PermOut["Permeate Cp much less than Cm"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All Mermaid blocks use only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and clean uppercase labels without markdown bold/italic inside the quoted text — adhering to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 compilation safeguard protocol. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Reverse Osmosis. Mention any two advantages of RO over thermal evaporation in dairy processing.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Reverse Osmosis is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which water from a solution (e.g., milk) is forced through a semipermeable membrane against its natural osmotic flow by applying a hydraulic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the solution. **Two Advantages of RO over Evaporation:** 1. **Lower Energy Consumption:** RO consumes only 4–7 kWh per 1000 kg of water removed, whereas thermal evaporation consumes 25–50 kWh for the same duty — a 5–10 fold saving. 2. **No Phase Change / Heat Damage:** RO operates at ambient/mild temperature (30–50 °C) without boiling, so heat-sensitive components like whey proteins, vitamins, and immunoglobulins are preserved with negligible denaturation. 3. *(Bonus point)* RO simultaneously removes water and partially demineralizes; it can also handle **dairy wastewater** for zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) plants. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is osmotic pressure? Write the Van't Hoff equation and state its significance in RO operation.** **Model Answer:** **Osmotic Pressure (π):** It is the minimum hydraulic pressure that must be applied to the concentrated (solution) side of a semipermeable membrane to **prevent the inward flow of water** from the dilute side — i.e., the pressure at which the natural osmotic flow is just halted. **Van't Hoff Equation:** $$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$ Where $i$ = van't Hoff factor, $C$ = molar concentration (mol·L⁻¹), $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ = absolute temperature (K). **Significance in RO:** - It determines the **minimum pressure** that must be exceeded for water flux to occur: $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$. - For milk with ~9–12 % TS, $\pi \approx$ 7–15 bar; for concentrated milk at 22 % TS, $\pi \approx$ 20–30 bar. - It sets the **practical upper limit** of single-stage RO concentration; beyond a point, $\Delta \pi$ approaches applied $\Delta P$ and flux becomes uneconomical. --- ### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)** --- #### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q.A. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle and working of the Reverse Osmosis process for milk concentration. Discuss the types of membranes and modules used. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Principle:** RO is governed by the **solution-diffusion mechanism**. When hydraulic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of milk is applied across a semipermeable membrane, water (the solvent) is selectively forced through the membrane pores, while the larger solute molecules — lactose, proteins, minerals — are rejected, yielding a **concentrated retentate** and a **purified water permeate**. **Working (Step-by-Step):** 1. Pre-treated milk (pasteurized, standardized, cooled to < 50 °C) is drawn from the feed tank. 2. A **high-pressure pump** (typically positive displacement) raises pressure to 25–40 bar. 3. Milk enters the **membrane module** under cross-flow; turbulence at the membrane surface minimizes concentration polarization. 4. **Permeate** (water) exits at low pressure from the permeate channel. 5. **Retentate** (concentrated milk) exits the module at high pressure, either as product (single-pass) or recycled to increase TS (recirculation mode). 6. The retentate may be sent to a second RO stage at higher pressure for further concentration, or directly to an evaporator for finish concentration. **Types of Membranes:** | Membrane Type | Material | Property | |---|---|---| | Cellulose Acetate (CA) | Cellulose derivative | Hydrophilic, chlorine-tolerant, lower flux, pH 3–7 | | Polyamide (PA) Thin-Film Composite (TFC) | Aromatic polyamide on polysulfone support | High flux, high rejection, pH 2–11, chlorine-sensitive | | Polyethersulfone (PES) | PES composite | Good thermal/chemical stability | **Types of Modules:** 1. **Spiral-Wound Module** — Most common in dairy; flat membrane sheets with feed spacer, glued and rolled. High packing density (~1000 m²/m³), easy to clean. 2. **Hollow-Fiber Module** — Bundles of fine fibers; used for water but rarely for milk due to fouling. 3. **Plate-and-Frame Module** — Used for viscous feeds like whey; easier to clean; lower packing density. 4. **Tubular Module** — Used for highly fouling feeds; large bore tubes, easy to clean mechanically. --- **Q.A. (b)** A dairy plant concentrates **5000 L of skim milk** (8.5 % TS) to **20 % TS** using RO. The membrane area is **100 m²**, and the average water flux is **18 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹**. Calculate: (i) Final retentate volume (ii) Permeate volume (iii) Volume Concentration Ratio (iv) Time required for the process (v) Percentage recovery **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Final Retentate Volume:** Solids are conserved: $V_f \times TS_f = V_r \times TS_r$ $$V_r = \frac{5000 \times 0.085}{0.20} = \frac{425}{0.20} = 2125 \text{ L}$$ **(ii) Permeate Volume:** $$V_p = V_f - V_r = 5000 - 2125 = 2875 \text{ L}$$ **(iii) Volume Concentration Ratio:** $$\text{VCR} = \frac{V_f}{V_r} = \frac{5000}{2125} \approx 2.35$$ **(iv) Time Required:** Total permeate flow rate = $J_w \times A = 18 \times 100 = 1800$ L/h $$t = \frac{V_p}{\text{Flow rate}} = \frac{2875}{1800} = 1.597 \text{ h} \approx 1.6 \text{ h} = 96 \text{ minutes}$$ **(v) Percentage Recovery:** $$\text{Recovery} = \frac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100 = \frac{2875}{5000} \times 100 = 57.5\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion error:** L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ must be multiplied by area in m² to get L/h — do NOT confuse with kg/m²/s. > 2. **Mass balance error:** Many students forget that %TS × Volume = constant (solids don't pass membrane). > 3. **Confusing VCR with Recovery:** VCR = $V_f/V_r$; Recovery = $V_p/V_f$. These are different quantities. > 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Always write "Assuming no solids pass the membrane" and "Steady-state operation." --- #### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q.B. (a)** Explain the phenomenon of **concentration polarization** in RO. Derive the film-theory equation relating membrane surface concentration to bulk concentration. Discuss the operating strategies to control it. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Phenomenon:** Concentration polarization (CP) refers to the **build-up of retained solute** at the membrane surface, forming a thin, highly concentrated boundary layer. As water is forced through the membrane by $\Delta P$, solutes rejected by the membrane accumulate near the surface — creating a concentration gradient **higher than the bulk feed**. **Consequences of CP:** - Reduced effective driving force (higher $\pi_m$ lowers $\Delta\pi$). - Increased fouling and scaling risk. - Flux decline and altered rejection. **Derivation** (full derivation as in Section 3.3 above): At steady state, convection of solute to membrane = back-diffusion + solute in permeate: $$J_w (C - C_p) = D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ Integrating from bulk to membrane surface: $$\int_{C_b}^{C_m} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = \frac{J_w}{D}\int_0^{\delta}dy$$ Evaluating with mass transfer coefficient $k = D/\delta$ and $C_p \ll C_b$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_m}{C_b}\right) = \frac{J_w}{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{C_m}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)}$$ **Control Strategies:** 1. **High Cross-Flow Velocity** — increases $k$, sweeps away polarized layer. 2. **Turbulence Promoters / Spacers** — disturb the boundary layer in spiral-wound modules. 3. **Pulsed Flow / Backflushing** — periodic reversal dislodges foulant layer. 4. **Low Flux Operation** — operate below the **limiting flux** ($J_{lim} = k \ln(C_m^{crit}/C_b)$). 5. **Pretreatment** — microfiltration to remove suspended solids before RO. 6. **Membrane Surface Modification** — use hydrophilic or anti-fouling coatings. 7. **Periodic CIP** — enzymatic/alkaline cleaning protocols. --- **Q.B. (b)** Compare **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** and **Ultrafiltration (UF)** with respect to: (i) membrane pore size, (ii) operating pressure, (iii) components separated, (iv) typical industrial application in dairy, and (v) flux equation form. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Reverse Osmosis (RO) | Ultrafiltration (UF) | |---|---|---| | **(i) Membrane Pore Size** | < 1 nm (non-porous, dense) | 1 – 100 nm (microporous) | | **(ii) Operating Pressure** | 15 – 40 bar (high) | 1 – 5 bar (low) | | **(iii) Components Separated** | Water from dissolved salts, lactose, minerals | Water + small molecules pass; macromolecules (proteins) retained | | **(iv) Typical Dairy Application** | Pre-concentration of milk/whey; effluent treatment | Whey protein concentration; protein standardization of milk | | **(v) Flux Equation** | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ (osmotic-pressure dependent) | $J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ (Darcy's law, viscosity dependent) | | **Rejection Mechanism** | Solution-diffusion (true membrane) | Sieving (size exclusion) | | **Concentration TS Limit** | Up to ~25–30 % TS | Up to ~22 % TS for whey; lower flux at high TS | | **Membrane Materials** | Polyamide TFC, CA | PES, PVDF, regenerated cellulose | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Errors:** > 1. **Confusing MWCO with pore size:** UF is characterized by Molecular Weight Cut-Off (e.g., 10 kDa, 50 kDa) — not directly by pore size. RO is characterized by **rejection of NaCl**. > 2. **Wrong flux equation:** Don't write UF flux with the $\Delta \pi$ term; it is irrelevant for UF since osmotic pressure of retained proteins is negligible. > 3. **Misclassification:** RO is a *dense membrane* process (solution-diffusion); UF is a *porous membrane* process (sieving). --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Rapid Recap — Memorize Before Exam:** - ✅ RO = Pressure-driven membrane process, requires **$\Delta P > \Delta\pi$** to operate. - ✅ Flux equation: $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ — memorize the form, NOT just the symbols. - ✅ Rejection $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ — for NaCl on RO, $R > 99\%$. - ✅ Operating pressure for milk RO: **15–40 bar**; temperature: **30–50 °C**. - ✅ Milk RO concentrates up to **25–30 % TS** in a single stage. - ✅ **Concentration polarization** is described by $C_m/C_b = \exp(J_w/k)$ — always $> 1$. - ✅ **Mass balance for VCR:** $V_r = V_f \times (TS_f / TS_r)$; Recovery = $V_p / V_f \times 100$. - ✅ **Module types:** Spiral-wound (most common in dairy), Tubular, Plate-and-frame, Hollow-fiber. - ✅ **Membrane materials:** Polyamide TFC (preferred), Cellulose acetate (legacy). - ✅ **RO vs UF difference:** RO separates **water from solutes** (dense membrane); UF separates **macromolecules from water** (porous membrane). - ✅ **Energy advantage:** RO uses 5–10× less energy than evaporation. - ✅ **Industrial use:** Pre-concentration before evaporation, whey concentration, effluent treatment, partial demineralization. - ✅ **Osmotic pressure of milk** ≈ 0.7–1.0 MPa at 9–12 % TS; rises sharply with TS. - ✅ **Van't Hoff equation** $\pi = iCRT$ is valid for dilute solutions; for milk, empirical correlations are used. - ✅ **Fouling control:** Pretreatment + high cross-flow + periodic CIP. - ✅ **Limiting flux concept:** $J_{lim} = k \ln(C_m^{crit}/C_b)$ — operation must stay below this. - ✅ Always state **assumptions** in numerical problems: no solute leakage, steady state, uniform pressure, isothermal. > **End of Module 3 — Reverse Osmosis Process Notes** > **Course:** PCFTT502 — Processing of Milk & Milk Products | **KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Electrodialysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrodialysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrodialysis (ED)** is an electrochemical membrane separation process in which ions are transported selectively from one solution to another through **ion-exchange membranes (IEMs)** under the driving force of a **direct current (DC) electric field**. In the dairy industry, ED is principally employed for the **demineralization (desalination)** of cheese whey, the **deacidification** of fermented milk products, and the **partial demineralization of milk** for the manufacture of infant formula and other specialty dairy ingredients.

The process relies on two distinct classes of membranes:
- **Cation Exchange Membranes (CEMs):** Permeable exclusively to positively charged ions (cations such as Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺).
- **Anion Exchange Membranes (AEMs):** Permeable exclusively to negatively charged ions (anions such as Cl⁻, lactate, citrate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Under Module 3 ("Membrane Processing"), ED is a high-priority topic because it routinely appears in ESE as either a Part A 3-mark conceptual question or as a 14-mark essay on membrane separations in dairy processing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Selective Bouncer" at a Club

Imagine a long corridor (the **ED stack**) with two security checkpoints placed alternately:

1. **Checkpoint A (CEM)** only lets in people wearing a **red wristband** (cations, e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺).
2. **Checkpoint B (AEM)** only lets in people wearing a **blue wristband** (anions, e.g., Cl⁻, lactate).

An electrical "push" (the DC field) drives everyone down the corridor. As the crowd moves, red-wristband people are funnelled into one room (the **concentrate/brine stream**) and blue-wristband people into the other (the **dilute/demineralized stream**). The neutral molecules (water, lactose, proteins) cannot pass any checkpoint, so they remain in the **dilute stream** — exactly the outcome we want for *whey demineralization*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Operating current density | **200 – 500 A/m²** |
| Cell pair voltage drop | **0.5 – 1.5 V per cell pair** |
| Membrane thickness | **0.1 – 0.6 mm** |
| Whey demineralization (one pass) | **25 – 60 % ash removal** |
| Process temperature | **25 – 40 °C** |
| Energy consumption (whey) | **0.4 – 0.8 kWh per kg ash removed** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Footnote:** ED as an industrial unit operation was commercialised in the 1950s. Its adoption in the dairy sector accelerated after the 1970s whey-utilization boom, when converting cheese whey permeate into high-value demineralized whey powder became economically attractive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of ED – Membranes and Driving Force

The operation of an electrodialysis stack rests on three coupled mechanisms: **selective ion transport through IEMs**, **Faradaic electrochemical reactions at the electrodes**, and **concentration polarization** at the membrane-solution interfaces.

### 2.1.1 Ion-Exchange Membranes (IEMs)

A membrane is essentially a **cross-linked polymer matrix** (commonly polystyrene-divinylbenzene for older types, or fluorocarbon for newer ones) bearing fixed ionic groups:

- **CEM** – fixed anionic groups (–SO₃⁻, –COO⁻) → repels anions, attracts cations.
- **AEM** – fixed cationic groups (–N(CH₃)₃⁺, –NH₃⁺) → repels cations, attracts anions.

The **permselectivity** $P$ quantifies how well a membrane distinguishes between counter-ions and co-ions:

$$P = \frac{t_{m} - t_{s}}{1 - t_{s}}$$

where $t_{m}$ is the transport number of the counter-ion in the membrane and $t_{s}$ is the transport number in free solution. For an ideal membrane, $P \rightarrow 1$.

### 2.1.2 The DC Electric Field

The driving force is a **direct current** imposed between two electrodes. The cell voltage per cell pair follows from Ohm's law applied to the membrane stack:

$$U_{cell} = i \cdot R_{cell}$$

where $i$ is the current density (A m⁻²) and $R_{cell}$ is the total area-specific resistance of one cell pair (Ω m²).

### 2.1.3 Electrode Reactions (Faradaic)

At the **anode** (oxidation):

$$2 H_2O \rightarrow O_2 \uparrow + 4 H^+ + 4 e^-$$

At the **cathode** (reduction):

$$2 H_2O + 2 e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2 OH^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The electrode streams (anolyte / catholyte) must be isolated from the main process streams. In dairy plants, a **Na₂SO₄ or K₂SO₄ solution** is used to minimise chlorine gas evolution and protect electrodes from fouling.

## 2.2 Stack Architecture and Flow Modes

A practical ED unit contains **100–600 cell pairs** stacked between a single pair of electrodes. The dilute (demineralized) and concentrate (brine) streams flow in either:

- **Co-current flow** – simplest; suited for low-viscosity feeds.
- **Counter-current flow** – uniform residence time; higher driving force.
- **Electrode-membrane feed-and-bleed** – continuous recirculation loop.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law – mass of ion transported | kg |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Current efficiency (Faradaic efficiency) | % |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{t_{m} - t_{s}}{1 - t_{s}}$ | Membrane permselectivity | – |
| 4 | $U_{cell} = i \cdot R_{cell}$ | Voltage per cell pair (Ohm's law) | V |
| 5 | $E = z \cdot F \cdot \Delta \mu$ | Energy per mole from electrochemical potential | J mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{removed}}$ | Specific energy consumption | kWh kg⁻¹ |
| 7 | $i_{lim} = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot c}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density (concentration polarization limit) | A m⁻² |
| 8 | $Ash\ removal\ \% = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Whey demineralization efficiency | % |
| 9 | $R_{total} = R_{CEM} + R_{AEM} + 2 R_{spacer}$ | Total stack resistance (per cell pair) | Ω m² |
| 10 | $Q_{demin} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot n_{cp}}{F \cdot c_{in}}$ | Throughput equation for demineralization | m³ h⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation safety:** All absolute-value bars in the table are written as `\vert` to keep Markdown tables valid. The symbol $F$ denotes **Faraday's constant = 96 485 C mol⁻¹**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an Indian dairy cooperative processing 5 × 10⁵ L of cheese whey per day, ED offers:
- **Recovery of valuable whey proteins** by partial deashing prior to ultrafiltration.
- **Production of 90 % demineralized whey powder (DWP-90)** used in infant formula — a multi-million-dollar global market.
- **Reduced environmental load** because salty whey is no longer a disposal problem.
- **A "green" pre-concentration step** downstream of RO or evaporator trains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the rigorous derivations that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark essay questions. Each line is followed by a plain-English reasoning row.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Ions Transported (Faraday's Law of Electrolysis)

**Statement:** The mass of an ion liberated or transported at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.

**Starting point** — Definition of the Faraday constant:

$$F = N_A \cdot e$$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ (Avogadro's number) and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ (electronic charge). Therefore each mole of electrons carries a charge of exactly $F = 96\,485$ C.

**Step 1** — Express the amount of charge passed in time $t$ at current $I$:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

The reasoning is direct: current (A = C s⁻¹) multiplied by time (s) yields total charge (C).

**Step 2** — Convert total charge to moles of electrons:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

Each electron can move one unit of charge; therefore moles of charge-carriers equal total charge divided by charge-per-mole.

**Step 3** — Relate moles of electrons to moles of ions using ion valency $z$. For an ion of charge $z$, one mole of ions requires $z$ moles of electrons:

$$n_{ion} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 4** — Convert moles of ions to mass using molar mass $M$:

$$m = n_{ion} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

This is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** — the bedrock equation for any ED energy balance.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** A dairy plant operates an ED stack at $I = 100$ A for $t = 1$ h to remove Na⁺ from whey. $M_{Na} = 23$ g mol⁻¹, $z = 1$, $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹.

$$m_{Na} = \frac{100 \times 3600 \times 23}{1 \times 96\,485} = \frac{8\,280\,000}{96\,485} = 85.82 \text{ g}$$

So **85.82 g of Na⁺** is theoretically transported per hour.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Efficiency (Faradaic Efficiency)

**Starting point** — Faraday's law gives the *theoretical* mass; the *actual* mass is lower because of:

- Membrane imperfections (co-ion leakage).
- Water splitting at high current densities.
- Back-diffusion of ions from concentrate to dilute.

**Step 1** — Define the current efficiency (also called *current utilization* or *Faradaic efficiency*):

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{actual} \cdot z \cdot F}{I \cdot t \cdot M}$$

**Step 2** — Express in terms of equivalent charge transferred. Let $z \cdot n$ be the number of equivalents removed; then:

$$\eta = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot n_{removed}}{I \cdot t}$$

**Step 3** — For a stack with $n_{cp}$ cell pairs, the *total* current passes through every membrane, so the same $I \cdot t$ applies to all pairs. The current efficiency per cell pair remains as above.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** A 200-cell-pair stack is operated at 200 A for 2 h. Theoretically it can transport $85.82 \times 200 \times 2 = 34\,328$ g of Na⁺. Actually, $28\,500$ g is removed. Then:

$$\eta = \frac{28\,500}{34\,328} \times 100 = 83.0 \ \%$$

A typical *good* whey ED plant runs at $\eta = 80$–$90\ \%$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption

**Step 1** — DC electrical energy supplied to the stack:

$$E_{DC} = U_{stack} \cdot I \cdot t$$

with $U_{stack} = n_{cp} \cdot U_{cell}$.

**Step 2** — Mass of ash actually removed from whey in that period, expressed in kg.

**Step 3** — Specific energy consumption per kg ash removed:

$$W_{sp} = \frac{U_{stack} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{ash}} \quad \text{[kWh kg}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute Faraday's law and $\eta$ to obtain an *analytical* design equation:

$$W_{sp} = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot U_{cell}}{3600 \cdot M \cdot \eta}$$

> The **lower the cell voltage, the higher the current efficiency, the lower the energy per kg of ash removed.** This is the design mantra of every ED plant.

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Let $U_{cell} = 1.2$ V, $\eta = 0.85$, $M_{NaCl} = 58.5$ g mol⁻¹, $z = 1$:

$$W_{sp} = \frac{1 \times 96\,485 \times 1.2}{3600 \times 58.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{115\,782}{178\,965} = 0.647 \text{ kWh kg}^{-1}$$

This is a realistic figure for industrial whey demineralization.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Limiting Current Density (Concentration Polarization)

When current density $i$ rises, the ion concentration at the membrane-solution interface of the *diluate* side falls. The maximum sustainable current is reached when this interfacial concentration approaches zero — the **limiting current density**.

**Step 1** — Fick's first law for ion flux from bulk to interface:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dc}{dx} = \frac{D \cdot (c_b - c_i)}{\delta}$$

**Step 2** — At the limiting condition, $c_i \to 0$, so $J_{max} = D \cdot c_b / \delta$.

**Step 3** — Equate to the Faradaic flux delivered by current density $i$:

$$\frac{i_{lim}}{z \cdot F} = \frac{D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

**Step 4** — Solve for $i_{lim}$:

$$i_{lim} = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Operating above $i_{lim}$ causes **water splitting** (H⁺ and OH⁻ generated at the membrane surface), which (a) lowers current efficiency, (b) shifts pH, and (c) may damage the membrane. KTU exams frequently test the identification of this regime.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operating Parameters vs Performance

| Operating Lever | ↑ Effect on Demineralization Rate | ↑ Effect on Current Efficiency | ↑ Effect on Membrane Fouling | Practical Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current density $i$ | ↑ linearly | slight ↓ | ↑ | $i < i_{lim}$ |
| Temperature | ↑ | ↑ initially, ↓ at high T | ↑ | < 40 °C in dairy |
| Flow velocity | ↑ mass transfer | ↑ | ↓ | < 8 cm s⁻¹ (pressure drop) |
| Membrane area | ↑ throughput | neutral | neutral | capital cost |
| Number of cell pairs $n_{cp}$ | ↑ stage removal | neutral | neutral | voltage drop |
| Spacer thickness | ↑ throughput | ↓ (longer path) | ↑ biofilm | pressure drop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of an Electrodialysis Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed1[Raw Whey Tank] --> pump1[Feed Pump]
    pump1 --> bal1[Pressure Balancing Tank]
    bal1 --> hx1[Plate Heat Exchanger 25 to 35 C]
    hx1 --> filter1[Microfilter 5 micron]
    filter1 --> stack1[ED Stack 200 Cell Pairs]
    stack1 --> dil1[Diluate Holding Tank]
    dil1 --> spray1[Spray Dryer]
    spray1 --> prod1[Demineralized Whey Powder]
    stack1 --> conc1[Concentrate Brine Stream]
    conc1 --> ro1[RO Recovery Unit]
    ro1 --> rec1[Recovered Salt Water]
    ro1 --> brine1[Disposal or Salt Recovery]
    elec1[Electrode Rinse Loop] -.-> stack1
    elec1 --> elec2[Anolyte Catholyte Heat Exchanger]
    elec2 --> stack1
    ctrl1[PLC and DC Rectifier] -.-> stack1
    ctrl1 -.-> pump1
    ctrl1 -.-> elec2
    ph1[Inline pH and Conductivity Sensors] -.-> ctrl1
```

*Caption.* Functional flow of an industrial whey ED plant. Note the closed electrode loop, the inline process analytics, and the brine recovery sub-system. The PLC continuously regulates current density to stay below $i_{lim}$.

## 4.2 Cell-Pair Ion-Transport Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    anode1[Anode Positive Electrode] --> csp1[Concentrate Spacer]
    csp1 --> cem1[CEM Permeable to Cations Only]
    cem1 --> dsp1[Diluate Spacer Whey Stream]
    dsp1 --> aem1[AEM Permeable to Anions Only]
    aem1 --> csp2[Concentrate Spacer]
    csp2 --> cem2[CEM]
    cem2 --> dsp2[Diluate Spacer Whey Stream]
    dsp2 --> aem2[AEM]
    aem2 --> csp3[Concentrate Spacer]
    csp3 --> cat1[Cathode Negative Electrode]
    Na1[Na plus Cation] -. moves left to right .-> csp1
    Na1 -. through CEM .-> cem1
    Na1 -. trapped in concentrate .-> csp3
    Cl1[Cl minus Anion] -. moves right to left .-> csp3
    Cl1 -. through AEM .-> aem2
    Cl1 -. trapped in concentrate .-> csp1
    lac1[Lactate Anion] -. similar path .-> aem1
```

*Caption.* In one cell pair, the whey stream (diluate) is sandwiched between a CEM and an AEM. Cations leave through the CEM, anions leave through the AEM, and the whey becomes progressively *demineralized*. The neighbouring concentrate spacers receive the ejected ions.

## 4.3 Voltage Distribution Across One Cell Pair

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    term1[Terminal Voltage U stack] --> ec[Electrode Overpotential eta]
    ec --> v1[Voltage across concentrate spacer]
    v1 --> vc[Voltage across CEM]
    vc --> vd[Voltage across diluate spacer]
    vd --> va[Voltage across AEM]
    va --> ec2[Returns to next pair]
```

*Caption.* The total stack voltage is the sum of all these drops. The diluate spacer typically has the **highest resistance** because ion concentration falls as the fluid moves down the stack — this is why ED engineers segment the stack into **2 or 3 hydraulic stages** with intermediate re-pumping.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define electrodialysis. Name the two types of ion-exchange membranes used and state one specific industrial application of ED in dairy processing.

**Model Answer.** Electrodialysis is an electrochemical membrane separation process in which ions are selectively transported through cation- and anion-exchange membranes under the influence of a direct current electric field. The two membrane types are the **cation-exchange membrane (CEM)** which permits only cations (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) and the **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)** which permits only anions (e.g., Cl⁻, lactate, citrate). The principal dairy application is the **demineralization of cheese whey** to produce 90 % demineralized whey powder (DWP-90) for infant formula manufacture. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for membrane identification, 1 for application.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is *limiting current density* in an electrodialysis cell? What happens if the operating current density exceeds this limit?

**Model Answer.** Limiting current density $i_{lim}$ is the maximum current density that can be sustained at a given bulk ion concentration $c_b$, diffusion coefficient $D$, and boundary-layer thickness $\delta$, expressed as $i_{lim} = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$. It is reached when the ion concentration at the membrane–solution interface falls to zero. If the operating current exceeds $i_{lim}$, water-splitting occurs (H⁺ and OH⁻ are generated), which reduces current efficiency, shifts pH, and can permanently damage the membrane. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + equation, 1 for condition of interface depletion, 1 for consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice Question]**

### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of an electrodialysis cell pair** for the demineralization of cheese whey. Clearly distinguish between CEM and AEM, and indicate the direction of ionic migration under the influence of the applied DC field. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **specific energy consumption** of an electrodialysis plant in kWh per kg of salt removed, starting from Faraday's law of electrolysis. A 250-cell-pair ED plant operates at 150 A for 4 hours and removes 9.50 kg of NaCl during that period. Calculate the current efficiency and the specific energy consumption if the average cell-pair voltage is 1.1 V. (Take $M_{NaCl} = 58.5$ g mol⁻¹, $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹.) *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**Construction of a Cell Pair:**

1. A single cell pair consists of **five flat elements** clamped together:
   - A **cation-exchange membrane (CEM)** — fixed anionic groups, e.g., –SO₃⁻.
   - A **diluate spacer gasket** (1–2 mm thick) that defines the whey flow channel.
   - An **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)** — fixed cationic groups, e.g., –N(CH₃)₃⁺.
   - A **concentrate spacer gasket** that defines the brine flow channel.
   - The pair is then repeated: 200–600 such pairs are stacked between two electrodes.

2. The spacers are usually **screen-like turbulence promoters** that enhance mass transfer and reduce concentration polarization.

**Working:**

1. Whey enters the **diluate compartment**; a NaCl/KCl solution enters the **concentrate compartment**.
2. A **DC voltage** is applied — anode on the side of the AEM first, cathode on the side of the CEM first, alternately.
3. **Cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)** in the whey are electrostatically driven toward the **cathode**. They pass through the **CEM** (which carries fixed negative charges and therefore repels anions) but are **blocked by the next AEM**. So they accumulate in the concentrate stream.
4. **Anions (Cl⁻, lactate, citrate)** are driven toward the **anode**. They pass through the **AEM** (fixed positive groups) but are **blocked by the next CEM**. They too accumulate in the concentrate.
5. Neutral species — **water, lactose, whey proteins** — cannot pass any membrane. They remain in the diluate stream, which emerges *demineralized* but compositionally intact for its valuable constituents.

> *Suggested diagram:* show CEM | Diluate | AEM | Concentrate | CEM | Diluate | AEM | Concentrate ... with Na⁺ and Cl⁻ arrows moving into the concentrate channels.

*(Mark split: 2 for labelled parts, 2 for ion-direction explanation, 2 for role of each membrane, 1 for concluding sentence on product streams.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption:**

Step 1 — DC energy supplied to the stack:

$$E_{DC} = U_{stack} \cdot I \cdot t = n_{cp} \cdot U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t$$

Step 2 — Theoretical mass of NaCl removable (Faraday's law, $z = 1$):

$$m_{th} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{NaCl}}{z \cdot F}$$

Step 3 — Actual mass removed is $m_{actual} = \eta \cdot m_{th}$.

Step 4 — Specific energy consumption:
