$$W_{sp} = \frac{E_{DC}}{m_{actual}} = \frac{n_{cp} \cdot U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{NaCl}}{z \cdot F}} = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot n_{cp} \cdot U_{cell}}{\eta \cdot M_{NaCl}}$$

> Per kg this becomes (converting J to kWh using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J):

$$W_{sp} = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot U_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot M_{NaCl} \cdot \eta} \quad \text{[kWh kg}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1 — Theoretical NaCl mass:

$$m_{th} = \frac{150 \times 4 \times 3600 \times 58.5}{1 \times 96\,485} = \frac{126\,360\,000}{96\,485} = 1309.6 \text{ g} = 1.310 \text{ kg}$$

Step 2 — Current efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{9.50}{1.310} \times 100$$

> Wait — this is *backwards*. $9.50$ kg cannot be the theoretical mass and $1.310$ kg the actual. The correct interpretation is: **$m_{th} = 1.310$ kg per cell pair**; with 250 cell pairs the *total* theoretical mass is $250 \times 1.310 = 327.5$ kg. So:

$$\eta = \frac{9.50}{327.5} \times 100 = 2.90 \ \%$$

> A more reasonable interpretation: the **stack as a whole** carries 150 A and the equivalent Faraday mass for 4 h is $327.5$ kg; the plant actually removed $9.50$ kg — yielding $\eta = 2.9$ %, which is unrealistically low. The most likely intended interpretation is: $\eta = \dfrac{9.50}{1.310} \times 100 = 725.4$ %, which is also impossible. **Therefore the question implicitly defines $m_{actual} = 9.50$ kg *per cell pair***, and the per-cell-pair theoretical mass is $1.310$ kg. Then:

$$\eta = \frac{9.50}{1.310} \times 100 = 725.4 \ \% \text{ (impossible)}$$

> **Exam tip (model correction):** the data are clearly inconsistent. The correct form of the answer is: *Faraday theoretical mass per cell pair = 1.310 kg; for the full stack = 327.5 kg. The actual removal of 9.50 kg per 4 h for 250 cell pairs corresponds to a per-cell-pair removal of 38 g and a per-cell-pair efficiency of $\eta = 38 / 1310 = 2.9$ %, indicating poor efficiency — students should comment on this as part of the answer.* The *specific energy consumption per kg removed* is:

$$W_{sp} = \frac{U_{stack} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{actual}} = \frac{250 \times 1.1 \times 150 \times 4 \times 3600}{9.50 \times 1000} = \frac{5.94 \times 10^8}{9500} = 62\,526 \text{ J g}^{-1} = 62.5 \text{ kJ g}^{-1} = 17.4 \text{ kWh kg}^{-1}$$

*Acceptable alternative:* if the examiner intended $9.50$ kg as the per-cell-pair actual, then $\eta$ is super-unity (impossible), and full credit is given to the derivation steps and a comment on data inconsistency.

*(Mark split: 2 for full derivation, 2 for Faraday's-law numerical, 2 for $W_{sp}$ numerical, 1 for correct units and comment on $\eta$.)*

### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Discuss the **principle, construction, and industrial applications of electrodialysis in the dairy industry**. Compare ED with **electrodialysis reversal (EDR)** in terms of fouling control. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to **Faraday's laws of electrolysis**, derive the expression for **current efficiency**. An ED plant processes 10 000 L h⁻¹ of cheese whey containing 0.8 % (w/v) total ash. After one pass, the ash content drops to 0.32 % (w/v). If the plant is operated at 250 A and 95 % current efficiency, calculate the **number of cell pairs** required. Assume whey density = 1.03 kg L⁻¹ and average ash molar mass = 60 g mol⁻¹, $z = 1$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**Principle:** Selective ion transport across IEMs under an applied DC field; anions migrate toward the anode through AEM, cations toward the cathode through CEM; whey (diluate) loses minerals while concentrate gains them.

**Construction of ED Module:**
- **Membrane stack** — 100–600 alternating CEM and AEM sheets separated by gaskets.
- **Spacers** — polyolefin turbulence promoters; define flow channels.
- **Electrodes** — Ti/Pt or Ti/MMO (mixed metal oxide) for corrosion resistance.
- **Anolyte / Catholyte circulation** — typically K₂SO₄ or Na₂SO₄.
- **End-plates and tie-rods** — provide mechanical compression.
- **Inlet / outlet manifolds** — distribute diluate, concentrate, and electrode flows.

**Industrial Applications in Dairy:**
1. **Demineralization of sweet and acid cheese whey** — produces DWP-40, DWP-70, DWP-90.
2. **Deacidification of fermented milks** — yogurts, kefir, and buttermilk.
3. **Partial desalination of milk** for infant formula and clinical nutrition products.
4. **Standardization of mineral content** of milk for specific nutritional targets.
5. **Recovery of valuable salts** (e.g., potassium citrate) from the concentrate stream.

**Comparison: ED vs EDR (Electrodialysis Reversal):**

| Feature | Conventional ED | EDR |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity reversal | None (every 1–8 h shut down for CIP) | Automatic reversal every 15–30 min |
| Fouling control | Chemical CIP needed | Self-cleaning — particles and scale slough off during reversal |
| Down-time | Higher | Lower (~5 %) |
| Capital cost | Lower | ~10–15 % higher |
| Membrane life | 3–5 years | 5–8 years |
| Calcium-phosphate scaling | Major issue | Largely suppressed |

*(Mark split: 2 principle, 2 construction, 2 applications, 1 ED vs EDR comparison.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Derivation of current efficiency:**

Step 1 — Mass of a substance liberated by charge $Q$:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

Step 2 — For charge $Q = I \cdot t$ and a stack of $n_{cp}$ cell pairs in series, the same $I \cdot t$ drives every pair. Define $m_{actual}$ as the measured mass of ash removed and $m_{theoretical}$ from Faraday's law.

Step 3 — Then:

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{actual} \cdot z \cdot F}{I \cdot t \cdot M}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1 — Ash removed per hour:

$$m_{actual} = 10\,000 \times 1.03 \times (0.008 - 0.0032) = 10\,000 \times 1.03 \times 0.0048 = 49.44 \text{ kg h}^{-1}$$

Step 2 — Theoretical mass that *one cell pair* can remove at $I = 250$ A and $\eta = 0.95$ in 1 hour:

$$m_{1,th} = \frac{250 \times 3600 \times 0.06}{1 \times 96\,485} = \frac{54\,000}{96\,485} = 0.5596 \text{ kg per cell pair per hour}$$

Step 3 — Actual removal per cell pair at 95 % efficiency:

$$m_{1,actual} = 0.5596 \times 0.95 = 0.5316 \text{ kg per cell pair per hour}$$

Step 4 — Number of cell pairs:

$$n_{cp} = \frac{49.44}{0.5316} = 93.0 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 93$$

**Final Answer: $n_{cp} \approx 93$ cell pairs** (or 95 if a safety margin is required).

*(Mark split: 2 derivation, 2 step-1 mass balance, 2 step-2/3 Faraday and efficiency, 1 final rounding.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "membrane" with "filter".** ED uses *ion-exchange* membranes, *not* microporous filters. Neutral molecules like lactose and protein pass freely.
> 2. **Forgetting the electrode reactions.** Always mention $\text{H}_2$ at cathode and $\text{O}_2$ at cathode-side (or anolyte oxidation); examiners deduct 1–2 marks if omitted.
> 3. **Reporting $\eta > 100$ % silently.** This indicates a calculation error in the Faraday step. Always check the order of magnitude: 250 A × 3600 s ≈ 9 × 10⁵ C, so one Faraday-cycle moves ~9.3 equivalents. Anything > 100 % is a red flag.
> 4. **Ignoring concentration polarization.** A complete 14-mark answer *must* discuss the limiting current density and why the plant runs at $i \approx 0.7 \, i_{lim}$.
> 5. **Unit mismatches in $W_{sp}$.** Common error: writing "kWh kg⁻¹" without converting J → kWh via $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ED is an electrochemical membrane process**, not a pressure-driven or concentration-driven one — the driving force is the **DC electric field**.
- Two membrane types only: **CEM** (fixed –) for cations, **AEM** (fixed +) for anions.
- The **diluate stream** is the *product* (demineralized whey / deacidified milk); the **concentrate stream** collects the removed ions.
- **Faraday's First Law** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$ is the foundation; memorize it verbatim with units.
- **Current efficiency $\eta$** is always between 0 and 1 (0 – 100 %); if your number exceeds 1, re-check.
- **Limiting current density** $i_{lim} = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$ — never operate above it; otherwise water-splitting destroys efficiency and membrane.
- **Specific energy consumption** $W_{sp} = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot U_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot M \cdot \eta}$ kWh kg⁻¹ — a key design figure.
- Industrial whey ED runs at **80 – 90 % current efficiency**, **0.5 – 1.5 V per cell pair**, and **< 40 °C** to avoid protein denaturation.
- **EDR (reversal)** variant fights fouling and is preferred for high-hardness whey feeds.
- Top **dairy applications**: DWP-90 for infant formula, deacidification of fermented milk, partial milk desalination.
- **Electrode reactions**: anode gives $\text{O}_2$, cathode gives $\text{H}_2$; anolyte-catholyte loop uses $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$.
- Stack design: **200 – 600 cell pairs** between two electrodes; segmented into 2–3 hydraulic stages.
- Common-sense check: more **membrane area** or more **cell pairs** → higher ash removal but higher voltage → check against $W_{sp}$ target.
- A clean **mermaid block diagram** of the cell pair, a correct **Faraday derivation**, and a **specific-energy numerical** together are the trifecta for a 14-mark full-score answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology of Traditional Indian Milk Products: Khoa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technology of Traditional Indian Milk Products: Khoa

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧀 MODULE 4 — TECHNOLOGY OF TRADITIONAL INDIAN MILK PRODUCTS: KHOA

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Khoa (also spelled Khoya or Mawa)** is a heat-desiccated, partially dehydrated, whole milk product obtained by continuous, vigorous boiling and scraping of standardized cow, buffalo, or mixed milk in a shallow open pan (kadhai) until the total solids (TS) concentration reaches approximately **65–70% (w/w)** and the moisture content drops to **25–35% (w/w)**. It represents the **base intermediate** for the manufacture of a wide spectrum of Indian sweets such as *burfi, peda, gulab jamun, kalakand, and laddoo*.

In the context of the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) Regulations, 2011** and the **Codex Alimentarius classification of heat-concentrated milk products**, khoa is categorized as a **partially concentrated/heat-desiccated milk product** that retains the natural fat, protein, lactose, and mineral matrix of the original milk in a plastic, moldable, semi-solid form.

### Standards of Identity (FSSAI, India)

| Parameter | Cow Milk Khoa | Buffalo Milk Khoa | Mixed Khoa |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Milk Solids (min) | 65.0% | 70.0% | 68.0% |
| Milk Fat (min) | 25.0% | 30.0% | 27.0% |
| Moisture (max) | 35.0% | 30.0% | 32.0% |
| Acidity (as lactic acid, max) | 0.6% | 0.6% | 0.6% |
| Coliform Count (max) | 90/g | 90/g | 90/g |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Paneer of Sweetness"**
> Think of khoa as the **"evaporated milk turned solid"** of Indian tradition. If you slowly simmer milk on a gas stove while constantly scraping the bottom to prevent scorching, the water (≈87% of milk) progressively evaporates. The remaining ≈13% solids (fat, casein, lactose) become so concentrated that they fuse into a thick, dough-like mass. This mass — that is khoa. **It is the Indian analogue of "dulce de leche"** of Latin America or the "tehina-based halva" of the Middle East, but uniquely characterized by its short, plastic texture and the **Maillard-driven nutty flavor** developed during prolonged open-pan boiling.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Khoa Based on Composition & Texture

Khoa is not a single uniform product — it is **graded commercially** into three distinct varieties, each with a specific end-use:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Commercial Grades of Khoa**

1. **Pindi Type** — Made primarily from cow milk. Lower fat (~20–25%), grainy & flaky texture, used for *peda, burfi, mathura-ka-peda*.
2. **Dhap Type** — Made from buffalo milk with deliberate addition of *Dhap* (stale whey/acid whey from previous batches). Soft, moist, sticky, used for *kalakand, milk-cake*.
3. **Danedar Type** — Granular, coarse, with deliberately incorporated acid (citric/lactic) or whey. Granular texture mimics *chhana*; used for *kalakand, kunda, gulab jamun-mix*.

### End-Use Mapping

$$\text{Milk (Whole)} \xrightarrow{\text{Boil + Scrape}} \text{Khoa} \xrightarrow{\text{+Sugar +Flavor}} \text{Sweets}$$

| Khoa Type | Typical End-Product |
|---|---|
| Pindi | Peda, Burfi, Sohan Halwa |
| Dhap | Kalakand, Milk Cake, Barfi (soft) |
| Danedar | Gulab Jamun, Kunda, Kalakand (granular) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Manufacturing Principle — Heat-Desiccation

Khoa-making is governed by a **simultaneous triple process** operating in series during a single batch operation:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Coupled Transport Phenomena of Khoa Manufacture**
> 1. **Heat Transfer** — From flame → karahi wall → milk → water molecule
> 2. **Mass Transfer** — Water vapor escapes the boiling surface (latent heat ~540 kcal/kg)
> 3. **Momentum Transfer** — Continuous scraping causes mechanical shearing, breaking the denatured protein film and exposing fresh surfaces

### 2.1.1 Why Buffalo Milk is Preferred

- Buffalo milk has **higher TS (16–17%)** and **higher fat (6–7%)** vs cow milk (**TS 12–13%, fat 3.5–4.5%**).
- Higher fat → more lubricating effect → smoother khoa.
- Buffalo casein is denser, giving a firmer body.
- Buffalo fat globules are larger and tend to coalesce during boiling — contributing to the characteristic glossy surface of *danedar*.

### 2.1.2 Step-Wise Process Logic (Bullet Breakdown)

1. **Selection of Raw Milk** — Fresh, sweet, free from antibiotics, neutralizers, or preservatives. Standardized to a pre-calculated **casein-to-fat ratio (CFR ≈ 0.7–0.8)**.
2. **Initial Boiling** — Milk is taken in a heavy-bottomed **brass or stainless-steel karahi (kadhai)** at the rate of **5–6 kg per m² of pan area** to avoid thin-layer scorching.
3. **Continuous Stirring & Scraping** — A flat-bottomed **wooden patila / iron scrapper** is used to continuously scrape the milk film forming on the pan surface. This:
   - Prevents localized burning.
   - Renews the evaporative surface.
   - Disperses fat uniformly.
4. **Acid/Sour-Whey Addition (for Danedar only)** — When the TS reaches ~50%, **2–3% (v/v) of sour whey or 0.05% citric acid solution** is added to cause controlled casein precipitation → granular texture.
5. **Final Stage — "Throwing Stage"** — At ~70% TS, the khoa mass leaves the bottom of the pan as a single cohesive lump on scraping. Operator experience judges the "throwing point" by:
   - **Visual cues** — Glossy sheen, color deepens to cream-yellow.
   - **Tactile cues** — Soft, plastic, non-sticky to fingers.
   - **Auditory cues** — Soft sizzling sound ceases (all free water gone).
6. **Cooling & Panning** — Hot khoa (80–85°C) is transferred to a cool marble slab, **flattened to 2–3 cm thickness**, and cooled to ~30°C before packaging.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Khoa Yield & Composition Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU numerical sections.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = M \times \dfrac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}}$ | **Standard Khoa Yield Equation** | kg khoa / kg milk |
| 2 | $Y = M \times \dfrac{100 - M_{k}}{100 - m_{k}}$ | Reformulated yield using milk TS and khoa moisture | kg khoa / kg milk |
| 3 | $R_{khoa} = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | **Process Recovery (%)** | % |
| 4 | $M_{k,adj} = \dfrac{F_{k} \times (100 - M_m)}{F_m \times 100}$ | Adjusted milk to hit target khoa fat (standardization) | kg milk |
| 5 | $TS_{khoa} = 100 - M_{khoa}$ | Total solids from moisture | % |
| 6 | $SNF_{milk} = \dfrac{TS_{milk} - F_{milk}}{100}$ | Solids-not-fat from gross composition | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{evap} = \dfrac{M_{water,evap}}{E_{input}}$ | Evaporation efficiency (thermal) | % |
| 8 | $L_{time} = \dfrac{(M_{in} - M_{out}) \times 540}{A \times h_{fg}}$ | Latent heat-based time estimate | min |

Where:
- $M$ = mass of milk (kg)
- $TS$ = total solids (%)
- $F$ = fat (%)
- $M_k$ = khoa moisture (%)
- $m_k$ = milk moisture (%)
- $A$ = pan surface area (m²)
- $h_{fg}$ = heat flux (kcal/m²/min)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for Yield Calculation**
> - $TS_{khoa}$ must lie in the range **65–70%** for legal compliance.
> - $M_{milk,std}$ must be standardized to **6.0% fat & 9.0% SNF (Legal milk in India)** or as per target khoa grade.
> - Recovery $R_{khoa}$ for traditional karahi is **15–17% from cow milk** and **20–22% from buffalo milk**.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Significance in Real-World Dairy Plants

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance**
> - Khoa is **India's largest heat-desiccated indigenous dairy product**, with an estimated **market size of 25,000+ tonnes/year** and a CAGR of **~8%**.
> - **Industrial continuous khoa-making machines** (e.g., scraped-surface heat exchangers, double-cone rollers, *Contherm* type) replace the artisan's karahi to improve:
>   - Recovery from 88% → 94%.
>   - Production rate from 5 kg/batch (4 h) → 200 kg/h continuous.
>   - Hygienic quality and shelf-life from 4 days → 21 days (refrigerated).
> - **Energy engineering**: 1 kg khoa requires evaporation of ~5.5 kg water → energy input of **2,970 kcal (≈ 3.5 kWh thermal)** per kg khoa.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Khoa Yield Formula (Standard KTU Numerical)

### Problem Setup

A dairy plant processes **1,000 kg of standardized buffalo milk** having **fat = 6.5%** and **SNF = 9.5%** into khoa with a target **moisture content of 28%**. Calculate:
1. Theoretical yield of khoa.
2. Percentage recovery if actual yield = 215 kg.
3. Quantity of water to be evaporated.

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Determine milk total solids.**

$$TS_{milk} = F_{milk} + SNF_{milk} = 6.5 + 9.5 = 16.0\%$$

*Logic*: Total Solids = Fat + Solids-Not-Fat (by mass balance on milk composition).

**Step 2 — Determine khoa total solids.**

$$TS_{khoa} = 100 - M_{khoa} = 100 - 28 = 72.0\%$$

*Logic*: Moisture and Total Solids are complementary; if moisture is 28%, solids are 72%.

**Step 3 — Apply the mass-balance yield equation.**

The general principle: *Solids entering = Solids leaving* (assuming no losses).

$$M_{milk} \times TS_{milk} = M_{khoa} \times TS_{khoa}$$

Solving for $M_{khoa}$:

$$M_{khoa} = M_{milk} \times \frac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{khoa} &= 1000 \times \frac{16.0}{72.0} \\[4pt]
M_{khoa} &= 1000 \times 0.2222 \\[4pt]
M_{khoa} &= 222.2 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: Every 1000 kg of milk gives 222.2 kg khoa — equivalent to 4.5 kg milk per kg khoa (the classic rule of thumb in Indian halwai practice).

**Step 5 — Calculate process recovery.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{khoa} &= \frac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
R_{khoa} &= \frac{215}{222.2} \times 100 \\[4pt]
R_{khoa} &= 96.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: Recovery < 100% because of unavoidable losses — burnt solids adhering to pan, splashing, fat leaching to scraper, and Maillard browning-induced mass loss (CO₂, H₂O from sugar-amine reactions).

**Step 6 — Calculate water to be evaporated.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{water,evap} &= M_{milk} - M_{khoa} \\[4pt]
M_{water,evap} &= 1000 - 215 \\[4pt]
M_{water,evap} &= 785 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic*: Mass in − mass out = water lost by evaporation. This is also the **design basis for the condenser** of any continuous khoa plant.

---

## 3.2 Standardization Derivation — Targeting a Specific Khoa Fat

**Problem:** A halwai wants to produce khoa of **30% fat** using milk of **4.2% fat and 8.8% SNF**. If he adds **cream of 50% fat**, what quantity of cream must be added to **100 kg of milk**?

**Step 1 — Pearson's Square Setup.**

Let $x$ = kg of cream per 100 kg milk. Mass balance on fat:

$$4.2 \times 100 + 50 \times x = (100 + x) \times F_{blend}$$

But the khoa fat depends on the **fat-to-TS ratio in the standardized milk**, since TS concentration during boiling preserves proportions. So target milk fat must be:

$$F_{milk,target} = F_{khoa} \times \frac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}}$$

**Step 2 — Set the khoa parameters.**

Target khoa: $F_k = 30\%$, $M_k = 28\%$, hence $TS_{khoa} = 72\%$.

Milk TS = $4.2 + 8.8 = 13.0\%$.

$$F_{milk,target} = 30 \times \frac{13.0}{72.0} = 5.42\%$$

**Step 3 — Pearson's Square (Cream–Skim Milk Mix).**

Assume skim milk has 0.1% fat. We blend:

- Cream: 50% fat
- Skim milk: 0.1% fat
- Target blend: 5.42% fat

**Pearson's Square:**

$$
\begin{array}{c}
\text{Cream } 50 \quad\searrow\quad\diagdown\quad \text{Blend } 5.42 \quad\nearrow \quad \text{Skim } 0.1 \\[4pt]
\text{Parts of cream} = 5.42 - 0.1 = 5.32 \\[4pt]
\text{Parts of skim} = 50 - 5.42 = 44.58
\end{array}
$$

Ratio Cream : Skim = 5.32 : 44.58.

**Step 4 — Calculate cream quantity for 100 kg milk.**

Let $y$ = kg cream added; then skim milk used = $y \times 44.58 / 5.32$. But the question asks for cream only:

$$\text{Cream fraction} = \frac{5.32}{5.32 + 44.58} = \frac{5.32}{49.90} = 0.1066$$

Hence for **100 kg raw milk**, blend requires:

- **Total blend target** = 100 + skim milk added (but we work per kg blend)
- Working directly: $y = 100 \times \dfrac{5.32}{44.58} = 11.93$ kg cream, and skim milk added = 100 kg (interpreted as base milk in this simple halwai context).

**Step 5 — Verify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{blend} &= \frac{(100 \times 4.2) + (11.93 \times 50)}{111.93} \\[4pt]
F_{blend} &= \frac{420 + 596.5}{111.93} = \frac{1016.5}{111.93} = 9.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Halwai Practice**: In actual halwai operations, the cream addition is gauged by the *khoa throwing test* — a small quantity of cream is added, a sample khoa prepared, fat tested by **Gerber method**, and corrected iteratively. The Pearson's Square is used by **industrial QC labs** for precise calculations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Khoa Quality Defects & Cures

| Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard, brittle texture** | Over-boiling, TS > 75% | Stop at throwing point; use steam-jacketed kettle |
| **Soapy / oily flavor** | Lipolysis — raw milk too old | Use only fresh milk; refrigerate raw milk < 4°C |
| **Smoky / burnt flavor** | Pan scorching due to thin milk layer | Maintain milk depth > 5 cm; stir constantly |
| **Bacterial spoilage, slime** | Post-process contamination | Sanitize hands, slab, knives; refrigerate ≤ 4°C |
| **Surface mold (white/green)** | High moisture + warm storage | Lower moisture to 26–28%; cold chain |
| **Grainy, sand-like (Pindi grade needed but got Danedar)** | Excessive acid addition | Reduce whey/citric acid addition rate |
| **Pale, washed-out color** | Cow milk + low-fat; over-clarification | Use buffalo milk or blend for richer color |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Traditional Khoa Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Filtration through muslin cloth]
    B --> C[Standardization Fat and SNF adjustment]
    C --> D[Pre-boiling in open karahi 5 to 6 kg per m2]
    D --> E[Continuous boiling and scraping stage 1, TS 20 to 40%]
    E --> F{Type of Khoa needed?}
    F -->|Pindi| G[Continue scraping, no acid, TS 65%]
    F -->|Dhap| H[Add 2 to 3 percent sour whey, TS 55%]
    F -->|Danedar| I[Add citric acid 0.05 percent, TS 50%]
    G --> J[Throwing point detection, TS 68 to 70%]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Transfer to cooling marble slab]
    K --> L[Flatten to 2 to 3 cm thickness]
    L --> M[Cooling to 30 degrees C]
    M --> N[Cutting into 250 g blocks]
    N --> O[Packaging in parchment / PP pouches]
    O --> P[Storage at 4 degrees C, shelf life 4 to 7 days]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Industrial Continuous Khoa Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW[Raw Milk Section]
        R1[Receiving Dock] --> R2[Weighing Tank] --> R3[Plate Cooler, 4 degrees C]
    end
    subgraph STD[Standardization Section]
        R3 --> S1[Separator] --> S2[Standardizing Tank with CFR Control]
    end
    subgraph MFG[Continuous Khoa Section]
        S2 --> M1[Balance Tank] --> M2[Pre-heater, 80 degrees C]
        M2 --> M3[Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger SSHE, 105 degrees C]
        M3 --> M4[Contherm Votator, twin-screw]
        M4 --> M5[Discharge Screw, TS 70%]
    end
    subgraph PKG[Finishing Section]
        M5 --> P1[Cooling Conveyor] --> P2[Slab Moulder]
        P2 --> P3[Cutting Station] --> P4[Vacuum Packager]
    end
    subgraph QC[Quality Control Loop]
        P4 --> Q1[Moisture, Fat, Acidity Tests] --> P5[Accept or Reject]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mass-Balance Sankey Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[1000 kg Buffalo Milk, TS 16%]
        direction TB
        I1[Water 840 kg]
        I2[Fat 65 kg]
        I3[SNF 95 kg]
    end
    IN --> EVAP[Boiling and Scraping]
    EVAP --> OUT[222.2 kg Khoa, TS 72%]
    EVAP --> LOSS[777.8 kg Water Vapor + minor losses]
    OUT --> O1[Fat 66.66 kg, reconcentrated]
    OUT --> O2[SNF 95.32 kg, retained]
    OUT --> O3[Moisture 62.22 kg, residual]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define khoa. State the FSSAI compositional standards for khoa prepared from cow and buffalo milk.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Khoa is a heat-desiccated, partially concentrated whole milk product prepared by evaporating moisture from standardized cow, buffalo, or mixed milk in an open pan with continuous stirring and scraping until a semi-solid, plastic mass develops, with moisture not exceeding 30–35%.

**FSSAI Standards:**

| Parameter | Cow Khoa | Buffalo Khoa |
|---|---|---|
| Total Solids (min) | 65.0% | 70.0% |
| Milk Fat (min) | 25.0% | 30.0% |
| Moisture (max) | 35.0% | 30.0% |
| Acidity as lactic acid (max) | 0.6% | 0.6% |

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the standard table.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Differentiate between Pindi, Dhap, and Danedar types of khoa.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pindi | Dhap | Danedar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Milk used | Cow | Buffalo | Cow/Buffalo |
| Texture | Smooth, granular, flaky | Soft, moist, sticky | Coarse, granular |
| Acid addition | None | Sour whey (2–3%) | Citric acid (0.05–0.1%) |
| End-use | Peda, Burfi | Kalakand, Milk Cake | Gulab Jamun Mix, Kunda |
| Moisture | 28–32% | 32–35% | 30–33% |

(½ mark for the table + ½ mark for clear end-use identification = 3 marks.)

---

## 📝 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the traditional method of khoa preparation from buffalo milk. Add a neat flow diagram and discuss the principle of heat and mass transfer involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 1,500 kg of cow milk with 4.5% fat and 8.7% SNF is to be processed into khoa with a final moisture of 30%. Calculate the theoretical yield, process recovery if actual yield is 235 kg, and the water to be evaporated. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Raw material selection (1 mark):**
Fresh cow or buffalo milk, free from neutralizers, antibiotics, and preservatives, is selected. Cow milk is standardized to ~4.5% fat and 8.5% SNF; buffalo milk used as such (~6.5% fat, 9.0% SNF).

**Step 2 — Pre-boiling (1 mark):**
5–6 kg milk per m² of pan area is poured into a clean, heavy-bottomed brass or stainless-steel karahi. The pan is mounted on a brick furnace with wood/coke/LPG fire.

**Step 3 — Continuous boiling and scraping (2 marks):**
As milk boils, a wooden *patila* (stirrer) or iron scrapper continuously scrapes the milk film from the pan surface. This serves three purposes:
- Prevents scorching.
- Renews the evaporative surface.
- Disperses fat uniformly and breaks denatured protein layers.

**Step 4 — Heat & mass transfer principle (2 marks):**
Heat flows from flame → karahi wall → milk → bulk boiling at 100–105°C. Latent heat of vaporization (~540 kcal/kg) drives the **mass transfer of water** from the milk to the atmosphere. Approximately 5–6 kg water is evaporated per kg khoa produced. Continuous scraping maintains the **thermal boundary layer** thin, maximizing the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ (typically 1,500–3,000 W/m²K for an open karahi).

**Step 5 — Throwing point and finishing (1 mark):**
At ~70% TS, the khoa leaves the pan as a single mass on scraping — the *throwing point*. The mass is transferred to a marble slab, cooled to 30°C, cut into 250 g blocks, and packaged.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Calculate milk TS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TS_{milk} &= F_{milk} + SNF_{milk} = 4.5 + 8.7 = 13.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate khoa TS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TS_{khoa} &= 100 - 30 = 70.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply mass balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{khoa} &= M_{milk} \times \frac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}} = 1500 \times \frac{13.2}{70.0} \\[4pt]
M_{khoa} &= 1500 \times 0.1886 = 282.86 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recovery calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{khoa} &= \frac{235}{282.86} \times 100 = 83.09\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Water evaporated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{water} &= 1500 - 235 = 1265 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical yield = **282.86 kg**; Recovery = **83.09%**; Water evaporated = **1,265 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert moisture to TS** — Most common error. Always write $TS_{khoa} = 100 - M_{khoa}$ before plugging in.
> 2. **Plugging in fat % alone as TS** — TS = Fat + SNF. Do not mix up.
> 3. **Recovery formula inversion** — It is *actual/theoretical × 100*, not the reverse. Recovery > 100% is physically impossible and signals a calculation mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final line** — KTU award partial marks only when numerical magnitudes are correct; always state the unit (kg, %).
> 5. **Ignoring the FSSAI minimum TS constraint** — In real exam answers, comment on whether the computed khoa meets the FSSAI 65% (cow) or 70% (buffalo) minimum.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** List and explain the three commercial grades of khoa. Discuss the role of acid/whey addition in the manufacture of *danedar* and *dhap* varieties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A halwai receives milk testing 4.0% fat and 8.6% SNF. He wishes to produce khoa with 26% fat and 28% moisture. Using Pearson's Square, determine the quantity of cream (50% fat) to be added to 100 kg of this milk to achieve the target. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Three grades of khoa (3 marks):**

1. **Pindi** — Prepared from cow milk; smooth, flaky, slightly granular texture; no acid addition. Used for *mathura-peda, burfi, sohan-halwa*.
2. **Dhap** — Made from buffalo milk; soft, sticky, high-moisture product; prepared by deliberate addition of *dhap* (stale, sour whey from previous batches, 2–3% v/v) which imparts characteristic softness and slight acidity. Used for *kalakand, milk-cake*.
3. **Danedar** — Coarse, granular texture mimicking *chhana*; prepared by controlled addition of food-grade acid (citric or lactic, 0.05–0.1%) or sour whey when TS reaches ~50%. The acid precipitates casein in coarse aggregates, giving the characteristic granularity. Used for *gulab jamun-mix, kunda, kalakand*.

**Role of acid/whey (4 marks):**

- **Acid addition lowers milk pH** from 6.6 → ~5.5, which is close to the isoelectric point of casein (pH 4.6). This causes **partial casein coagulation**, forming discrete curd granules dispersed in the fat-serum phase.
- These granules survive the boiling process, providing the **characteristic grainy texture** of danedar khoa.
- In dhap khoa, the sour whey provides lactic acid bacteria and acidity simultaneously, leading to a softer body and a slight tangy note preferred in milk-cake and kalakand.
- Excessive acid (pH < 5.0) → hard, crumbly khoa; inadequate acid → smooth, paste-like khoa indistinguishable from pindi.
- **Standardization of acid addition is therefore critical** for grade identification and consumer acceptance.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute milk TS and target milk fat:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TS_{milk} &= 4.0 + 8.6 = 12.6\% \\[4pt]
F_{milk,target} &= F_{khoa} \times \frac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}} = 26 \times \frac{12.6}{72} = 4.55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pearson's Square (Cream + Skim Milk → Blend):**

Assume skim milk (SNF source) has 0.1% fat.

- Cream: 50% fat
- Skim: 0.1% fat
- Target: 4.55% fat

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parts of cream} &= 4.55 - 0.1 = 4.45 \\[4pt]
\text{Parts of skim milk} &= 50 - 4.55 = 45.45 \\[4pt]
\text{Ratio cream : skim} &= 4.45 : 45.45 = 1 : 10.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cream quantity for 100 kg base milk:**

If 100 kg of original milk (4.0% fat) is the base, we need to add cream. Solving the fat balance with $x$ kg cream:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(100 \times 4.0) + (x \times 50) &= (100 + x) \times 4.55 \\[4pt]
400 + 50x &= 455 + 4.55x \\[4pt]
45.45x &= 55 \\[4pt]
x &= 1.21 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Add **1.21 kg of cream (50% fat)** to 100 kg of base milk, plus make up SNF with skim-milk powder or condensed skim if needed to maintain the desired 8.6% SNF.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Khoa — The 1-Page Last-Minute Revision**
> - **Definition**: Heat-desiccated whole milk product; moisture ≤ 30–35%; TS 65–70%.
> - **Three Grades**: Pindi (cow, smooth, for peda) | Dhap (buffalo + whey, soft, for kalakand) | Danedar (acid-added, granular, for gulab jamun).
> - **FSSAI minimums**: Cow khoa fat 25%, TS 65%; Buffalo khoa fat 30%, TS 70%.
> - **Raw milk preference**: Buffalo > Cow for yield & color.
> - **Equipment**: Karahi (traditional) / Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (industrial).
> - **Process**: Boil → Scrape → Acidify (optional) → Throw point (~70% TS) → Cool on slab → Cut → Pack.
> - **Throwing point cues**: Visual gloss + tactile plastic feel + cessation of sizzling.
> - **Key formula (memorize)**:
>   $$M_{khoa} = M_{milk} \times \frac{TS_{milk}}{TS_{khoa}}$$
> - **Recovery benchmark**: Traditional 88–92% | Industrial 94–96%.
> - **Defects**: Hardness (over-cook), oiliness (lipolysis), smoky (scorch), mold (high moisture + warm storage).
> - **Packaging**: Parchment paper / PP pouches; refrigerated storage (≤ 4°C); shelf life 4–7 days fresh, 21 days vacuum-packed.
> - **Energy fact**: ~3.5 kWh thermal energy per kg khoa produced.
> - **Industrial yield rule**: ~5 kg milk → 1 kg khoa (cow); ~4.5 kg milk → 1 kg khoa (buffalo).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Final-Word Examiner Tips**
> 1. Always draw a **flow diagram** in any khoa manufacturing question — it is worth **1–2 marks** minimum even if your text is incomplete.
> 2. In numericals, write down the **assumed TS / moisture values** at each step; don't jump to the calculator.
> 3. Use the phrase *"throwing point"* explicitly when describing the endpoint of khoa manufacture — it is a **board-exam keyword**.
> 4. For Pearson's Square questions, **draw the square** on the answer script — partial marks are awarded even if arithmetic fails.
> 5. Cite **FSSAI 2011 / Codex standards** wherever possible — it signals depth of preparation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Channa and Butter Manufacture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Channa and Butter Manufacture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Channa (Paneer) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Channa (also called Paneer)** is a **traditional Indian heat-acid coagulated dairy product** obtained by the precipitation of casein from standardized, heat-treated buffalo or cow milk using permitted food-grade organic acids (citric acid, lactic acid, or vinegar) or by natural souring (fermentation with lactic acid bacteria). It is a soft, unripened, non-melting cheese variety with a moisture content of **~50–70%** and a fat content of **~50% on dry matter basis** (for whole milk channa).

**As per FSSAI standards (Food Safety and Standards Regulations):**
- Channa/Paneer shall contain **not less than 50% milk fat** on the dry matter basis.
- Moisture content shall **not exceed 70%** of the total mass.
- It shall be free from starch, added color, and preservatives (other than permitted ones).

> [!NOTE]
> **Channa vs. Paneer:** The two terms are functionally synonymous in dairy processing, though "Channa" is more commonly used in Eastern India (Bengal, Odisha) for the granular, drier variant, while "Paneer" refers to the firm, sliceable variant popular in North India. The processing technology is identical.

## 1.2 Butter – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Butter** is a **water-in-oil emulsion** (where fat globules form the continuous phase and moisture droplets form the dispersed phase) derived exclusively from milk, cream, or curd, with or without the addition of common salt (NaCl) and permitted starter cultures. It must contain **not less than 80% milk fat, not more than 16% moisture, and not more than 2% curd (SNF).**

**FSSAI Compositional Standards for Table Butter:**
| Parameter | Limit |
| :--- | :--- |
| Milk fat | **≥ 80% (w/w)** |
| Moisture | **≤ 16% (w/w)** |
| Curd (SNF) | **≤ 2.0% (w/w)** |
| Common salt (NaCl) | **≤ 2.5% (w/w)** (optional) |
| Peroxide value (PV) | **≤ 1.0 meq O₂/kg fat** |
| Free fatty acids (as oleic) | **≤ 0.4%** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy for Channa:** Imagine boiling milk in a pot and slowly squeezing a lemon into it — you see white fluffy curds floating in greenish-yellow whey. Those curds, when drained and pressed into a block, are essentially Channa. The acid (lemon juice) acts like a "key" that unlocks the casein proteins (the structural scaffold of milk), causing them to clump together. The heat accelerates this clumping and expels more water, giving Channa its characteristic firm texture.

**Analogy for Butter:** Picture a salad dressing where oil and vinegar don't mix. Now imagine shaking cream violently — the mechanical agitation shatters the protective fat globule membranes. The fat globules then clump together (a process called *destabilization of the emulsion*), trapping moisture and air. What you get is a yellow, semi-solid mass with a watery byproduct (buttermilk) — that is butter. Salt is added to enhance flavor, control microbial growth, and extend shelf life.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **Channa = Acid + Heat + Drain** ; **Butter = Cream + Agitation + Destabilize**

## 1.4 Significance in the Indian Dairy Industry

- India is the **largest producer of milk globally** (~230+ million tonnes/year), and Channa/Paneer + Butter together represent a massive **value-added dairy segment** in the organized dairy sector.
- Channa is the **base ingredient** for sweets like *Rasogulla, Sandesh, Rasmalai, Chum Chum, and Cham Cham*, which form the backbone of the Indian confectionery industry.
- Butter is the foundation for **ghee, table butter, white butter, salted butter, and unsalted butter** products.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Channa Manufacture – Complete Process Flow

### Step 1: Raw Milk Reception and Standardization
- Buffalo milk (preferred for richer channa) or cow milk is received and tested.
- **Standardization** is done to achieve a desired **Fat:SNF ratio of 1:0.7** for cow milk and **1:0.65** for buffalo milk, ensuring optimum channa quality.
- The milk is typically standardized to **3.5–5.0% fat** and **8.5–9.0% SNF**.

### Step 2: Heat Treatment (Pasteurization)
- The standardized milk is heated to **85–90°C for 5–10 minutes** (or HTST: 90°C for 1 min).
- **Why heat?** Heating:
  - Denatures whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin), which co-precipitate with casein → **increases yield by 8–10%**.
  - Improves texture (firmer body).
  - Kills pathogens and extends shelf life.

### Step 3: Cooling to Coagulation Temperature
- Milk is cooled to **70–75°C** (optimum for acid coagulation).
- Too low temperature → soft, fragile curd; Too high → brittle, rubbery curd.

### Step 4: Acid Coagulation
- Food-grade **citric acid solution (1–2%)** or **lactic acid (0.5–1.0%)** is added slowly with gentle stirring until the pH drops to **4.6 (isoelectric point of casein)**.
- The clear **greenish whey** separates from the white curd.

### Step 5: Straining and Pressing
- The curd is drained through a muslin cloth/cheese cloth to remove whey.
- Then pressed at **~1–1.5 kg/cm²** for 1–2 hours to remove excess moisture.
- Final moisture target: **~55–65%**.

### Step 6: Cooling, Packaging, and Storage
- Channa blocks are cooled to **4–7°C**, cut, vacuum-packed, and stored under refrigeration (shelf life: **7–10 days** at 4°C).

## 2.2 Butter Manufacture – Complete Process Flow

### Step 1: Cream Separation
- Whole milk is passed through a **mechanical cream separator (centrifugal)** to separate skim milk (0.05% fat) from cream (35–45% fat).
- *Gravity method* (older) → inefficient; *Centrifugal method* (modern) → rapid and hygienic.

### Step 2: Standardization of Cream
- Cream is standardized to **35–40% fat** for table butter and **38–42%** for salted butter.
- Lower fat → softer butter; Higher fat → greasy, oily texture.

### Step 3: Pasteurization of Cream
- Cream is pasteurized at **72°C for 15 sec (HTST)** or **80°C for 15 min (LTLT)**.
- **Vacreator** treatment (95–110°C) is used industrially to inactivate lipases and produce a "sweet" butter with no off-flavors.

### Step 4: Cooling and Aging (Maturation) of Cream
- Cream is cooled to **4–5°C** and aged for **12–16 hours (overnight)**.
- **Why age?** Aging:
  - Crystallizes milk fat into mixed crystals (β′ form) → improves body and texture.
  - Allows fat globule membrane proteins to equilibrate → better churning efficiency.

### Step 5: Churning (Agitation)
- Aged cream is churned in a **batch churn (traditional) or continuous churn (modern Fritz/Eskilstuna type)** at **8–12°C** until phase inversion occurs.
- Phase inversion: **water-in-oil emulsion → oil-in-water (butter) + water-in-oil (buttermilk) phases separate**.
- Churning time: **30–45 minutes** (batch) or **seconds** (continuous).

### Step 6: Washing of Butter Grains
- The butter grains are washed 1–2 times with cold potable water (5–10°C) to remove residual buttermilk, lactose, and curd particles.
- Reduces microbial load and improves keeping quality.

### Step 7: Salting (Optional)
- Common salt (NaCl) is added at **1.5–2.5%** of butter weight.
- Salt enhances flavor, controls microbial growth, and acts as a mild preservative.

### Step 8: Working (Kneading/Paddling)
- Butter is worked in a **butter worker** to uniformly distribute moisture as fine droplets (≤10 µm), preventing microbial proliferation and giving uniform texture.

### Step 9: Packaging and Storage
- Butter is packed in **aluminum foil, parchment paper, or LDPE pouches** (250g, 500g standard packs).
- Stored at **-10°C to -25°C** for long-term storage or **4–5°C** for short-term.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD and frequently appear in KTU University Examinations under Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Y_{channa} = \dfrac{(F_m \times R_f + SNF_m \times R_s)}{100} \times W_m$ | Channa yield from milk | kg | — |
| 2 | $Y_{channa} = \dfrac{(0.94 F_m + 0.42 SNF_m) \times W_m}{100}$ | Empirical yield (94% fat, 42% SNF recovery) | kg | — |
| 3 | $Y_{butter} = \dfrac{F_m \times R_b \times W_m}{F_b \times 100}$ | Butter yield from whole milk | kg | — |
| 4 | $Y_{butter} = \dfrac{F_c \times R_b \times W_c}{F_b \times 100}$ | Butter yield from cream | kg | — |
| 5 | $M_{butter} = \dfrac{F_b}{S_{TS}} \times 100$ | Standardization of fat in butter | % | $\geq 80$ |
| 6 | $pH_{coag} = pI_{casein} = 4.6$ | Isoelectric precipitation of casein | — | 4.6 |
| 7 | $E_{churn} = \dfrac{V_{butter}}{V_{cream} \times t} \times 100$ | Churning efficiency | % | 70–85% |
| 8 | $W_{acid} = \dfrac{(0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \times V_m}{100}$ | Required acid volume | L | 0.5–1.0% |

**Where:**
- $F_m$ = Fat % in milk, $SNF_m$ = SNF % in milk, $W_m$ = Weight of milk (kg)
- $R_f$ = Fat recovery (~0.94), $R_s$ = SNF recovery (~0.42) in channa
- $F_b$ = Fat % in butter (typically 80%), $R_b$ = Fat recovery in butter (~0.95)
- $F_c$ = Fat % in cream, $W_c$ = Weight of cream
- $V_{butter}$ = Volume of butter obtained, $t$ = churning time

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real-World Engineering

- **Process economics:** Yield formulas allow plant managers to estimate **production capacity**, raw milk requirements, and **revenue forecasting** in commercial dairies.
- **Quality control:** The 80% fat, 16% moisture, 2% curd composition of butter is legally enforced. Any deviation triggers **regulatory action** and **product recall**.
- **Mass balance:** Knowing fat and SNF distribution helps engineers design **CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems** and **effluent treatment plants** for whey and buttermilk discharge.
- **Standardization:** The Pearson's Square method (used to standardize milk/cream) relies on these formulas to compute exact proportions of whole milk and skim milk.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Channa Yield from Standardized Milk

**Problem Setup:** 100 kg of cow milk standardized to 4.0% fat and 8.5% SNF is used for channa manufacture. Given that fat recovery in channa = **94%** and SNF recovery = **42%**, calculate the expected channa yield (in kg).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** $R_f$ = fraction of fat transferred from milk to channa; $R_s$ = fraction of SNF transferred from milk to channa. These are empirical constants determined experimentally.

### Step 1: Write the General Yield Formula

The yield of channa is the **sum of recovered fat + recovered SNF + recovered minerals + recovered lactose** (in a generalized mass balance):

$$
Y_{channa} = W_{fat,recovered} + W_{SNF,recovered} + W_{minerals} + W_{lactose}
$$

For industrial calculations, this is simplified to a **two-component model (fat + SNF)** because the recovery of minerals and lactose is bundled into the SNF term:

$$
Y_{channa} = W_{fat,recovered} + W_{SNF,recovered}
$$

### Step 2: Express Recovered Mass in Terms of Milk Composition

$$
W_{fat,recovered} = \frac{F_m \times W_m \times R_f}{100}
$$

$$
W_{SNF,recovered} = \frac{SNF_m \times W_m \times R_s}{100}
$$

**Explanation:** $F_m \times W_m / 100$ gives the **total fat mass in milk (kg)**. Multiplying by recovery fraction $R_f$ gives the fat that actually ends up in channa. Same logic for SNF.

### Step 3: Combine into the Master Formula

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{F_m \times W_m \times R_f}{100} + \frac{SNF_m \times W_m \times R_s}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{W_m \times (F_m \times R_f + SNF_m \times R_s)}{100}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Standard Recovery Constants

Using $R_f = 0.94$ and $R_s = 0.42$:

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{W_m \times (0.94 \times F_m + 0.42 \times SNF_m)}{100}
$$

### Step 5: Plug in Numerical Values

Given: $W_m = 100 \text{ kg}, F_m = 4.0\%, SNF_m = 8.5\%$

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{100 \times (0.94 \times 4.0 + 0.42 \times 8.5)}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = (0.94 \times 4.0) + (0.42 \times 8.5)
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = 3.76 + 3.57
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = 7.33 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 6: Final Result with Moisture Check

The channa obtained has a moisture content of ~60% (after pressing). Hence:

$$
Y_{dry\ matter} = Y_{channa} \times \frac{(100 - M_{channa})}{100} = 7.33 \times 0.4 = 2.93 \text{ kg TS}
$$

**Verification:** Fat on dry matter = $(3.76 / 7.33) \times 100 = 51.3\%$. Since FSSAI requires ≥50% fat on dry matter, **the channa meets the standard** ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Butter Yield from Cream

**Problem Setup:** 50 kg of cream standardized to 40% fat is churned into butter. Fat recovery in butter churning = 95%. Butter contains 82% fat, 16% moisture, 2% curd. Calculate butter yield.

### Step 1: Mass Balance on Fat

The fat recovered in butter must equal 82% of the butter mass (since butter is 82% fat):

$$
W_{fat,butter} = 0.82 \times Y_{butter}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Fat Recovery from Cream

The fat recovered in butter is 95% of the fat in cream:

$$
W_{fat,butter} = 0.95 \times \frac{F_c \times W_c}{100}
$$

### Step 3: Equate and Solve for Yield

$$
0.82 \times Y_{butter} = 0.95 \times \frac{40 \times 50}{100}
$$

$$
0.82 \times Y_{butter} = 0.95 \times 20
$$

$$
0.82 \times Y_{butter} = 19.0
$$

$$
Y_{butter} = \frac{19.0}{0.82}
$$

$$
Y_{butter} = 23.17 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4: Verify Composition

Moisture in butter = 16% of 23.17 = **3.71 kg** (within standard). Salt addition (~2%) → 23.17 × 0.02 = **0.46 kg NaCl**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pearson's Square Standardization of Cream for Butter

**Problem Setup:** Cream of 45% fat is to be standardized to 40% fat using skim milk (0.05% fat). Find the proportion of each.

**Pearson's Square Setup:**

- Center: **40** (desired fat %)
- Top-left: **45** (high-fat component)
- Bottom-left: **0.05** (low-fat component, i.e., skim milk)
- Diagonal differences:
  - Top right: $45 - 40 = 5$ (parts of skim milk)
  - Bottom right: $40 - 0.05 = 39.95$ (parts of cream)

$$
\text{Proportion of cream} = \frac{5}{5 + 39.95} = \frac{5}{44.95} = 0.1112 \rightarrow 11.12\%
$$

$$
\text{Proportion of skim milk} = \frac{39.95}{44.95} = 0.8888 \rightarrow 88.88\%
$$

**For 100 kg of standardized cream:** 11.12 kg of 45% cream + 88.88 kg of skim milk.

**Verification:** Fat mass = $(11.12 \times 0.45) + (88.88 \times 0.0005) = 5.00 + 0.0444 = 5.0444 \text{ kg}$, which is 5.0444% of 100 kg = **5.04%**... 

> **Correction:** Recompute as: $(11.12 \times 45 + 88.88 \times 0.05) / 100 = (500.4 + 4.444) / 100 = 5.04\%$ (this is wrong). The Pearson's square should yield a 40% mix. The discrepancy arose from the small denominator; the correct interpretation is that the **ratios** (5:39.95) mean for every 5 parts of skim milk, you use 39.95 parts of cream. Total parts = 44.95, hence 5/44.95 × 100 = **11.12% skim milk** and **88.88% cream** — which is incorrect because the cream (45% fat) should dominate. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrected Pearson's Square:** When standardizing **downward** (from 45% to 40%), the **larger quantity is the 45% cream** (the higher one) and the **smaller quantity is the skim milk**. Diagonal: $|45 - 40| = 5$ parts of skim milk; $|40 - 0.05| = 39.95$ parts of cream. So for 100 kg of mixture: cream = 39.95/44.95 × 100 = **88.88 kg**; skim milk = 5/44.95 × 100 = **11.12 kg**. Verification: $(88.88 \times 0.45 + 11.12 \times 0.0005) / 100 = (39.996 + 0.00556)/100 = 0.4000 = 40\%$ ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Channa vs. Butter

| Parameter | Channa (Paneer) | Butter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type of product** | Acid-coagulated cheese | Fat-in-water emulsion → water-in-fat |
| **Starting material** | Whole milk | Cream (separated) |
| **Key process step** | Acid coagulation + heating | Churning/agitation |
| **Temperature** | 70–90°C (hot) | 8–12°C (cold) |
| **Coagulating agent** | Citric acid / Lactic acid / Starter | Mechanical + Thermal |
| **Byproduct** | Whey (acidic, pH ~4.6) | Buttermilk |
| **Moisture content** | 50–70% | ≤ 16% |
| **Fat content** | ≥ 50% on dry basis | ≥ 80% (as is) |
| **Shelf life (4°C)** | 7–10 days | 2–3 weeks |
| **Yield from 100 kg milk** | ~7–9 kg | ~4.5–5.5 kg |
| **pH of product** | 5.4–5.8 | 6.0–6.5 |
| **Texture** | Soft, sliceable | Plastic, semi-solid |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    L --> M[Cutting and Weighing]
    M --> N[Vacuum Packaging]
    N --> O[Cold Storage 4 C Shelf life 7 to 10 days]
    J --> P[Whey Utilization]
```

**Node Function Key:**
- **A–D:** Pre-processing and standardization zone

- **I–K:** Dewatering and shaping zone

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> F[Pasteurization HTST 72 C 15 sec]
    F --> G[Vacuum Deaeration Optional]

    H --> I[Aging 12 to 16 hours]

```

**Node Function Key:**
- **A–C:** Cream separation zone (centrifugal)
- **E–G:** Standardization and pasteurization zone
