# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Channa. List any four factors affecting the yield of Channa.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Channa is a traditional Indian dairy product obtained by acid coagulation of heated standardized milk followed by drainage of whey. It is a soft, unripened cheese with ≥50% milk fat on dry matter basis (FSSAI standard).

**Four Factors Affecting Channa Yield:**
1. **Fat and SNF content** of raw milk — higher fat & SNF → higher yield.
2. **Type of acid** used (citric acid gives lower yield than lactic acid due to stronger curd breakdown).
3. **Temperature of coagulation** — 70–75°C is optimum; lower temperatures give soft curd with more fines loss.
4. **Strength of acid** (concentration) — dilute acid gives better yield, less fines.
5. **Quality of milk** — type of milk (cow vs buffalo), freshness, mastitis status.
6. **Pressing time and pressure** — affects moisture retention.

### Question 2: What is churning? List the three main changes that occur in cream during churning.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Churning:** Churning is the process of **violent agitation of aged cream** in a butter churn (batch or continuous) at low temperature (8–12°C) to cause **phase inversion of the fat-in-water emulsion** and form butter grains, with buttermilk as the byproduct.

**Three Main Changes During Churning:**
1. **Destabilization of the fat globule membrane (FGM):** Mechanical shear disrupts the protein-phospholipid membrane surrounding each fat globule.
2. **Clumping and aggregation of fat globules:** The exposed liquid fat (released by partial crystallization during aging) causes globules to coalesce into butter grains.
3. **Phase inversion:** The emulsion flips from fat-in-water (cream) to water-in-fat (butter), releasing buttermilk.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) – Internal Choice 1

### Question A (14 Marks): Channa Manufacture and Yield Calculation

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) Describe the complete manufacturing process of Channa (Paneer) from standardized milk with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of heat treatment in determining Channa yield and quality.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) 200 kg of cow milk containing 3.8% fat and 8.4% SNF is used for Channa manufacture. The fat recovery is 95% and SNF recovery is 40%. If the channa obtained has 60% moisture, determine:**
**(i) The yield of fresh channa in kg**
**(ii) The fat content of channa on dry matter basis**
**(iii) Whether the channa meets FSSAI standards (≥ 50% fat on dry matter)** **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Process Flow Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block for the architecture.)

**Step-by-Step Description:**

1. **Reception and Standardization:** Milk is received, tested, and standardized to 3.5–5.0% fat and 8.5–9.0% SNF.

2. **Heat Treatment:** Milk is heated to 85–90°C for 5–10 minutes (or 95°C × 5 min for industrial).

   **Role of Heat Treatment:**
   - Denatures whey proteins (especially β-lactoglobulin), which then complex with κ-casein via disulfide bonds.
   - This protein–protein complex co-precipitates with casein, **increasing channa yield by 8–10%**.
   - Improves body/texture and creates a smoother, less granular curd.
   - Destroys pathogenic microorganisms and most vegetative cells → extends shelf life.
   - Inactivates native milk enzymes (plasmin, lipases) → prevents off-flavors.

3. **Cooling to 70–75°C:** The hot milk is cooled to the optimum coagulation temperature. Too hot → hard, brittle curd; too cold → soft, fragile curd with high fines loss.

4. **Acid Addition:** Food-grade citric acid (1–2%) or lactic acid (0.5–1.0%) is added with gentle stirring until clear whey separates and pH reaches 4.6 (isoelectric point of casein).

5. **Straining:** The curd is separated from whey using a muslin cloth.

6. **Pressing:** The curd is pressed at 1–1.5 kg/cm² for 1–2 hours to remove moisture. Target moisture: ~55–65%.

7. **Cooling and Packaging:** Channa is cooled to 4–7°C, cut into blocks, vacuum-packed, and stored at refrigeration temperature.

### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Given:**
- $W_m = 200 \text{ kg}$
- $F_m = 3.8\%$
- $SNF_m = 8.4\%$
- $R_f = 0.95$, $R_s = 0.40$
- Moisture in channa = 60% → TS = 40%

**Step 1: Apply the yield formula:**

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{W_m \times (F_m \times R_f + SNF_m \times R_s)}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{200 \times (3.8 \times 0.95 + 8.4 \times 0.40)}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{200 \times (3.61 + 3.36)}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = \frac{200 \times 6.97}{100}
$$

$$
Y_{channa} = 13.94 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Fat content on dry matter basis:**

$$
\text{Fat on DM} = \frac{W_{fat,recovered}}{Y_{channa}} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{channa}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Fat on DM} = \frac{3.61}{13.94} \times \frac{100}{40} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Fat on DM} = 0.2589 \times 2.5 \times 100 = 64.74\%
$$

**Step 3: FSSAI compliance check:**

Since 64.74% > 50% (FSSAI minimum), **the channa meets the FSSAI standard** ✓.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick calculation shortcut:** Fat on DM (%) = (W_fat_recovered / W_TS_recovered) × 100 = (3.61 / (13.94 × 0.40)) × 100 = (3.61 / 5.576) × 100 = 64.74%.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice 2

### Question B (14 Marks): Butter Manufacture and Process Engineering

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) Explain the role of cream aging in butter manufacture. List the standard compositional requirements for table butter as per FSSAI.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) 1000 kg of whole milk containing 4.0% fat is separated into cream (45% fat) and skim milk (0.05% fat). The cream is then standardized to 40% fat using skim milk and churned into butter (80% fat). The fat recovery in butter is 95%. Calculate:**
**(i) The mass of cream and skim milk obtained from initial separation**
**(ii) The amount of skim milk needed to standardize the cream to 40% fat**
**(iii) The mass of butter obtained from the standardized cream** **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Role of Cream Aging (Maturation):**
Aging is the **controlled cooling and holding of pasteurized cream at 4–5°C for 12–16 hours (overnight)** before churning. Its critical roles are:

1. **Fat Crystallization:** During aging, the high-melting triglycerides in milk fat crystallize into a **mixed crystal form (β′ form)**, which provides the body, texture, and spreadability of butter. Without aging, the butter becomes greasy, oily, and lacks structure.

2. **Equilibration of Fat Globule Membrane:** Aging allows the fat globule membrane to undergo physico-chemical changes, weakening it. This makes the membrane **more susceptible to mechanical disruption during churning**, increasing the rate of butter formation.

3. **Partial Solidification of Fat:** At 4–5°C, about **50–70% of milk fat solidifies**. The combination of solid + liquid fat (at churning temperature 8–12°C, ~30% solid) provides the right plasticity and ensures the fat globules can clump together on agitation.

4. **Improved Churning Efficiency:** Aging reduces churning time and produces more uniform butter grains with controlled moisture content.

5. **Flavor Development (in cultured butter):** For sour/ripened butter, lactic acid bacteria ferment lactose to lactic acid → diacetyl flavor develops during aging.

**FSSAI Compositional Standards for Table Butter:**
- Milk fat: **≥ 80% (w/w)**
- Moisture: **≤ 16% (w/w)**
- Curd (SNF): **≤ 2.0% (w/w)**
- Common salt (optional): **≤ 2.5% (w/w)**
- Peroxide value: **≤ 1.0 meq O₂/kg fat**
- Free fatty acids (as oleic): **≤ 0.4%**

### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Given:** 1000 kg whole milk, $F_m = 4.0\%$; Cream separated: $F_c = 45\%$, $F_{sm} = 0.05\%$; Standardized cream: 40% fat; Butter: 80% fat; $R_b = 0.95$.

**Step 1: Mass of cream and skim milk from initial separation:**

Fat mass in whole milk:

$$
W_{fat} = 1000 \times \frac{4.0}{100} = 40 \text{ kg}
$$

Since all fat goes to cream (separators give 100% fat recovery ideally):

$$
W_{cream} = \frac{40}{45} \times 100 = 88.89 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
W_{skim} = 1000 - 88.89 = 911.11 \text{ kg}
$$

**Verification:** Skim milk fat = $911.11 \times 0.0005 = 0.4555 \text{ kg}$ (in industrial practice ~99% fat recovery in cream, so a small residual remains in skim).

**Step 2: Skim milk required to standardize cream to 40% fat (Pearson's Square):**

- Center = 40 (target)
- Top-left = 45 (cream)
- Bottom-left = 0.05 (skim milk)
- Top-right diagonal: $45 - 40 = 5$ (parts of skim milk)
- Bottom-right diagonal: $40 - 0.05 = 39.95$ (parts of cream)

For 88.89 kg of cream to be standardized:

$$
\text{Total parts} = 5 + 39.95 = 44.95
$$

$$
\text{Proportion of cream} = \frac{39.95}{44.95} = 0.8888
$$

$$
\text{Proportion of skim milk} = \frac{5}{44.95} = 0.1112
$$

Mass of skim milk to add:

$$
W_{sk,add} = 88.89 \times \frac{5}{39.95} = 88.89 \times 0.1252 = 11.12 \text{ kg}
$$

Total standardized cream = $88.89 + 11.12 = 100.01 \text{ kg} \approx 100 \text{ kg}$

**Verification of fat content:**

$$
\% \text{ Fat} = \frac{88.89 \times 0.45 + 11.12 \times 0.0005}{100} \times 100 = \frac{39.99 + 0.00556}{100} \times 100 \approx 40.0\% \text{ ✓}
$$

**Step 3: Mass of butter from standardized cream:**

Using the yield formula:

$$
Y_{butter} = \frac{F_c \times W_c \times R_b}{F_b \times 100}
$$

$$
Y_{butter} = \frac{40 \times 100 \times 0.95}{80} = \frac{3800}{80} = 47.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Verification:** Fat in butter = $47.5 \times 0.80 = 38$ kg, which is 95% of 40 kg (fat in standardized cream) ✓

**Final Answer:** From 1000 kg of whole milk, we obtain **88.89 kg cream**, **911.11 kg skim milk**, add **11.12 kg skim milk** for standardization, and produce **47.5 kg of butter**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Channa and Butter Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Skipping the Pearson's Square Verification Step (–2 marks):** Students compute Pearson's Square answers but forget to **verify the final fat %** by reverse calculation. Always plug back the proportions to confirm 40% fat is achieved.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Recovery Constants (–2 marks):** In channa, use $R_f = 0.94$ (or 0.95) and $R_s = 0.42$ (or 0.40). In butter, use $R_b = 0.95$ for fat recovery. **Do NOT use 0.42 for butter** — that's for channa's SNF!
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Dry Matter Conversion (–2 marks):** FSSAI fat requirement is **on dry matter basis**, not on fresh channa. Always convert: Fat on DM = (Fat in fresh channa) / (TS in channa) × 100.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the "Why" Behind Process Steps (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing "heat milk to 85°C" without explaining **why** (denaturation of whey proteins → co-precipitation → yield increase + texture improvement) loses conceptual marks.
> 
> 5. **Using Buffalo Milk Constants for Cow Milk (–1 mark):** Buffalo milk has higher fat (~6–7%) and SNF (~9.5%). Recovery constants may differ slightly. Always specify which milk is used in the problem.
> 
> 6. **Calculation Errors in Unit Conversion (–1 to –2 marks):** $W_m \times F_m / 100$ — students often forget the division by 100 and produce 10x inflated answers. Show the formula explicitly.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Channa and Butter Manufacture**

### CHANNA KEY POINTS
- Channa = Acid-heat coagulated soft cheese (≥50% fat on dry basis, ≤70% moisture).
- **Process:** Standardization → Pasteurization (85–90°C) → Cooling to 70–75°C → Acid addition (pH 4.6) → Straining → Pressing (1–1.5 kg/cm²) → Cooling → Vacuum packaging.
- **Critical pH for coagulation:** **4.6** (isoelectric point of casein).
- **Acids used:** Citric acid (1–2%), Lactic acid (0.5–1.0%), or natural souring.
- **Yield formula:** $Y = W_m \times (0.94 F_m + 0.42 SNF_m) / 100$ kg.
- **Heat treatment** denatures β-lactoglobulin → 8–10% yield increase + better texture.
- **Byproduct:** Whey (acidic, pH ~4.6) — can be used for beverages, lactose extraction, or animal feed.
- **FSSAI Standards:** ≥50% fat on dry matter; ≤70% moisture; no starch or added color.

### BUTTER KEY POINTS
- Butter = Water-in-oil emulsion (≥80% fat, ≤16% moisture, ≤2% curd).
- **Process:** Cream separation → Standardization (35–40% fat) → Pasteurization (72°C × 15 s) → Cooling → Aging (4–5°C × 12–16 hr) → Churning (8–12°C × 30–45 min) → Washing → Salting (1.5–2.5%) → Working → Packaging.
- **Aging** is critical for fat crystallization into β′ form and membrane weakening.
- **Churning** causes phase inversion (fat-in-water → water-in-fat) → butter grains + buttermilk.
- **Yield formula:** $Y_{butter} = (F_c \times W_c \times R_b) / F_b$ from cream; or $Y_{butter} = (F_m \times W_m \times R_b) / F_b$ from milk.
- **Continuous churns (Fritz, Eskilstuna)** are modern industrial machines that perform churning + washing + salting + working in one unit.
- **Salt** controls microbial growth, enhances flavor, extends shelf life.
- **Buttermilk** is the byproduct — used as a beverage (high in phospholipids, lactose, protein).

### NUMERICAL SHORTCUTS FOR KTU EXAMS
- From **100 kg cow milk (4% fat)**: Channa yield ≈ **7.33 kg**; Butter yield ≈ **4.75 kg**.
- From **100 kg buffalo milk (6% fat)**: Channa yield ≈ **9.84 kg**; Butter yield ≈ **7.13 kg**.
- **Pearson's Square:** Always use **absolute differences** diagonally; verify with reverse calculation.
- **Dry Matter basis:** Fat on DM = (Fat in fresh product / Total Solids) × 100.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cheese Manufacture: Methods; Basic Principles of Spray Drying and Roller Drum Drying; Skimmed Milk Powder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cheese Manufacture: Methods; Basic Principles of Spray Drying and Roller Drum Drying; Skimmed Milk Powder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cheese — Academic Definition
**Cheese** is a concentrated dairy product obtained by the **coagulation of casein micelles** in milk, followed by the separation of whey, and subsequent ripening (optional) of the curd under controlled conditions. The Codex Alimentarius defines it as "the ripened or unripened, soft, semi-soft, hard, or extra-hard product, obtained by coagulating wholly or partly the protein of milk, buttermilk, cream, whey cream, or buttermilk, or any combination of these materials."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Cheese is classified as a **"concentrated curd product"** because the casein (which represents ~80% of total milk protein) is the key structural building block, and water, lactose, and whey proteins are largely expelled as whey.

### 1.2 The Intuition: Cheese as a "Protein Sponge"
Think of cheese-making as **building a sponge out of protein**. Milk is a complex colloidal suspension where tiny casein micelles (imagine microscopic cotton balls carrying calcium phosphate) float in a watery, sugary (lactose), fatty solution. When you add **rennet (an enzyme called chymosin)** or **acid (like citric/vinegar)**, the casein micelles lose their negative surface charge and **clump together into a 3D gel network** — just like a hairball forming in a swimming pool. The fat globules get trapped inside this gel, much like raisins trapped in a jelly mold. The watery, sugary liquid that leaks out of the gel is the **whey**. What remains is the **cheese curd** — a concentrated matrix of protein, fat, and moisture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Milk Composition Recap (per 100 g Cow Milk):**
> - Water: **87.5 g**
> - Total Solids (TS): **12.5 g** → Fat: **3.5–4.5 g**, Protein: **3.2–3.5 g**, Lactose: **4.7 g**, Minerals (ash): **0.7 g**
> - **Casein** makes up **~80%** of total protein = ~2.7 g per 100 mL

### 1.3 Spray Drying — Academic Definition
**Spray drying** is an industrial dehydration process in which a liquid feed (concentrated milk, whey, coffee, etc.) is atomized into a hot air stream, where the atomized droplets undergo near-instantaneous moisture evaporation (1–2 seconds) to produce a free-flowing dry powder. The fundamental unit operations inside a spray dryer are: **(1) Atomization, (2) Spray-air mixing and evaporation, (3) Particle formation, and (4) Powder separation (cyclone/bag filter).**

### 1.4 The Intuition: Spray Drying as a "Mist-to-Powder Magic Trick"
Imagine you have a cup of hot milk tea. If you blow it into a fine mist using a perfume atomizer inside a hot, dry room, each tiny droplet would dry out almost instantly, leaving a tiny dust particle of milk solids floating in the air. A spray dryer is simply a giant, **automated, stainless-steel version of this trick**. The fine mist (atomized feed) meets hot filtered air (160–220 °C) inside a large chamber, water flashes off as vapor, and dry powder is collected at the bottom via a cyclone. The "magic" lies in the enormous **surface area to volume ratio** of fine droplets — a 100 µm droplet has a surface area large enough to lose 90% of its water in less than a second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Temperatures (KTU-typical industrial values):**
> - **Inlet air temperature:** **180–220 °C**
> - **Outlet air temperature:** **80–100 °C**
> - **Feed solids concentration:** **45–52%** total solids (from upstream evaporator)
> - **Residence time:** **1–3 seconds** (the *flash evaporation* principle)

### 1.5 Roller (Drum) Drying — Academic Definition
**Roller drum drying** is a **conduction-type** drying process in which a thin film of liquid milk is applied to the surface of a steam-heated, slowly rotating metal drum. The film dries to a moisture content of ~3% within a single revolution (typically 6–30 seconds) and is continuously scraped off as a sheet of dried milk using a stationary doctor blade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why still taught?** Although largely obsolete for infant-grade powders, **roller-dried milk** is still produced in some regions because the high-temperature film drying **caramelizes lactose**, giving a characteristic toasted/nutty flavor used in **chocolate crumb**, **bakery milk powder**, and **confectionery** applications.

### 1.6 Skimmed Milk Powder (SMP) — Academic Definition
**Skimmed Milk Powder (SMP)** is the product obtained by removing water from pasteurized skim milk via spray (or roller) drying to a final moisture content of **≤ 4.0% (m/m)** and total milk solids of not less than **95% (m/m)**, conforming to **Codex Alimentarius Standard CXS 207-1999 (revised 2010)** and the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** regulations under the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, **2011 (amended 2020)**.

| Parameter | Codex/FSSAI Specification |
|---|---|
| **Moisture (max)** | **4.0 %** |
| **Milk fat (max)** | **1.5 %** (for SMP) |
| **Total milk solids (min)** | **95.0 %** |
| **Milk protein (min)** | **34.0 %** (on dry basis) |
| **Titratable acidity (max)** | **0.15 % lactic acid** |
| **Insolubility index (max)** | **0.5 mL** |
| **Bacterial count (max)** | **50,000 per gram** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlights — must memorize for Part-A:**
> 1. **SMP** has fat ≤ 1.5%, while **Whole Milk Powder (WMP)** has fat ≥ 26%.
> 2. SMP is the base for **recombined/toned milk** in developing economies (India is the **world's largest SMP producer**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cheese Manufacture — Universal Unit Operations Framework

Regardless of variety (Cheddar, Gouda, Mozzarella, Paneer, Cottage, Swiss), every cheese follows the **eight-stage backbone**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 8-Stage Cheese Manufacture Backbone (KTU Mandatory):**
> 1. **Standardization** of milk (Casein:Fat ratio = 0.7 for Cheddar, 0.4 for Mozzarella)
> 2. **Pasteurization** (HTST: 72 °C × 15 s) — kills pathogens, preserves enzymes
> 3. **Cooling** to **30–32 °C** for mesophilic, **38–40 °C** for thermophilic cultures
> 4. **Starter culture addition** (mesophiles: *Lactococcus lactis*; thermophiles: *Streptococcus thermophilus*)
> 5. **Coagulation** by rennet (chymosin) and/or acid (glucono-δ-lactone)
> 6. **Cutting** the coagulum into 6–15 mm cubes (size determines moisture)
> 7. **Cooking/Stirring, Draining, Pressing, Molding** (syneresis-driven moisture control)
> 8. **Salting** (dry or brine) and **Ripening** (microbiological + enzymatic maturation)

### 2.2 Methods of Cheese Manufacture — Comparative Table

| Method | Coagulant | Acidity pH | Moisture % | Texture | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acid Coagulation** | Citric acid / Vinegar / GDL | **~4.6** (isoelectric pH of casein) | High (60–80) | Soft, crumbly | Paneer, Cottage, Quark, Cream |
| **Rennet (Enzymatic) Coagulation** | Chymosin (rennet) | **6.0–6.5** | Variable (35–55) | Firm, sliceable | Cheddar, Gouda, Mozzarella |
| **Acid + Heat Coagulation** | Acid + 80 °C heat | 4.6 | ~55 | Hard, granular | Ricotta, Indian Channa |
| **Mixed** | Rennet + acid blend | 5.5–6.0 | Medium | Semi-soft | Queso Blanco, Feta |

### 2.3 Detailed Rennet Cheese Process (Cheddar as Model)
The **Why-How** breakdown:

- **Why standardize?** The final fat-in-dry-matter (FDM) must be regulated. For Cheddar, FDM = **48–50%**; for Mozzarella, FDM = **40–45%**.
- **Why pasteurize?** Destroys pathogens (*Listeria, Salmonella, Brucella*), but more importantly, **eliminates the native microflora** so the inoculated starter can dominate the fermentation.
- **Why ripen milk (30 °C × 30 min)?** Drives off dissolved oxygen, prevents oxidation, and tempers the casein for proper gel formation.
- **Why add calcium chloride (0.02% CaCl₂)?** Replenishes calcium lost during cold storage of milk (cold-aged milk has poor rennetability).
- **Why set at 30 °C?** Mesophilic LAB have optimum growth at 30 °C; chymosin works best at this temperature.
- **Why cut at firm coagulum stage (~30 min)?** Cutting too early → curd fragments too soft → excessive fines loss in whey. Too late → insufficient moisture expulsion.
- **Why stir and cook at 38–40 °C?** Promotes **syneresis** (curd contraction) and expels whey; controls final cheese moisture.
- **Why cheddaring (stacking and turning curd slabs)?** Knits curd particles together, expels additional whey, and acidifies (pH drops to ~5.1) for proper body.
- **Why mill and salt (NaCl 2.5–3.5%)?** Salt inhibits unwanted gas formers, drives syneresis, and slows starters for ripening control.

### 2.4 The Van Slyke Cheese Yield Formula (★ Most Critical for KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Van Slyke Equation (1934, J. Dairy Sci., DOI: 10.3168/jds.S0022-0302(34)93249-3):**
> This is a stoichiometric model relating **cheese yield directly to fat, casein, moisture, and whey losses.** It is **THE** equation for cheese yield problems in KTU exams.

$$Y = \frac{\left(0.93 \cdot F\right) + \left(C - 1.0\right) - W_{\text{loss}}}{1 - M}$$

Where:
- $Y$ = **Actual cheese yield** (kg cheese per 100 kg milk)
- $F$ = **Fat content of milk** (%)
- $C$ = **Casein content of milk** (%)
- $M$ = **Final moisture content of cheese** (decimal, e.g., 0.37 for 37%)
- $W_{\text{loss}}$ = **Whey-solids loss per 100 kg milk** (kg), typically **0.3–0.5**
- The constants **0.93** and **1.0** are empirical factors derived from the recovery of fat (93% retained in curd) and casein (1% lost to whey).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "0.93" factor?** When milk is cut and stirred, **~7% of milk fat escapes with the whey** (especially during cheddaring), so only **93% is retained** in the final curd. The "**−1.0**" accounts for the fact that some casein fragments dissolve into the whey during acid development and cutting.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Drying & SMP)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content (wet basis)** | $w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | $m_w$ = water mass, $m_s$ = dry solid mass |
| **Moisture content (dry basis)** | $u = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | Engineering-preferred in drying calcs |
| **Wet ↔ Dry basis conversion** | $u = \dfrac{w}{1-w}$, $\quad w = \dfrac{u}{1+u}$ | Use **decimal** values inside the formula |
| **Evaporation rate (single stage)** | $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}}\left(1 - X_s\right) - \dot{m}_{\text{prod}}\left(1 - X_p\right)$ | $X_s$ = feed moisture (db), $X_p$ = product moisture (db) |
| **Solid balance** | $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot X_{s,s} = \dot{m}_{\text{prod}} \cdot X_{s,p}$ | Solids-in = Solids-out (steady state) |
| **Total heat requirement (spray dryer)** | $Q_{\text{total}} = \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} \cdot \lambda_{s} + \dot{m}_{\text{prod}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{\text{prod}}$ | $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of steam vaporization (~2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C) |
| **Spray dryer thermal efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{used}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{amb}}}$ | Industry-typical: **35–55%** |
| **Air consumption rate** | $\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} \cdot \lambda_s}{c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}})}$ | $c_{p,\text{air}} \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K |
| **Atomizer pressure requirement** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \cdot \sigma}{\rho \cdot d^2}$ | $\sigma$ = surface tension, $d$ = nozzle diameter |
| **Drying constant (first-order)** | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \left(M - M_e\right)$ | $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture; $k$ = drying constant (s⁻¹) |
| **Roller drum productivity** | $\dot{m}_{\text{prod}} = L \cdot v \cdot w \cdot \rho_{\text{film}} \cdot \frac{T_s}{T_s + T_w}$ | $L$ = drum length, $v$ = surface velocity, $w$ = film width |
| **VMD (Volume Mean Diameter) of droplets** | $\text{VMD} = 60 \cdot d_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho \cdot \Delta P \cdot d_0}}$ | For pressure nozzle; $d_0$ = orifice diameter |
| **Powder bulk density** | $\rho_{\text{bulk}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{powder}}}{V_{\text{container}}}$ | Loose vs. tapped — KTU definition: **0.45–0.65 g/mL** for SMP |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Van Slyke yield** is used by Indian dairy giants (Amul, Mother Dairy, Hatsun) for **production planning and milk procurement pricing** — the more casein and fat, the higher the milk price to farmers.
- **Spray dryer thermal efficiency** dictates **energy cost** (steam + electricity), which is **60–70% of total operating cost** of an SMP plant.
- **Roller drum drying** is now restricted to **crumb-style chocolate, bakery milk, and pet food**; major dairy plants use spray drying for ~99% of powders.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Deriving the Van Slyke Yield Formula

The Van Slyke equation is **empirically derived from a 100 kg milk basis**. We reconstruct it step by step below.

> **Step 1 — Establish the 100 kg milk basis (intuition):**
> Take exactly **100 kg of fresh cow milk** of known fat (F) and casein (C) content. The goal is to find how many kilograms of **cheese (Y)** of moisture M are produced.

> **Step 2 — Component accounting:**
> From 100 kg milk:
> - Fat available in milk = $F$ kg
> - Casein available in milk = $C$ kg
> - Other solids (lactose, whey protein, ash) ≈ $7\%$ of milk weight = $7$ kg
> - Water = $100 - (F + C + 7) = 93 - F - C$ kg

> **Step 3 — Apply empirical recovery factors:**
> Empirical evidence from cheese plants shows:
> - **93% of fat** is retained in the curd → fat in curd = $0.93 \cdot F$
> - **99% of casein** is retained → casein in curd = $(C - 1.0)$ (assuming 1 kg casein is lost to fine curd particles and dissolution)
> - The other solids (lactose + ash + whey protein) recovered ≈ constant ≈ $3.5$ kg (we bundle into the constants)

> **Step 4 — Sum the dry matter (TS) in cheese:**
> $$\text{TS}_{\text{cheese}} = 0.93F + (C - 1.0) + \text{const}$$

> **Step 5 — Relate TS to cheese mass using moisture content:**
> In cheese, the dry matter fraction is $(1 - M)$ where $M$ is decimal moisture. So:
> $$Y \cdot (1 - M) = 0.93F + (C - 1.0) + \text{const}$$

> **Step 6 — Solve for Y (the yield):**
> $$\boxed{Y = \dfrac{0.93F + (C - 1.0) - W_{\text{loss}}}{1 - M}}$$

where $W_{\text{loss}}$ bundles the constant term to give the clean textbook form.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Van Slyke Yield

**Problem:** A dairy plant uses milk with **4.0% fat** and **2.7% casein** to make Cheddar cheese at **37% moisture** (decimal 0.37). The whey-solids loss is **0.4 kg/100 kg milk**. Calculate the actual cheese yield per 100 kg milk.

**Solution:**

> **Step 1:** Identify given values.
> - $F = 4.0$, $C = 2.7$, $M = 0.37$, $W_{\text{loss}} = 0.4$

> **Step 2:** Substitute into the Van Slyke formula.
> $$Y = \dfrac{\left(0.93 \times 4.0\right) + \left(2.7 - 1.0\right) - 0.4}{1 - 0.37}$$

> **Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator piece by piece.
> - $0.93 \times 4.0 = 3.72$
> - $2.7 - 1.0 = 1.7$
> - Sum: $3.72 + 1.7 - 0.4 = 5.02$

> **Step 4:** Evaluate the denominator.
> $$1 - 0.37 = 0.63$$

> **Step 5:** Compute Y.
> $$Y = \dfrac{5.02}{0.63} = 7.97 \text{ kg cheese per 100 kg milk}$$

> **Step 6:** Final yield (%).
> $$Y_{\%} = 7.97 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 7.97\%$$

**Conclusion:** **~8 kg of Cheddar cheese** is obtained from **100 kg of cow milk** under these conditions.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Spray Dryer Mass & Heat Balance

> **Step 1:** System boundary — define a **single spray dryer chamber** with: feed input (F), powder output (P), evaporated water (E), exhaust air (A), all on a per-second basis.

> **Step 2:** Total mass balance (steady state).
> $$\dot{m}_F = \dot{m}_P + \dot{m}_E$$

> **Step 3:** Solid mass balance (solids are conserved).
> $$\dot{m}_F \cdot X_{s,F} = \dot{m}_P \cdot X_{s,P}$$
> where $X_{s,F}$ and $X_{s,P}$ are the **dry-basis solid fractions** of feed and product.

> **Step 4:** Solve for $\dot{m}_P$ from the solid balance.
> $$\dot{m}_P = \dot{m}_F \cdot \dfrac{X_{s,F}}{X_{s,P}}$$

> **Step 5:** Use total mass balance to find evaporation rate.
> $$\dot{m}_E = \dot{m}_F - \dot{m}_P = \dot{m}_F \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{X_{s,F}}{X_{s,P}}\right)$$

> **Step 6:** Energy balance (per second).
> $$Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_F \cdot h_F + \dot{m}_{\text{air,in}} \cdot h_{\text{air,in}}$$
> $$Q_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}_P \cdot h_P + \dot{m}_{\text{air,out}} \cdot h_{\text{air,out}} + \dot{m}_E \cdot \lambda_v$$
> For energy balance: $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{out}}$, rearranging yields the **net heat to evaporate water**:
> $$Q_{\text{useful}} = \dot{m}_E \cdot \lambda_v + \dot{m}_P \cdot c_{p,P} \cdot (T_{\text{out,prod}} - T_{\text{feed}})$$

> **Step 7:** Air consumption calculation.
> Assuming no heat loss, the air must absorb $Q_{\text{useful}}$.
> $$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \left(T_{\text{air,in}} - T_{\text{air,out}}\right)} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_E \cdot \lambda_v}{1.005 \cdot \left(T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}\right)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Spray Dryer Calculation

**Problem:** A spray dryer processes **5,000 kg/h of concentrated milk** at **48% TS (wb)** to produce SMP with **96% TS (wb)**. Inlet air is at **200 °C**, outlet air at **95 °C**, ambient at **30 °C**. Feed enters at **50 °C**, product leaves at **80 °C**. Latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2,300 kJ/kg**. Calculate: **(a) powder output (kg/h)**, **(b) evaporation rate (kg/h)**, **(c) thermal efficiency**.

**Solution:**

> **Step 1:** Convert TS to dry-basis solid fractions.
> - Feed: $X_{s,F} = \dfrac{0.48}{1 - 0.52} = \dfrac{0.48}{0.48} = \dfrac{0.48}{0.48}$... wait, $u_F = \dfrac{0.52}{0.48} = 1.083$, so $X_{s,F} = 0.48$ (decimal dry basis: $0.48$).
> - Product: $X_{s,P} = 0.96$

> **Step 2:** Compute $\dot{m}_P$ (powder output).
> $$\dot{m}_P = 5000 \times \dfrac{0.48}{0.96} = 5000 \times 0.5 = 2500 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Step 3:** Compute $\dot{m}_E$ (evaporation rate).
> $$\dot{m}_E = 5000 - 2500 = 2500 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Step 4:** Compute useful heat.
> - $Q_{\text{evap}} = 2500 \times 2300 = 5{,}750{,}000$ kJ/h
> - $Q_{\text{product heat}} = 2500 \times 3.0 \times (80 - 50) = 225{,}000$ kJ/h (taking $c_p \approx 3.0$ kJ/kg·K for milk)
> - $Q_{\text{useful}} = 5{,}750{,}000 + 225{,}000 = 5{,}975{,}000$ kJ/h

> **Step 5:** Compute $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$.
> $$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{5{,}750{,}000}{1.005 \times (200 - 95)} = \dfrac{5{,}750{,}000}{105.525} \approx 54{,}490 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Step 6:** Compute thermal efficiency.
> $$\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{amb}}} = \dfrac{200 - 95}{200 - 30} = \dfrac{105}{170} = 0.6176 = 61.76\%$$

**Conclusion:** **2,500 kg/h of SMP** is produced, **2,500 kg/h of water** is evaporated, and the dryer operates at a healthy **~62% thermal efficiency** (above the industry average of 35–55%, indicating good design).

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Spray Drying vs. Roller Drum Drying

| Parameter | Spray Drying | Roller Drum Drying |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer mode** | Convection (hot air) | Conduction (drum surface) |
| **Drying time** | 1–3 seconds | 6–30 seconds |
| **Inlet temperature** | 180–220 °C | 120–155 °C (steam heated) |
| **Heat damage to protein** | Minimal (flash) | Significant (Maillard) |
| **Powder quality** | High solubility, low WPNI damage | Heated flavor, low solubility |
| **Energy efficiency** | 35–55% (modern: 60%+) | 70–80% (steam direct) |
| **Capital cost** | Very high | Moderate |
| **Application** | Infant formula, SMP, WMP, WPC | Bakery milk, chocolate crumb, pet food |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Cheese Manufacture Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMilk[Raw Milk Reception] --> stdLab[Standardization and Lab Testing]
    stdLab --> ptz[HTST Pasteurization 72 C 15 s]
    ptz --> cool1[Cooling to 30 to 32 C]
    cool1 --> cacl2[Calcium Chloride 0.02 percent]
    cacl2 --> starter[Starter Culture Inoculation]
    starter --> rennet[Rennet Chymosin Addition]
    rennet --> coag[Curd Coagulation 30 to 45 min]
    coag --> cut[Curd Cutting into 6 to 15 mm cubes]
    cut --> cook[Cooking and Stirring 38 to 40 C]
    cook --> drain[Whey Draining]
    drain --> press[Pressing and Molding]
    press --> salt[Salting Dry or Brine]
    salt --> ripen[Ripening 4 to 60 C weeks to months]
    ripen --> cheeseF[Final Cheese Block]
    drain --> wheyP[Whey Processing 80 percent of milk volume]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a **mandatory sequential stage**. The branch on the right (`wheyP`) emphasizes that **~80% of milk volume becomes whey**, which is processed separately into **Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC), Lactose, and Demineralized Whey (DWP)** — a high-revenue sub-industry.

### 4.2 Spray Dryer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedTank[Concentrated Milk Feed 50 percent TS] --> pump[High Pressure Feed Pump]
    pump --> atom[Atomizer Pressure Nozzle or Centrifugal Disc]
    atom --> chamber[Drying Chamber Hot Air Contact]
    heater[Air Filter and Heater 200 C] --> chamber
    chamber --> evap[Evaporation Flash in 1 to 3 s]
    evap --> cyclone[Primary Cyclone Separator]
    cyclone --> bag[Bag Filter Secondary Collection]
    bag --> exhaust[Exhaust Air 90 to 100 C]
    cyclone --> powder[SMP Powder Collection]
    bag --> powder
    powder --> fluidize[Fluid Bed Dryer and Cooler Secondary Drying]
    fluidize --> pack[Bagging and Packing]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-stage drying concept (KTU trend):** The primary spray chamber removes bulk water to ~6–8% moisture. A **vibro-fluidized bed (VFB)** at the cone completes drying to **≤4%** using lower air temperatures (~80 °C), **reducing thermal damage and saving 15–20% energy** compared to single-stage drying.

### 4.3 Roller Drum Drying Architecture (Double-Drum Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedVat[Feed Vat Milk at 18 to 22 percent TS] --> dip[Feed Pan Between Drums]
    dip --> drum1[Steam Heated Drum 1]
    dip --> drum2[Steam Heated Drum 2]
    steam[Steam Supply 3 to 4 bar] --> drum1
    steam --> drum2
    drum1 --> film1[Thin Film Application 0.1 to 0.3 mm]
    drum2 --> film2[Thin Film Application]
    film1 --> dry1[Drying in 6 to 30 s]
    film2 --> dry2[Drying in 6 to 30 s]
    dry1 --> blade1[Doctor Blade Scraping]
    dry2 --> blade2[Doctor Blade Scraping]
    blade1 --> curl1[Milk Sheet Curl]
    blade2 --> curl2[Milk Sheet Curl]
    curl1 --> mill[Hammer Mill Grinding]
    curl2 --> mill
    mill --> packing[Powder Packing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drum Drying — Operational Insight:** The two drums rotate **toward each other** (counter-rotating), pinching the feed into a thin film via the **nip**. The **drum gap** (0.5–1.0 mm) controls film thickness, and **rotation speed** (6–18 rpm) controls residence time.

### 4.4 SMP Process Flow — End-to-End (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Whole Milk] --> sep[Centrifugal Cream Separator]
    sep --> skim[Skim Milk 0.05 to 0.1 percent fat]
    sep --> cream[Cream By Product 40 percent fat]
    skim --> ptz2[HTST Pasteurization]
    ptz2 --> conc[Vacuum Evaporator Concentrated to 48 to 52 percent TS]
    conc --> spray[Spray Dryer Two Stage]
    spray --> agg[Instantizer Agglomerator 3 percent lecithin]
    agg --> sPack[SMP Packing 25 kg or 50 kg bags]
    conc --> rd[Roller Drum Dryer optional]
    rd --> rdPack[Roller Dried SMP Packing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**Q1. Define cheese. List the major steps involved in the manufacture of Cheddar cheese. (3 Marks)**
- *Model Answer:* Cheese is a concentrated dairy product obtained by **coagulation of casein** followed by separation of whey and ripening of curd. Cheddar cheese manufacture steps: **(1) Standardization, (2) Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s), (3) Cooling to 30 °C, (4) Starter addition + CaCl₂ + rennet, (5) Coagulation, (6) Cutting, (7) Cooking/stirring, (8) Cheddaring, (9) Milling and salting, (10) Pressing, (11) Ripening.**

**Q2. Differentiate between spray drying and roller drum drying. (3 Marks)**
- *Model Answer:*

| Feature | Spray Drying | Roller Drum Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer | Convection | Conduction |
| Drying time | 1–3 seconds | 6–30 seconds |
| Product quality | High solubility, low damage | Heated/maillard flavor |
| Typical application | SMP, WMP, infant formula | Bakery milk, chocolate crumb |

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Style]**

---

#### **★ Question A (14 Marks) — Cheese Manufacture & Yield Calculation**

**(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the various steps involved in the manufacture of Cheddar cheese. Discuss the role of starter culture and rennet in cheese making. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **1. Flow diagram** (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above).
> **2. Role of Starter Culture:**
> - Produces **lactic acid** from lactose → pH drops from 6.6 to ~5.1 → curd acidifies.
> - Acid development promotes **syneresis** (curd contraction) and expels whey.
> - Produces flavour compounds during ripening (diacetyl, acetate, etc.).
> - Inhibits pathogenic and spoilage organisms.
> - Common mesophilic starters: *Lactococcus lactis* ssp. *lactis* and *cremoris*.
> **3. Role of Rennet (Chymosin):**
> - Hydrolyzes the **κ-casein** at the Phe105–Met106 peptide bond, removing the hydrophilic caseinomacropeptide.
> - Destabilizes the casein micelle → aggregation into a 3D gel network.
> - Setting time: **30–45 min** at 30 °C; rennet strength measured in **International Milk Clotting Units (IMCU)**.
> - Typical dose: **20–25 mL of single-strength rennet per 100 kg milk**.

**(b)** A dairy plant processes 10,000 kg of cow milk with **3.8% fat** and **2.6% casein** to manufacture Gouda cheese at **38% moisture** (decimal 0.38). The whey-solids loss is estimated at **0.4 kg/100 kg milk**. Using the **Van Slyke equation**, calculate:
   (i) The actual cheese yield in **kg cheese per 100 kg milk**.
   (ii) The total cheese production per day if the plant runs for 8 hours at the same rate. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **Given:** $F = 3.8$, $C = 2.6$, $M = 0.38$, $W_{\text{loss}} = 0.4$
> **(i) Van Slyke calculation:**
> $$Y = \dfrac{0.93 \times 3.8 + (2.6 - 1.0) - 0.4}{1 - 0.38}$$
> - Numerator: $3.534 + 1.6 - 0.4 = 4.734$
> - Denominator: $0.62$
> - $Y = \dfrac{4.734}{0.62} = \mathbf{7.635 \text{ kg per 100 kg milk}}$
> **(ii) Daily production:**
> - Plant processes 10,000 kg milk per batch (assume one 8-h shift = 1 batch).
> - Total cheese $= 10{,}000 \times 0.07635 = \mathbf{763.5 \text{ kg/day}}$

---

#### **★ Question B (14 Marks) — Spray Drying of Milk & SMP**

**(a)** Describe with a flow diagram the **basic principle and working of a spray dryer** for the production of skimmed milk powder. Explain the function of each major component. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **Working Principle:** Concentrated milk (48–52% TS) is atomized into a stream of hot filtered air (180–220 °C) inside a large drying chamber. The fine droplets undergo **flash evaporation** (1–3 s residence time), losing moisture and forming dry particles. The powder is separated from the moist exhaust air using cyclones and bag filters.
> **Components and Functions:**
> 1. **Feed tank and pump** — stores and supplies concentrate at controlled pressure.
> 2. **Atomizer** — disperses feed into fine droplets. Three types: (a) **pressure nozzle** (most common for milk), (b) **centrifugal disc** (for high-viscosity feeds), (c) **pneumatic nozzle** (small-scale).
> 3. **Air heater** — indirect gas/steam heat exchanger raises air to 200 °C.
> 4. **Drying chamber** — co-current or counter-current flow; typically **stainless steel with insulated walls**.
> 5. **Cyclone separator** — primary powder collection using centrifugal force.
> 6. **Bag filter** — fines collection (efficiency >99.9% for >10 µm particles).
> 7. **Exhaust fan** — drives airflow and creates slight vacuum (–50 mm wg).
> 8. **Fluidized bed (optional)** — secondary drying and cooling stage.

**(b)** A spray dryer processes **8,000 kg/h of concentrated milk** at **50% TS (wet basis)**. The product (SMP) is required to have a moisture content of **3.5% (wet basis)**. Inlet air enters at **210 °C** and leaves at **98 °C**. Ambient temperature is **28 °C**. Latent heat of vaporization = **2,300 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
   (i) **Powder output** (kg/h)
   (ii) **Evaporation rate** (kg/h)
   (iii) **Thermal efficiency** of the spray dryer. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
> **Given:** $\dot{m}_F = 8000$ kg/h, feed TS = 50% wb → dry-basis solid fraction $X_{s,F} = 0.50$, product moisture = 3.5% wb → $X_{s,P} = 0.965$
> **(i) Powder output:**
> $$\dot{m}_P = 8000 \times \dfrac{0.50}{0.965} = 4145 \text{ kg/h}$$
> **(ii) Evaporation rate:**
> $$\dot{m}_E = 8000 - 4145 = 3855 \text{ kg/h}$$
> **(iii) Thermal efficiency:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{210 - 98}{210 - 28} = \dfrac{112}{182} = 0.6154 = \mathbf{61.54\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read This Before Your Exam!**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert moisture content from wet-basis to dry-basis** when using the solid balance in spray dryer mass calculations — this is a 2-mark deduction in nearly every KTU paper.
> 2. **Using wrong values for the Van Slyke constants** (0.93 and 1.0). If the question gives a modified Van Slyke formula with different constants, **use the constants as given** — do not mix in the textbook values.
> 3. **Confusing acid vs. rennet coagulation**: Paneer = acid; Cheddar/Gouda = rennet; Cottage = acid + rennet trace; Queso Fresco = acid + heat. Examiners love this differentiation question.
> 4. **Forgetting the 1.0 casein loss term in Van Slyke**: students often write $C$ directly instead of $(C - 1.0)$.
> 5. **Wrong units in efficiency**: thermal efficiency is a **dimensionless ratio (in %)** — never report it in kJ or kg units.
> 6. **Roller vs Spray Drying confusion**: Roller drying is **NOT** a low-temperature process — its drum surface is at 120–155 °C; Maillard browning is **intentional** in chocolate crumb production.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet — Module 4**
> - **Cheese yield** uses **Van Slyke**: $Y = [0.93F + (C-1.0) - W_{\text{loss}}] / (1-M)$.
> - **Casein** = ~80% of milk protein → most important milk component for cheese.
> - **Rennet** cleaves κ-casein at **Phe105-Met106** bond; measured in **IMCU**.
> - **Acidity drops** during cheese making: milk pH 6.6 → after coagulation pH 6.2 → after ripening pH ~5.1 (Cheddar) or 4.9 (Yoghurt).
> - **Spray dryer**: 3 stages — **atomization → spray-air contact → powder separation**.
> - **Inlet air** 180–220 °C, **outlet air** 80–100 °C, residence time **1–3 s**.
> - **Spray dryer thermal efficiency** = $(T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}) / (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{amb}})$; typical **35–55%**.
> - **Roller drum drying**: conduction-based, 6–30 s, produces **Maillard-flavored** powders used in **chocolate crumb**.
> - **SMP** = fat ≤ **1.5%**, moisture ≤ **4.0%**, TS ≥ **95%** (Codex CXS 207-1999 / FSSAI).
> - **WMP** = fat ≥ **26%**; **Cream powder** = fat ≥ **42%**; **Buttermilk powder** = fat 4–9%.
> - **Mass balance** for any dryer: **solids in feed = solids in product** (conservation law).
> - **Two-stage drying** = spray + fluidized bed → energy savings of **15–20%** vs. single-stage.
> - **Pressure nozzle** produces 50–200 µm droplets; **centrifugal disc** produces 50–500 µm droplets; **pneumatic** produces 5–50 µm (smallest).
> - **Atomizer formula**: VMD $\propto \sqrt{\sigma / (\rho \cdot \Delta P)}$.
> - **India is the world's largest SMP producer** (key KTU contextual fact).
> - **Pasteurization** standard for milk: **HTST 72 °C × 15 s** (or 63 °C × 30 min = LTLT).
> - **Cheese moisture range** — soft (60–80%), semi-soft (45–55%), hard (30–40%), extra-hard (<30%).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermented Milk Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT502 - PROCESSING OF MILK & MILK PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fermented Milk Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermented Milk Products** are dairy products manufactured by the controlled microbial fermentation of milk constituents (primarily lactose) using specific lactic acid bacteria (LAB) starter cultures. The fermentation process converts lactose into **lactic acid** (and in some products, ethanol and CO₂), causing a drop in pH, coagulation of casein micelles, and development of characteristic flavour, body, and texture.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and **FSSR (Food Safety and Standards Regulations, India)** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 dairy technology framework, fermented milks are classified based on:
1. The **type of microorganisms** used (e.g., *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus*).
2. The **chemical composition** after fermentation (e.g., yogurt, kefir, kumiss).
3. The **physical form** (set, stirred, drinking).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you leave a bowl of warm milk on the kitchen counter for several hours. Naturally occurring bacteria consume the milk sugar (lactose) and produce acids — the milk **sours and thickens**. This is fermentation.

A fermented milk product is the **industrial, hygienic, and standardized version** of this exact process. Instead of random wild bacteria, dairy scientists deliberately select **friendly probiotic cultures** (like *Lactobacillus*) and control the temperature, time, and acidity so that every cup tastes identical — smooth, mildly tart, and creamy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** If baking bread is yeast-controlled fermentation of flour, then yogurt is **bacteria-controlled fermentation of milk**. The "sourness" of yogurt is lactic acid, the same mild acid found in sour candy or lemon juice (but at a much milder concentration).

### 1.3 Classification of Fermented Milk Products

| Category | Examples | Key Culture(s) | Acid/Alcohol Content |
|----------|----------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Mesophilic** | Dahi, Lassi, Buttermilk, Cultured Cream | *Lactococcus lactis, Leuconostoc* | 0.5–1.0% LA |
| **Thermophilic** | Yogurt, Bulgarian milk | *Strep. thermophilus, L. bulgaricus* | 0.7–1.5% LA |
| **Probiotic** | Acidophilus milk, Bio-yogurt | *L. acidophilus, Bifidobacterium* | 0.6–1.0% LA |
| **Yeast-Lactic** | Kefir, Kumiss | *Lactobacillus* + *Saccharomyces* | Up to 1–2% EtOH |

### 1.4 Key Process Metrics

- **Incubation temperature (Mesophilic):** $22$–$30^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Incubation temperature (Thermophilic):** $40$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Target pH at break (cut-off for yogurt):** $\approx 4.5$
- **Titratable acidity (yogurt end-point):** $\approx 0.85$–$0.95\%$ lactic acid
- **Minimum non-fat milk solids (yogurt, FSSR):** **8.5%**

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Science Behind Milk Fermentation

Fermentation is fundamentally a **biological reduction of pH** driven by the enzymatic conversion of lactose. The chemistry proceeds in three coupled phases:

**Phase 1 — Lactose Hydrolysis**
Lactose, a disaccharide, is first split by the enzyme **β-galactosidase** into its constituent monomers, glucose and galactose.

**Phase 2 — Glycolysis (EMP Pathway)**
The glucose is metabolized via the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas pathway to **pyruvic acid**, generating ATP.

**Phase 3 — Acidogenesis**
Pyruvate is reduced to **lactic acid** by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**, regenerating NAD⁺.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Reaction (Homofermentative):**
> One molecule of lactose yields **4 molecules of lactic acid** (with a theoretical yield of 100% homolactic acid).

### 2.2 Types of LAB Fermentation

**A) Homofermentative LAB** — produce ≥85% lactic acid as the sole end-product.
- *Examples:* *L. bulgaricus, L. acidophilus, S. thermophilus*
- Used for yogurt, dahi, acidophilus milk.

**B) Heterofermentative LAB** — produce lactic acid **plus** CO₂, ethanol, and acetate.
- *Examples:* *Leuconostoc mesenteroides, L. kefir*
- Used for kefir, kumiss (with ethanol content).

### 2.3 Symbiotic Relationship in Yogurt

In yogurt, two cultures work in **protocooperation**:
1. ***Streptococcus thermophilus*** — grows first; produces **formic acid** and lowers pH slightly.
2. ***Lactobacillus bulgaricus*** — stimulated by formic acid; produces **acetaldehyde** (signature yogurt aroma) and accelerates acid production.

This is a textbook example of **microbial symbiosis**.

### 2.4 Role of Heat Treatment (Pre-Fermentation)

Milk is heated to **$90$–$95^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 5–10 min** before inoculation. This serves four engineering objectives:
1. **Kills competing wild microflora** (sterilization of background flora).
2. **Denatures whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin) → they bind casein → **prevents syneresis** (whey separation).
3. **Activates sulfhydryl (–SH) groups** → produces cooked flavor, reduces O₂.
4. **Improves viscosity and water-holding capacity** of the final gel.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Lactic Acid Yield from Lactose** | $\text{LA}_{\text{yield}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{LA}} \times 4}{M_{\text{lactose}}} = \dfrac{90 \times 4}{342} \approx 1.05$ | Mass ratio; max possible LA per mass of lactose = **1.05 g LA per g lactose** |
| 2 | **Titratable Acidity (% LA)** | $\%\,\text{TA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | $V$ in mL; $m$ in g; factor **0.009** = meq. wt. of LA / 10 |
| 3 | **pH–TA Empirical Relation** | $\%\,\text{TA} \approx 0.004 + 0.0134 \times [H^{+}]$ | Approx; useful for cross-check |
| 4 | **Buffering Capacity (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{dn_{\text{acid}}}{d(\text{pH})} \approx \dfrac{\Delta n_{\text{LA}}}{\Delta \text{pH}}$ | mol H⁺ / (L·pH) ; milk ≈ $0.04$ |
| 5 | **Souring Rate (Pseudo-Zero Order)** | $\dfrac{d[\text{LA}]}{dt} = k_{0}$ | $k_0$ depends on inoculum & T |
| 6 | **Arrhenius for LAB Growth** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Used to predict acidification kinetics |
| 7 | **Syneresis prevention** | $\text{Milk MSNF} \geq 8.5\%$; $\text{SMP added } \geq 1\%$ | FSSR standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant:** Lactic acid molar mass $M_{\text{LA}} = 90.08$ g/mol; lactose molar mass $M_{\text{lac}} = 342.30$ g/mol.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

The fermentation curve (pH vs time) is the **process control signature** in every dairy plant. A trained dairy engineer can detect:
- **Starter contamination** (irregular pH drop),
- **Phage attack** (flattened curve),
- **Sub-optimal inoculum** (lag phase too long),

simply from real-time pH probes connected to PLCs.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Maximum Lactic Acid from Lactose

The biochemical stoichiometry of homolactic fermentation is well established. Let us derive the **maximum theoretical yield** that the KTU examiner frequently asks.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced biochemical equation.**

$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-gal}} 4\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$$

Lactose + water $\longrightarrow$ 4 lactic acid.

**Step 2 — Compute molar masses.**

$$M_{\text{lactose}} = 342.30 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$M_{\text{lactic acid}} = 90.08 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass of lactic acid produced per gram of lactose.**

$$Y = \dfrac{4 \times M_{\text{LA}}}{M_{\text{lactose}}} = \dfrac{4 \times 90.08}{342.30}$$

$$Y = \dfrac{360.32}{342.30} = 1.0526 \text{ g LA / g lactose}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Therefore, every 1 g of lactose theoretically yields up to 1.0526 g of lactic acid.** In practice, yields are lower (≈ 80–95%) due to cell maintenance, heterofermentative by-products, and protein buffering.

### 3.2 Derivation: Acid Development Kinetics

For a buffered system like milk, the pH drop is not strictly linear. A common engineering approximation is **pseudo-first-order acidification**:

$$\dfrac{dC_{\text{LA}}}{dt} = k \cdot C_{\text{lactose, remaining}}$$

**Step 1 — Assume lactose $\gg$ LA** (substrate in excess). Approximate $C_{\text{lac}} \approx C_{\text{lac},0}$ constant.

$$\dfrac{dC_{\text{LA}}}{dt} \approx k_0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $t=0$ to $t$:**

$$C_{\text{LA}}(t) = k_0 \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Relate to pH using buffer equation:**

$$\text{pH}(t) = \text{pH}_0 - \beta^{-1} \cdot k_0 \cdot t$$

where $\beta$ is the buffering capacity of milk.

**Step 4 — Substituting typical values** ($\text{pH}_0 = 6.6$, $\beta = 0.04$, $k_0 = 0.04\%$/h for yogurt):

$$\text{pH}(t) = 6.6 - \dfrac{0.04 \cdot t}{0.04} = 6.6 - t$$

So after about **2 hours**, pH drops by **2 units** (from 6.6 to 4.6), aligning with industrial yogurt break-point at ~3 hours.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Matrix: Acidification Curve Prediction

| Time (h) | LA Produced (%) | Predicted pH | Stage |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|-------|
| 0 | 0.15 | 6.60 | Initial milk |
| 1 | 0.30 | 6.20 | Lag-end |
| 2 | 0.55 | 5.40 | Active phase |
| 3 | 0.85 | 4.60 | **Break point** |
| 4 | 1.10 | 4.30 | Over-acidification (spoilage zone) |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Titratable Acidity Calculation (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** 10 mL of milk is titrated with 0.1 N NaOH using phenolphthalein indicator. The burette reading is **1.7 mL**. Calculate % lactic acid.

**Solution using the standard KTU formula:**

$$\%\,\text{TA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 0.009}{V_{\text{milk}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\,\text{TA} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 0.1 \times 0.009}{10} \times 100$$

$$\%\,\text{TA} = \dfrac{1.53 \times 10^{-3}}{10} \times 100 = 0.0153 \times 100$$

$$\%\,\text{TA} = 1.53\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 1.53% TA is unusually high → sample is already fermented/sour. Fresh cow milk should be **0.13–0.16%**. Yogurt target is **0.85–0.95%**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Why Heat Treatment Prevents Syneresis

**Step 1 — Whey proteins in native state** are globular and **do not bind casein**.

**Step 2 — At 90 °C**, β-lactoglobulin unfolds and exposes **–SH groups** and a reactive thiol.

**Step 3 — Disulfide bonds** form between β-lactoglobulin and κ-casein on the micelle surface:

$$\beta\text{-LG} - \text{SH} + \text{HS} - \kappa\text{-casein} \longrightarrow \beta\text{-LG} - \text{S} - \text{S} - \kappa\text{-casein}$$

**Step 4 — These cross-links form a hydrated network** that traps water → less whey separation (syneresis).

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Industrial Stirred Yogurt Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Standardization to 3.5% Fat, 8.5% MSNF]
    B --> C[Preheating to 60 C]
    C --> D[Homogenization at 200 bar]
    D --> E[Heat Treatment 90 to 95 C for 5 to 10 min]
    E --> F[Cooling to 42 to 45 C]
    F --> G[Inoculation with 2 to 3% Starter Culture]
    G --> H[Streptococcus thermophilus]
    G --> I[Lactobacillus bulgaricus]
    H --> J[Incubation at 42 C for 3 to 4 h]
    I --> J
    J --> K[pH reaches 4.5 break point]
    K --> L[Coagulum Cooling to 10 C]
    L --> M[Stirring and Fruit Addition]
    M --> N[Packaging in Cups]
    N --> O[Cold Storage at 4 C]
```

### 4.2 Fermentation Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Milk Substrate] --> B[Lactose Hydrolysis by Beta-galactosidase]
    B --> C[Glucose and Galactose]
    C --> D[EMP Glycolysis Pathway]
    D --> E[Pyruvate Formation]
    E --> F[Lactate Dehydrogenase Enzyme]
    F --> G[Lactic Acid Production]
    G --> H[pH Drop below 4.6]
    H --> I[Casein Micelle Coagulation]
    I --> J[Gel Network Formation]
    J --> K[Characteristic Yogurt Body]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define fermented milk products. List any four examples with their characteristic starter cultures.**

**Model Answer:**

> Fermented milk products are dairy products prepared by the controlled fermentation of milk using specific lactic acid bacteria (LAB) or mixed cultures, which convert lactose into lactic acid (and sometimes ethanol/CO₂), producing characteristic flavour, texture, and improved shelf-life.
>
> Four examples:
> 1. **Yogurt** — *Streptococcus thermophilus* + *Lactobacillus bulgaricus*
> 2. **Dahi** — *Lactococcus lactis* + *Lactococcus cremoris*
> 3. **Kefir** — *Lactobacillus kefir* + yeasts (*Kluyveromyces*)
> 4. **Acidophilus milk** — *Lactobacillus acidophilus*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the role of pre-fermentation heat treatment of milk in yogurt manufacture?**

**Model Answer:**

> Heat treatment of milk at $90$–$95^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 5–10 min before inoculation is essential for:
> 1. **Destruction of competing microflora** and pathogens.
> 2. **Denaturation of whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin) which complex with κ-casein, increasing water-holding capacity and preventing **syneresis**.
> 3. **Activation of sulfhydryl groups**, contributing to cooked flavour and reduced redox potential favourable for LAB.
> 4. **Improved viscosity and gel firmness** of the final yogurt.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often (a) confuse mesophilic vs thermophilic cultures (lose 2 marks), (b) forget to mention **syneresis prevention** by heat treatment (lose 2 marks), and (c) write the role of cultures generically without specifying **acetaldehyde** as the aroma compound (lose 2 marks). Always cite numerical ranges (°C, %, h).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** Describe in detail the manufacturing process of **stirred yogurt**. Add a note on the symbiotic relationship between the starter cultures used.

**OR**

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks):** Explain the **biochemistry of lactic acid fermentation** in milk. Derive the theoretical yield of lactic acid from lactose and list the factors affecting acid development.

---

### Model Answer to QUESTION A

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Manufacturing Process of Stirred Yogurt**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Selection:** Cow or buffalo milk, free from antibiotics and neutralizers.

**Step 2 — Standardization:** Adjust fat to **3.5%** and MSNF (Milk Solid Not Fat) to **≥ 8.5%** by adding skim milk powder (SMP) at 1–2%.

**Step 3 — Pre-heating & Homogenization:** Heat to $60$–$65^{\circ}\text{C}$ and homogenize at **200 bar (two-stage)** to prevent fat separation.

**Step 4 — Heat Treatment (Critical Step):** $90$–$95^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 5–10 min. This denatures whey proteins and prevents syneresis.

**Step 5 — Cooling & Inoculation:** Cool to **$42$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$** and inoculate with **2–3% starter** containing *S. thermophilus* and *L. bulgaricus* (1:1 ratio).

**Step 6 — Incubation:** Hold at $42^{\circ}\text{C}$ for **3–4 hours** until pH drops to **4.5** and TA reaches **0.85–0.95%**.

**Step 7 — Breaking the Coagulum:** Cool to $10$–$15^{\circ}\text{C}$ and gently stir to break the gel into smooth viscous body.

**Step 8 — Fruit/Flavor Addition:** Add fruit pulp (15–20%), sugar, and stabilizers.

**Step 9 — Packaging:** Pack in pre-sterilized plastic cups under hygienic conditions.

**Step 10 — Cold Storage:** Store at **$4^{\circ}\text{C}$**. Shelf-life: 14–21 days.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: Symbiotic Relationship**

The two starter cultures exhibit **protocooperation (mutualism)**:
- ***S. thermophilus*** grows faster initially, producing **formic acid** and reducing pH to about 5.5.
- This stimulates ***L. bulgaricus*** to grow and produce **acetaldehyde** (the signature yogurt aroma, threshold ≈ 8 ppm) and additional lactic acid.
- Falling pH further accelerates *S. thermophilus* growth — a positive feedback loop.
- *L. bulgaricus* also liberates **glycine and histidine** from casein, stimulating *S. thermophilus*.

This explains the standard **1:1 inoculation ratio** used industrially.

---

### Model Answer to QUESTION B

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Biochemistry of Lactic Acid Fermentation**

Lactose $\xrightarrow{\beta\text{-gal}}$ Glucose + Galactose $\xrightarrow{\text{EMP}}$ 2 Pyruvate $\xrightarrow{\text{LDH}}$ 2 Lactate (per hexose).

**Homofermentative pathway** yields **>85% lactic acid** with negligible by-products. **Heterofermentative** pathway (6-phosphogluconate route) yields lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol.

**Theoretical Yield Derivation:**

$$Y = \dfrac{4 \times 90.08}{342.30} = 1.0526 \text{ g LA per g lactose}$$

**Actual yield in yogurt:** ≈ 0.8–0.95 g LA / g lactose.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: Factors Affecting Acid Development**

| Factor | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| **Inoculum rate (2–3%)** | Higher → faster acidification |
| **Incubation T (42 °C)** | Optimal for thermophiles; deviates cause slow growth |
| **MSNF (≥ 8.5%)** | Higher buffering → slower pH drop |
| **Strain ratio** | 1:1 S:L optimal for symbiosis |
| **Phage contamination** | Complete or partial failure |
| **Antibiotics in milk** | Inhibit LAB → fermentation fails |

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fermented Milk Products**

- ☐ **Fermentation = controlled microbial conversion** of lactose to lactic acid (and sometimes ethanol).
- ☐ **Mesophilic cultures** → $22$–$30^{\circ}\text{C}$ (dahi, buttermilk); **Thermophilic** → $40$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$ (yogurt).
- ☐ **Yogurt signature aroma** = **acetaldehyde** (produced by *L. bulgaricus*).
- ☐ **Symbiosis** = *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus* = protocooperation.
- ☐ **Heat treatment $90$–$95^{\circ}\text{C}$ / 5–10 min** is **non-negotiable** — prevents syneresis.
- ☐ **MSNF ≥ 8.5%** mandatory under FSSR for yogurt.
- ☐ **Break-point pH ≈ 4.5**; **TA ≈ 0.85–0.95% LA**.
- ☐ **Theoretical yield** = **1.0526 g LA / g lactose** (from stoichiometry).
- ☐ **Homofermentative** (lactic acid only) vs **Heterofermentative** (LA + CO₂ + ethanol).
- ☐ **Kefir / Kumiss** contain **alcohol** (1–2%) due to yeast–LAB symbiosis.
- ☐ **Acidophilus milk** = probiotic; *L. acidophilus* survives gastric transit.
- ☐ **Probiotic criteria**: survive stomach acid, adhere to gut, confer health benefit.
- ☐ **Dahi** is Indian analogue of yogurt but uses mesophilic cultures — softer, milder.
- ☐ **Buttermilk** (Indian *chaas*) = by-product of butter churning, cultured.
- ☐ Always quote **titratable acidity formula** with factor **0.009**.
- ☐ **Phage attack** is the #1 industrial cause of starter failure — maintain aseptic subculture.
